title: Dynamic-TD3: A Novel Algorithm for UAV Path Planning with Dynamic Obstacle Trajectory Prediction
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) finds extensive application in autonomous drone navigation within complex, high-risk environments. However, its practical deployment faces a safety-exploration dilemma: soft penalty mechanisms encourage risky trial-and-error, while most constraint-based methods suffer degraded performance under sensor noise and intent uncertainty. We propose Dynamic-TD3, a physically enhanced framework that enforces strict safety constraints while maintaining maneuverability by modeling navigation as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP). This framework integrates an Adaptive Trajectory Relational Evolution Mechanism (ATREM) to capture long-range intentions and employs a Physically Aware Gated Kalman Filter (PAG-KF) to mitigate non-stationary observation noise. The resulting state representation drives a dual-criterion policy that balances mission efficiency against hard safety constraints via Lagrangian relaxation. In experiments with aggressive dynamic threats, this approach demonstrates superior collision avoidance performance, reduced energy consumption, and smoother flight trajectories.

AI.tldr: Dynamic-TD3 is a safety-constrained DRL framework for autonomous drone navigation that uses CMDP, an Adaptive Trajectory Relational Evolution Mechanism (ATREM), a Physically Aware Gated Kalman Filter (PAG-KF), and Lagrangian relaxation to balance safety and efficiency, showing improved collision avoidance, energy efficiency, and smoother trajectories under dynamic threats.
AI.motivation: Address the safety-exploration trade-off in deep reinforcement learning for autonomous drone navigation, where soft penalties lead to unsafe trials and constraint-based methods falter under sensor noise and intent uncertainty.
AI.method: Model navigation as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP). Introduce Adaptive Trajectory Relational Evolution Mechanism (ATREM) to capture long-range intentions. Employ a Physically Aware Gated Kalman Filter (PAG-KF) to mitigate non-stationary observation noise. Use a dual-criterion policy optimized via Lagrangian relaxation to balance mission efficiency with hard safety constraints.
AI.result: Under aggressive dynamic threats, the approach achieves superior collision avoidance, reduces energy consumption, and produces smoother flight trajectories compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic-TD3 demonstrates that integrating CMDP-based safety, long-horizon intention modeling (ATREM), physically informed state estimation (PAG-KF), and Lagrangian-based policy optimization can maintain maneuverability while enforcing strict safety in DRL-controlled drones.

title: Do Open-Loop Metrics Predict Closed-Loop Driving? A Cross-Benchmark Correlation Study of NAVSIM and Bench2Drive
summary: Open-loop evaluation offers fast, reproducible assessment of autonomous driving planners, but its ability to predict real closed-loop driving performance remains questionable. Prior work has shown that traditional open-loop metrics such as Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE) exhibit no reliable correlation with closed-loop Driving Score. In this paper, we ask whether the more recent, safety-aware open-loop metrics introduced by NAVSIM~v2 can bridge this gap. By systematically cross-referencing published results from 15 state-of-the-art methods across NAVSIM (open-loop) and Bench2Drive (closed-loop), we compile a paired dataset of open-loop sub-metrics and closed-loop performance, yielding 8 methods with complete paired data. Our analysis reveals three key findings: (1) the aggregate NAVSIM PDM Score shows a strong positive but non-monotonic correlation with Bench2Drive Driving Score, with clear ranking inversions; (2) among individual NAVSIM sub-metrics, Ego Progress (EP) is the strongest single predictor of closed-loop success, substantially exceeding the safety-critical collision metric NC; (3) the safety-progress trade-off manifests differently in open-loop and closed-loop: methods that maximize safety at the expense of progress rank highly in NAVSIM but underperform in closed-loop due to timeout and slow-driving penalties. We further demonstrate that a much simpler 3-metric formula matches the predictive power of the full 5-metric PDMS at the same Spearman $ρ{=}0.90$ on our paired sample of $n{=}8$ methods, suggesting that within current state-of-the-art methods -- where TTC and Comfort approach saturation -- these two sub-metrics add little marginal information for closed-loop ranking. Additionally, we identify the snowball effect -- where small open-loop deviations compound into closed-loop failures -- as a candidate mechanism for the residual gap.

AI.tldr: NAVSIM open-loop metrics predict closed-loop Driving Score with limitations: the aggregate metric shows non-monotonic correlation and ranking inversions; Ego Progress is the strongest single predictor; a simple 3-metric formula matches the full PDMS; safety-progress trade-off and snowball effects explain residual gaps.
AI.motivation: Assess whether NAVSIM v2 open-loop safety-aware metrics can bridge the gap to closed-loop driving performance, addressing limitations of traditional ADE/FDE correlations.
AI.method: Cross-reference 15 state-of-the-art methods across NAVSIM open-loop and Bench2Drive closed-loop results, compiling a paired dataset; identify 8 methods with complete data; analyze correlations between open-loop sub-metrics and closed-loop Driving Score; compare multi-metric PDMS with a reduced 3-metric model; explore saturation effects (TTC and Comfort) and the 'snowball' mechanism.
AI.result: (1) NAVSIM PDM Score correlates positively with Bench2Drive Driving Score but non-monotonically; ranking inversions observed. (2) Ego Progress (EP) is the strongest single predictor of closed-loop success, outperforming NC (safety metric). (3) Methods prioritizing safety over progress rank high in NAVSIM but perform worse in closed-loop due to timeouts and slow driving. A 3-metric formula matches the PDMS’s predictive power at Spearman ρ = 0.90 on n=8, suggesting TTC and Comfort saturation leaves two sub-metrics with little marginal information. The snowball effect—small open-loop deviations amplifying into closed-loop failures—is proposed as a mechanism for the residual gap.
AI.conclusion: Open-loop NAVSIM metrics provide limited, non-monotonic predictive power for closed-loop performance. A compact 3-metric model can match the PDMS’s predictive ability, reducing complexity. Caution is needed regarding safety-only optimization, as it can hurt closed-loop outcomes due to timeouts and slow driving; the snowball effect may underlie the remaining gap and should be investigated further.

title: Smallest Enclosing Disk Queries Using Farthest-Point Voronoi Diagrams
summary: Let $S$ be a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Our goal is to preprocess $S$ to efficiently compute the smallest enclosing disk of the points in $S$ that lie inside an axis-aligned query rectangle. Previous data structures for this problem achieve a query time of $O(\log^6 n)$ with $O(n \log^2 n)$ preprocessing time and space by lifting the points to 3D, dualizing them into polyhedra, and searching through their intersections. We present a significantly simpler approach, solely based on 2D geometric structures, specifically 2D farthest-point Voronoi diagrams. Our approach achieves a deterministic query time of $O(\log^4 n)$ and, via randomization, an expected query time of $O(\log^{5/2} n \log\log n)$ with the same preprocessing bounds.

AI.tldr: New 2D-based data structure for axis-aligned-range MEC queries: replaces a prior 3D lifting approach with a purely 2D method using farthest-point Voronoi diagrams. Achieves deterministic O(log^4 n) query time and randomized O(log^{5/2} n log log n) expected time, with preprocessing still O(n log^2 n).
AI.motivation: Efficiently compute the smallest enclosing disk of the subset of points within an axis-aligned query rectangle. The existing solution relies on lifting to 3D, dualizing into polyhedra, and searching intersections, yielding O(log^6 n) query time; a simpler, 2D approach is desirable.
AI.method: Develops a deterministic 2D data-structure based entirely on 2D geometric constructs, notably 2D farthest-point Voronoi diagrams. Encodes how the MEC of the rectilinearly-constrained subset changes with the query rectangle and enables query by planar point location within augmented 2D structures. Achieves O(log^4 n) deterministic queries; with randomization, expected O(log^{5/2} n log log n) queries.
AI.result: Demonstrates significantly improved query time over the previous 3D-lifting method while maintaining the same O(n log^2 n) preprocessing/space bounds.
AI.conclusion: A simpler, strictly 2D approach using farthest-point Voronoi diagrams yields competitive to improved query performance for axis-aligned rectangle MEC queries, with a deterministic optimum and a randomized trade-off.

title: Being-H0.7: A Latent World-Action Model from Egocentric Videos
summary: Visual-Language-Action models (VLAs) have advanced generalist robot control by mapping multimodal observations and language instructions directly to actions, but sparse action supervision often encourages shortcut mappings rather than representations of dynamics, contact, and task progress. Recent world-action models introduce future prediction through video rollouts, yet pixel-space prediction is a costly and indirect substrate for control, as it may model visual details irrelevant to action generation and introduces substantial training or inference overhead. We present Being-H0.7, a latent world-action model that brings future-aware reasoning into VLA-style policies without generating future frames. Being-H0.7 inserts learnable latent queries between perception and action as a compact reasoning interface, and trains them with a future-informed dual-branch design: a deployable prior branch infers latent states from the current context, while a training-only posterior branch replaces the queries with embeddings from future observations. Jointly aligning the two branches at the latent reasoning space leads the prior branch to reason future-aware, action-useful structure from current observations alone. At inference, Being-H0.7 discards the posterior branch and performs no visual rollout. Experiments across six simulation benchmarks and diverse real-world tasks show that Being-H0.7 achieves state-of-the-art or comparable performance, combining the predictive benefits of world models with the efficiency and deployability of direct VLA policies.

AI.tldr: Latent world-action model Being-H0.7 enables future-aware reasoning in VLA policies without pixel-space rollouts, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive results across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current VLAs with sparse action supervision risk shortcut mappings and existing world-action models rely on costly pixel-space video rollouts; there is a need for future-aware reasoning that is deployable and efficient.
AI.method: Introduce learnable latent queries between perception and action. Use a dual-branch training: (1) a deployable prior branch infers latent states from current context, (2) a training-only posterior branch replaces the queries with embeddings from future observations. Align the two branches in latent space. Inference uses only the prior branch and does not perform visual rollouts.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art or comparable performance across six simulation benchmarks and diverse real-world tasks, combining predictive benefits of world models with the efficiency and deployability of direct VLA policies.
AI.conclusion: Provides future-aware reasoning in VLA-style policies without expensive video prediction, blending world-model predictive capabilities with efficient, deployable action-conditioned perception.

title: World Model for Robot Learning: A Comprehensive Survey
summary: World models, which are predictive representations of how environments evolve under actions, have become a central component of robot learning. They support policy learning, planning, simulation, evaluation, data generation, and have advanced rapidly with the rise of foundation models and large-scale video generation. However, the literature remains fragmented across architectures, functional roles, and embodied application domains. To address this gap, we present a comprehensive review of world models from a robot-learning perspective. We examine how world models are coupled with robot policies, how they serve as learned simulators for reinforcement learning and evaluation, and how robotic video world models have progressed from imagination-based generation to controllable, structured, and foundation-scale formulations. We further connect these ideas to navigation and autonomous driving, and summarize representative datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation protocols. Overall, this survey systematically reviews the rapidly growing literature on world models for robot learning, clarifies key paradigms and applications, and highlights major challenges and future directions for predictive modeling in embodied agents. To facilitate continued access to newly emerging works, benchmarks, and resources, we will maintain and regularly update the accompanying GitHub repository alongside this survey.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of world models for robot learning, unifying architectures, roles, and embodied domains, with datasets/benchmarks and a living GitHub resource.
AI.motivation: Literature on world models is fragmented across architectures, roles, and domains; a unified perspective is needed to organize, compare, and guide predictive modeling for embodied agents.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and taxonomy: categorize world models by function (policy coupling, learned simulators, evaluation/benchmarks, video/vision-based world models) and by domain (robotics, navigation, autonomous driving); synthesize datasets and benchmarks and evaluation protocols; identify challenges and future directions; provide a maintained GitHub resource.
AI.result: Clarified landscape of world-model literature for robot learning; explicit paradigms, relationships, and design choices; gaps and challenges identified; a living resource for ongoing updates.
AI.conclusion: The survey maps key paradigms and applications, highlights major challenges, and outlines future directions for predictive modeling in embodied agents; promotes community engagement and continuous updates via the GitHub repository.

title: Cloud Is Closer Than It Appears: Revisiting the Tradeoffs of Distributed Real-Time Inference
summary: The increasing deployment of deep neural networks (DNNs) in cyber-physical systems (CPS) enhances perception fidelity, but imposes substantial computational demands on execution platforms, posing challenges to real-time control deadlines. Traditional distributed CPS architectures typically favor on-device inference to avoid network variability and contention-induced delays on remote platforms. However, this design choice places significant energy and computational demands on the local hardware. In this work, we revisit the assumption that cloud-based inference is intrinsically unsuitable for latency-sensitive control tasks. We demonstrate that, when provisioned with high-throughput compute resources, cloud platforms can effectively amortize network and queueing delays, enabling them to match or surpass on-device performance for real-time decision-making. Specifically, we develop a formal analytical model that characterizes distributed inference latency as a function of the sensing frequency, platform throughput, network delay, and task-specific safety constraints. We instantiate this model in the context of emergency braking for autonomous driving and validate it through extensive simulations using real-time vehicular dynamics. Our empirical results identify concrete conditions under which cloud-based inference adheres to safety margins more reliably than its on-device counterpart. These findings challenge prevailing design strategies and suggest that the cloud is not merely a feasible option, but often the preferred inference location for distributed CPS architectures. In this light, the cloud is not as distant as traditionally perceived; in fact, it is closer than it appears.

AI.tldr: Cloud-based inference can match or exceed on-device latency for latency-sensitive CPS tasks when provided with high-throughput cloud resources, by amortizing network and queueing delays; demonstrated via emergency braking in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Challenging the prevailing view that cloud inference is unsuitable for real-time control due to network latency; investigate whether cloud platforms can meet strict timing and safety constraints in distributed CPS.
AI.method: Develop a formal analytical model of distributed inference latency as a function of sensing frequency, platform throughput, network delay, and task-specific safety constraints; instantiate in emergency braking for autonomous driving; validate via extensive simulations with real-time vehicular dynamics; compare cloud versus on-device performance under varying conditions.
AI.result: The model and simulations identify concrete conditions where cloud-based inference adheres to safety margins more reliably than on-device execution; under high-throughput cloud resources, network delays can be amortized to achieve competitive or superior real-time performance; results challenge conventional design choices in distributed CPS.
AI.conclusion: Cloud inference should be considered a viable, and often preferred, location for distributed CPS inference when compute resources are provisioned appropriately; the cloud can be closer in terms of effective latency than previously assumed, motivating re-evaluation of edge-vs-cloud deployment strategies.

title: Learning from the Unseen: Generative Data Augmentation for Geometric-Semantic Accident Anticipation
summary: Anticipating traffic accidents is a critical yet unresolved problem for autonomous driving, hindered by the inherent complexity of modeling interactions between road users and the limited availability of diverse, large-scale datasets. To address these issues, we propose a dual-path framework. On the one hand, we employ a video synthesis pipeline that, guided by structured prompts, derives feature distributions from existing corpora and produces high-fidelity synthetic driving scenes consistent with the statistical patterns of real data. On the other hand, we design a graph neural network enriched with semantic cues, enabling dynamic reasoning over both spatial and semantic relations among participants. To validate the effectiveness of our approach, we release a new benchmark dataset containing standardized, finely annotated video sequences that cover a broad spectrum of regions, weather, and traffic conditions. Evaluations across existing datasets and our new benchmark confirm notable gains in both accuracy and anticipation lead time, highlighting the capacity of the proposed framework to mitigate current data bottlenecks and enhance the reliability of autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: Dual-path framework for anticipatory driving: synthetic data generation via a prompts-guided video synthesis pipeline and a semantic-aware GNN for dynamic interaction reasoning; new benchmark dataset; results show improved accuracy and lead time.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and the complexity of modeling multi-agent interactions hinder reliable accident anticipation in autonomous driving.
AI.method: 1) A video synthesis pipeline guided by structured prompts that derives feature distributions from real data and generates high-fidelity synthetic driving scenes matching statistical patterns. 2) A graph neural network enriched with semantic cues to reason over spatial and semantic relations among road users. 3) Release of a standardized, finely annotated video dataset covering diverse regions, weather, and traffic conditions. 4) Evaluation on existing datasets and the new benchmark.
AI.result: Notable gains in accuracy and anticipation lead time across existing datasets and the new benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The combination of synthetic data generation and semantic-aware graph reasoning mitigates data bottlenecks and enhances the reliability of autonomous driving systems for accident anticipation; the new dataset supports robust evaluation and further research.

title: Predictive Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs for Semi-Static Scenes
summary: We have seen tremendous recent progress in our ability to build "spatio-semantic" representations that enable robots to perform complex reasoning across geometry and semantics. However, the vast majority of these methods lack any ability to perform reasoning across time. This is a desirable property in situations where a robot repeatedly observes an environment where instances may change in between observations, but in a structured way. Consider as an example a home environment where the location of a mug typically moves from the cupboard to a countertop to the sink and then back to the cupboard on a daily basis. We should be able to learn this cyclic behavior and use it to predict the state of the mug in the future. In this work, we propose a method that is able to perform this type of tempo-spatio-semantic reasoning. Underpinning the method is a filter, Perpetua$^*$, that performs Bayesian reasoning on the states of the environment that are observed over time. This filter is integrated within a 3D scene graph structure that we call PredictiveGraphs, where nodes represent objects and edges function as Perpetua$^*$ filters encoding spatio-semantic relationships. We validate the method in both simulation and real-world dynamic navigation tasks, where our real world experiments consist of an environment that is undergoing semi-static changes at a bi-hourly frequency over a period of three weeks. In both settings, we demonstrate that our method outperforms baselines in predicting future environment states, even in the presence of distributional shifts.

AI.tldr: Introduces tempo-spatio-semantic reasoning for robots by embedding a Bayesian temporal filter (Perpetua*) into a 3D scene-graph (PredictiveGraphs) to model and predict cyclic semi-static changes in environments.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of temporal reasoning in predominantly spatial/semantic scene representations. Enables robots to predict how objects move in semi-static, cyclic patterns (e.g., mugs moving between cupboard, countertop, sink) over time.
AI.method: A Perpetua* Bayesian filter tracks the temporally evolving states of observed environment elements. This filter is integrated into PredictiveGraphs, where nodes are objects and edges carry Perpetua* filters to encode spatio-semantic temporal relationships. The approach is validated in both simulation and real-world dynamic navigation tasks, including a long-term study with semi-static changes bi-hourly over three weeks.
AI.result: The method outperforms baselines in predicting future environment states and remains robust under distributional shifts, demonstrated across simulation and real-world experiments with bi-hourly cyclic changes over three weeks.
AI.conclusion: Tempo-spatio-semantic reasoning via a Bayesian temporal filter within a 3D scene-graph enables accurate long-horizon predictions of evolving environments, addressing a key gap in dynamic robotic perception.

title: FedACT: Concurrent Federated Intelligence across Heterogeneous Data Sources
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative intelligence across decentralized data source devices in a privacy-preserving way. While substantial research attention has been drawn to optimizing the learning process for an individual task, real-world applications increasingly require multiple machine learning tasks simultaneously training their models across a shared pool of devices. Naively applying single-FL optimization techniques in multi-FL systems results in suboptimal system performance, particularly due to device heterogeneity and resource inefficiency. To address such a critical open challenge, we introduce {\em FedACT}, a novel resource heterogeneity-aware device scheduling approach designed to efficiently schedule heterogeneous devices across multiple concurrent FL jobs, with the goal of minimizing their average job completion time (JCT). {\em FedACT} dynamically assigns devices to FL jobs based on an alignment scoring mechanism that evaluates the compatibility between available resources of devices and resource demands of jobs. Additionally, it incorporates participation fairness to ensure balanced contributions from devices across jobs, further enhancing the accuracy levels of learned global models. An optimal scheduling plan is formulated in {\em FedACT} by prioritizing devices with higher alignment scores, while ensuring fair participation across jobs. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed scheduling algorithm, we carried out comprehensive experiments using diverse FL jobs and benchmark datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that {\em FedACT} reduces the average JCT by up to 8.3\(\times\) and improves model accuracy by up to 44.5\%, compared to the state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: FedACT is a heterogeneity-aware device scheduler for concurrent federated learning (FL) tasks that aligns device resources with job demands to minimize average job completion time (JCT) while ensuring fair participation, achieving notable improvements in speed and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Real-world FL systems with multiple concurrent tasks suffer from device heterogeneity and inefficient resource utilization. Naive single-task FL optimization fails to scale across multiple tasks on shared devices, leading to suboptimal performance and longer completion times.
AI.method: FedACT uses an alignment scoring mechanism that evaluates compatibility between a device's available resources and a job's resource demands. Devices are dynamically assigned to FL jobs based on higher alignment scores, with fairness constraints to balance contributions across jobs. The scheduler formulates an optimal plan prioritizing high-scoring devices while maintaining participation fairness across all FL jobs.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on diverse FL tasks and benchmark datasets shows FedACT reduces average job completion time (JCT) by up to 8.3× and improves model accuracy by up to 44.5% compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: FedACT effectively tackles resource heterogeneity in multi-task FL, delivering faster training times and higher accuracy, illustrating its potential for practical deployment in multi-job FL environments.

title: AgentReputation: A Decentralized Agentic AI Reputation Framework
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, accepted to FSE 2026, Ideas, Visions and Reflections track
summary: Decentralized, agentic AI marketplaces are rapidly emerging to support software engineering tasks such as debugging, patch generation, and security auditing, often operating without centralized oversight. However, existing reputation mechanisms fail in this setting for three fundamental reasons: agents can strategically optimize against evaluation procedures; demonstrated competence does not reliably transfer across heterogeneous task contexts; and verification rigor varies widely, from lightweight automated checks to costly expert review. Current approaches to reputation drawing on federated learning, blockchain-based AI platforms, and large language model safety research are unable to address these challenges in combination. We therefore propose \textbf{AgentReputation}, a decentralized, three-layer reputation framework for agentic AI systems. The framework separates task execution, reputation services, and tamper-proof persistence to both leverage their respective strengths and enable independent evolution. The framework introduces explicit verification regimes linked to agent reputation metadata, as well as context-conditioned reputation cards that prevent reputation conflation across domains and task types. In addition, AgentReputation provides a decision-facing policy engine that supports resource allocation, access control, and adaptive verification escalation based on risk and uncertainty. Building on this framework, we outline several future research directions, including the development of verification ontologies, methods for quantifying verification strength, privacy-preserving evidence mechanisms, cold-start reputation bootstrapping, and defenses against adversarial manipulation.

AI.tldr: A decentralized, three-layer AgentReputation framework for agentic AI systems to address reputation challenges across task contexts, featuring explicit verification regimes, context-conditioned reputation cards, and a policy engine for risk-aware decisions; outlines future research directions in verification ontologies, evidence mechanisms, cold-start bootstrapping, and defenses against adversarial manipulation.
AI.motivation: Reputation mechanisms in decentralized AI marketplaces face strategic manipulation, poor cross-domain transfer of demonstrated competence, and heterogeneous verification rigor. No existing approach robustly addresses all three simultaneously.
AI.method: Propose AgentReputation, a three-layer architecture with independent layers for task execution, reputation services, and tamper-proof persistence. Introduce verification regimes tied to reputation metadata, and context-conditioned reputation cards to avoid domain/task conflation. Include a decision-facing policy engine for resource allocation, access control, and adaptive verification escalation based on risk/uncertainty.
AI.result: A conceptual design and blueprint for a decentralized reputation framework, along with concrete components and interactions; no empirical results are reported, but the paper outlines viable future research directions and potential implementations.
AI.conclusion: AgentReputation offers a structured, extensible pathway to robust reputation management in agentic AI systems by separating concerns and elevating verification standards, while acknowledging numerous future challenges in ontologies, privacy, bootstrapping, and adversarial resistance.

title: Two-View Accumulation as the Primary Training Lever for Hybrid-Capture Gaussian Splatting: A Variance-Decomposition View of When Gradient Surgery Helps
summary:
Hybrid-capture novel view synthesis combines images at substantially different camera distances (e.g., aerial drone and ground-level views). Standard 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), trained for 30K iterations with one rendered view per optimizer step, under-fits the minority regime by 1-3 dB on five hybrid-capture benchmarks. We isolate the lever that closes this gap.
  Among compute-matched alternatives -- vanilla 60K iterations, magnitude corrections (GradNorm), direction-aware near/far gradient surgery, projective preconditioning, confidence-gated sample-level surgery, and a random two-view-per-step control -- the simplest structural change wins: rendering two views per optimizer step. The pairing rule (geometry-defined near/far, random, or active loss-disparity) does not change PSNR beyond seed variance on any of the five scenes; the structural change of having two views per step does.
  We propose a variance-decomposition framework that predicts and explains this finding: under bimodal camera regimes, between-regime gradient variance turns out to be small relative to within-regime variance in 3DGS, so structured and random pairings are variance-equivalent in expectation, and the variance halving from two-view accumulation itself is the dominant effect. We verify the framework on five scenes whose camera-altitude bimodality coefficients span [0.55, 1.00], and we report the negative result that direction-aware projection, magnitude correction, confidence gating, and an active loss-disparity pairing all fall within seed variance of random two-view pairing. The two-view structural lever transfers cleanly to the Scaffold-GS and Pixel-GS backbones.
  We position this work as an honest characterization of which training-side axes do and do not move PSNR for hybrid-capture 3DGS, together with the framework that explains why.

AI.tldr: Rendering two views per optimization step is the sole training-side lever that consistently improves PSNR for hybrid-capture 3DGS across five scenes; a variance-decomposition explains why other training-side tweaks fail.
AI.motivation: Identify which training-time choices move PSNR in bimodal camera regimes and explain variances observed across hybrid-capture scenarios.
AI.method: Compare compute-matched alternatives (60K iterations, GradNorm, direction-aware near/far gradient surgery, projective preconditioning, confidence-gated sample-level surgery, and a random two-view-per-step control) to a simple two-view-per-step change; develop a variance-decomposition framework; test on five scenes with varying camera-altitude bimodality; verify transfer to Scaffold-GS and Pixel-GS backbones.
AI.result: Two-view-per-step yields variance-halving and PSNR gains; other methods show no significant improvement beyond seed variance; variance framework explains why two-view accumulation dominates; lever transfers to multiple backbones.
AI.conclusion: Provide an honest characterization of training-side axes affecting PSNR for hybrid-capture 3DGS and present a variance-explanation framework; emphasize two-view-per-step as a robust, transferable lever across backbones.

title: E$^2$DT: Efficient and Effective Decision Transformer with Experience-Aware Sampling for Robotic Manipulation
summary: In reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic manipulation, the Decision Transformer (DT) has emerged as an effective framework for addressing long-horizon tasks. However, DT's performance depends heavily on the coverage of collected experiences. Without an active exploration mechanism, standard DT relies on uniform replay, which leads to poor sample efficiency, limited exploration, and reduced overall effectiveness. At the same time, while excessive exploration can help avoid local optima, it often delays policy convergence and leads to degraded efficiency. To address these limitations, we propose E$^2$DT, a DT-guided k-Determinantal Point Process sampling framework that enables the model to actively shape its own experience selection. Our framework is experience-aware, allowing E$^2$DT to be both efficient, by prioritizing sampling quality, such as high-return, high-uncertainty, and underrepresented trajectories, and effective, by ensuring diversity across trajectory windows to preserve policy optimality. Specifically, DT's internal latent embeddings measure diversity across trajectory windows, while quality is quantified through a composite metric that integrates return-to-go (RTG) quantiles, predictive uncertainty, and stage coverage based on inverse frequency. These two dimensions are integrated into a novel quality-diversity joint kernel that prioritizes the most informative experiences, thereby enabling learning that is both efficient and effective. We evaluate E$^2$DT on challenging robotic manipulation benchmarks in both simulation and real-robot settings. Results show that it consistently outperforms prior methods. These findings demonstrate that coupling policy learning with experience-aware sampling provides a principled path toward robust long-horizon robotic learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes E^2DT, an experience-aware DT sampling framework using a k-Determinantal Point Process to actively shape replay by balancing quality and diversity of experiences, improving sample efficiency and long-horizon learning in robotic manipulation; validated in simulation and on real robots with consistent gains.
AI.motivation: DTs depend on uniform replay, which leads to poor sample efficiency and limited exploration. There is a need for principled, active experience selection to balance exploration and convergence speed without sacrificing policy optimality.
AI.method: Introduce E^2DT: a DT-guided k-DPP sampling framework. Use DT latent embeddings to quantify diversity across trajectory windows. Define a quality metric that combines return-to-go (RTG) quantiles, predictive uncertainty, and stage coverage via inverse frequency. Integrate these into a quality-diversity joint kernel to prioritize informative experiences. Sample experiences through the k-DPP mechanism to create efficient, diverse training batches for DT.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on challenging robotic manipulation benchmarks in simulation and on real robots shows that E^2DT consistently outperforms prior sampling methods, indicating improved sample efficiency and policy performance for long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: Coupling policy learning with an experience-aware sampling strategy yields a principled and effective path toward robust long-horizon robotic learning, combining efficiency (quality-focused sampling) with effectiveness (diversity-preserving exploration).

title: What Physics do Data-Driven MoCap-to-Radar Models Learn?
summary: Data-driven MoCap-to-radar models generate plausible micro-Doppler spectrograms, but do they actually learn the underlying physics? We introduce a physics-based interpretability framework to answer this question via two proposed complementary metrics: one measures alignment between model predictions and the physics-derived Doppler frequency, while the other tests whether predictions preserve the velocity-frequency relationship under velocity intervention. Both metrics require only MoCap input and model predictions, without access to measured radar data. Experiments across several model architectures reveal that low reconstruction error does not guarantee physical consistency: some, but not all, models achieve low error yet perform poorly on the two physics-based metrics. Further analysis shows that temporal attention is critical for transformer-based models to learn the underlying physics.

AI.tldr: Introduces a physics-based interpretability framework for MoCap-to-radar models using two metrics; reveals that low reconstruction error does not guarantee physical consistency; highlights the importance of temporal attention for learning physics in transformer models.
AI.motivation: To determine whether data-driven MoCap-to-radar models capture the underlying physics of radar micro-Doppler signatures rather than merely fitting data.
AI.method: Proposes two complementary, physics-based metrics that require only MoCap input and model predictions: (1) alignment between model-predicted Doppler frequency and physics-derived Doppler frequency, and (2) preservation of the velocity–frequency relationship under velocity intervention. Evaluated across several model architectures; dependence on temporal attention analyzed for transformer models.
AI.result: Across architectures, some models achieve low reconstruction error yet fail the physics-based metrics, indicating a mismatch with the underlying physics. Temporal attention is crucial for transformer-based models to learn the physics.
AI.conclusion: A physics-based interpretability framework can reveal whether data-driven MoCap-to-radar models truly capture physics, not just data-fitting. Temporal mechanisms, especially in transformers, play a key role in learning physics; future work could integrate physics into training or architecture design.

title: Minimal, Local, Causal Explanations for Jailbreak Success in Large Language Models
summary: Safety trained large language models (LLMs) can often be induced to answer harmful requests through jailbreak prompts. Because we lack a robust understanding of why LLMs are susceptible to jailbreaks, future frontier models operating more autonomously in higher-stakes settings may similarly be vulnerable to such attacks. Prior work has studied jailbreak success by examining the model's intermediate representations, identifying directions in this space that causally encode concepts like harmfulness and refusal. Then, they globally explain all jailbreak attacks as attempting to reduce or strengthen these concepts (e.g., reduce harmfulness). However, different jailbreak strategies may succeed by strengthening or suppressing different intermediate concepts, and the same jailbreak strategy may not work for different harmful request categories (e.g., violence vs. cyberattack); thus, we seek to give a local explanation -- i.e., why did this specific jailbreak succeed? To address this gap, we introduce LOCA, a method that gives Local, CAusal explanations of jailbreak success by identifying a minimal set of interpretable, intermediate representation changes that causally induce model refusal on an otherwise successful jailbreak request. We evaluate LOCA on harmful original-jailbreak pairs from a large jailbreak benchmark across Gemma and Llama chat models, comparing against prior methods adapted to this setting. LOCA can successfully induce refusal by making, on average, six interpretable changes; prior work routinely fails to achieve refusal even after 20 changes. LOCA is a step toward mechanistic, local explanations of jailbreak success in LLMs. Code to be released.

AI.tldr: LOCA provides Local, Causal explanations for jailbreak success in LLMs by identifying a minimal set of interpretable intermediate representation changes that causally induce refusal on a jailbreak prompt, outperforming prior global explanation methods.
AI.motivation: Global, post-hoc explanations of jailbreaks are insufficient for understanding why a specific jailbreak succeeds. There is a need for local, mechanistic explanations to capture strategy- and category-specific effects, especially as models operate in higher-stakes settings.
AI.method: LOCA identifies a minimal, interpretable set of intermediate representations whose causal manipulation causes the model to refuse a jailbreak. This yields local explanations for a given jailbreak instance rather than a global trend. The authors evaluate LOCA on harmful jailbreak pairs from a large benchmark across Gemma and Llama chat models, comparing against adapted prior methods.
AI.result: On average, LOCA induces refusal by making six interpretable changes; prior methods require up to 20 changes and still often fail to induce refusal.
AI.conclusion: LOCA advances mechanistic, local explanations of jailbreak success in LLMs and provides a pathway toward understanding model vulnerability at the level of concrete, interpretable intermediate representations. They plan to release code.

title: AIDA-ReID: Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation for Generalizable and Source-Free Person Re-Identification
summary: Person re-identification (Re-ID) aims to match images of the same individual across non-overlapping camera views and remains challenging due to domain shifts caused by variations in illumination, background, camera characteristics, and population distributions. Although supervised models perform well under matched training and testing conditions, their performance degrades significantly when deployed in unseen environments. Existing intermediate domain approaches such as IDM and IDM++ alleviate this gap by constructing bridge feature distributions between domains; however, they rely on fixed mixing strategies and joint source-target access, limiting their applicability to multi-source and source-free settings. To address these limitations, this paper proposes Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation (AIDA), also referred to as Source-Free Multi-Source Intermediate Domain Adaptation (SF-MIDA). The proposed framework treats intermediate-domain learning as a dynamically regulated process, where feature mixing and regularization strength are adaptively controlled using feedback signals derived from model uncertainty and training stability. A multi-source intermediate domain generator synthesizes diverse intermediate representations, while a pseudo-mirror regularization strategy preserves identity consistency under domain perturbations. Extensive experiments across domain generalization and source-free settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation for person re-identification (SF-MIDA): a dynamic, source-free, multi-source approach that learns intermediate domains via an adaptive mixing/regulation mechanism guided by uncertainty and training stability, with a multi-source intermediate-domain generator and pseudo-mirror regularization to preserve identity under perturbations; proven effective in domain generalization and source-free settings.
AI.motivation: Domain shifts (illumination, background, camera, population) cause performance drop when models are deployed out of the training environment. Existing intermediate-domain methods (IDM/IDM++) rely on fixed mixing and joint access to source/target, limiting multi-source and source-free applicability.
AI.method: Introduce Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation (AIDA)/SF-MIDA that treats intermediate-domain learning as a dynamically regulated process. It adaptively controls feature mixing and regularization strength using feedback from model uncertainty and training stability. A multi-source intermediate-domain generator creates diverse intermediate representations. Pseudo-mirror regularization enforces identity consistency under domain perturbations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate effectiveness across domain generalization and source-free settings, indicating improved bridging of domain gaps and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive, source-free, multi-source intermediate-domain framework effectively mitigates domain shifts in Re-ID, enabling better generalization across unseen environments and under source-free adaptation.

title: Lucid-XR: An Extended-Reality Data Engine for Robotic Manipulation
summary: We introduce Lucid-XR, a generative data engine for creating diverse and realistic-looking multi-modal data to train real-world robotic systems. At the core of Lucid-XR is vuer, a web-based physics simulation environment that runs directly on the XR headset, enabling internet-scale access to immersive, latency-free virtual interactions without requiring specialized equipment. The complete system integrates on-device physics simulation with human-to-robot pose retargeting. Data collected is further amplified by a physics-guided video generation pipeline steerable via natural language specifications. We demonstrate zero-shot transfer of robot visual policies to unseen, cluttered, and badly lit evaluation environments, after training entirely on Lucid-XR's synthetic data. We include examples across dexterous manipulation tasks that involve soft materials, loosely bound particles, and rigid body contact. Project website: https://lucidxr.github.io

AI.tldr: Lucid-XR is a generative data engine for multi-modal synthetic data to train real-world robots, using on-device vuer physics in XR headsets and NL-guided video generation to achieve zero-shot policy transfer in unseen environments.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, realistic synthetic data and robust policy transfer for robotics, while minimizing reliance on external hardware and latency; enable immersive data collection on-device.
AI.method: Lucid-XR framework; vuer web-based physics sim running on XR headset; on-device physics with human-to-robot pose retargeting; physics-guided video generation pipeline steerable by natural language; zero-shot training and evaluation on synthetic data; example tasks with soft materials, loose particles, and rigid contact.
AI.result: Zero-shot transfer of robot visual policies to unseen cluttered, poorly lit environments after training on Lucid-XR synthetic data; demonstrated across dexterous manipulation tasks involving soft materials and complex contacts.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of integrated on-device synthetic data generation and NL-guided augmentation for robust robotic perception and manipulation; enables scalable, internet-scale data generation; project site.

title: AirFM-DDA: Air-Interface Foundation Model in the Delay-Doppler-Angle Domain for AI-Native 6G
summary: The success of large foundation models is catalyzing a new paradigm for AI-native 6G network design: wireless foundation models for physical layer design. However, existing models often operate on channel state information (CSI) in the space-time-frequency (STF) domain, where distinct multipath components are inherently superimposed and structurally entangled. This hinders the learning of universal channel representation. Meanwhile, their reliance on global attention mechanisms incurs prohibitive computational overhead. In this paper, we propose AirFM-DDA, an Air-interface Foundation Model operating in the Delay-Doppler-Angle (DDA) domain for physicallayer tasks. Specifically, AirFM-DDA reparameterizes CSI from the STF domain into the DDA domain to explicitly resolve multipath components along physically meaningful axes. It employs a window-based attention module augmented with framestructure-aware positional encoding (FS-PE). This window-based attention aligns with locally clustered multipath dependencies while avoiding quadratic-complexity global attention, and FS-PE injects frame-structure priors into network. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AirFM-DDA achieves superior zero-shot generalization across unseen scenarios and datasets, consistently outperforming the baselines on channel prediction and estimation tasks. Compared to the global attention, its window-based attention reduces training and inference costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Moreover, AirFM-DDA maintains robustness under high mobility, large delay spreads, severe noise, and extreme aliasing conditions.

AI.tldr: AirFM-DDA is a wireless foundation model designed for the Delay-Doppler-Angle domain to improve physical-layer tasks by reparameterizing channels, using window-based attention and frame-structure-aware encoding for efficiency and robustness across unseen scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current STF-domain models entangle multipath components, hindering universal channel representations, and rely on computationally expensive global attention. A domain-redefined, efficient model can enhance generalization and scalability for PHY tasks.
AI.method: Reparameterize CSI from STF to DDA to separate multipath components along physical axes. Employ window-based attention to capture locally clustered dependencies with lower complexity, augmented by frame-structure-aware positional encoding (FS-PE) that injects frame priors.
AI.result: AirFM-DDA achieves superior zero-shot generalization across unseen datasets and scenarios, outperforms baselines on channel prediction and estimation, reduces training/inference cost by about an order of magnitude compared to global attention, and remains robust under high mobility, large delay spreads, strong noise, and severe aliasing.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that physical-domain reparameterization and localized attention enable effective, scalable wireless foundation models for PHY-layer tasks with strong generalization and efficiency benefits.

title: Are Tools All We Need? Unveiling the Tool-Use Tax in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-augmented reasoning has become a popular direction for LLM-based agents, and it is widely assumed to improve reasoning and reliability. However, we demonstrate that this consensus does not always hold: in the presence of semantic distractors, tool-augmented reasoning does not necessarily outperform native CoT. To explain this performance gap, we propose a Factorized Intervention Framework that isolates the cost of prompt formatting, the overhead of the tool-calling protocol, and the actual gain from executing tools. Our analysis reveals a critical tradeoff: under semantic noise, the gains from tools often fail to offset the "tool-use tax", which is the performance degradation introduced by the tool-calling protocol itself. To address this, we introduce G-STEP, a lightweight inference-time gate to mitigate protocol-induced errors. While this yields partial recovery, our findings suggest that more substantial improvements still require strengthening the model's intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction capabilities.

AI.tldr: Tool-augmented reasoning under semantic noise often incurs a 'tool-use tax' that can negate gains from tool usage; a Factorized Intervention Framework isolates costs and gains, and G-STEP mitigates protocol-induced errors, but substantial improvements require stronger intrinsic reasoning and tool interaction capabilities.
AI.motivation: To understand when tool-augmented reasoning actually improves performance, especially in the presence of semantic distractors, and to diagnose the sources of performance gaps.
AI.method: Proposes a Factorized Intervention Framework that deconstructs the total performance change into prompt-formatting cost, tool-calling overhead, and tool-execution gain; conducts analysis (likely theoretical and empirical) under semantic noise; introduces G-STEP as an inference-time gate to reduce protocol-induced errors.
AI.result: Finds that under semantic noise, tool gains are often insufficient to offset the tool-use tax; G-STEP provides partial recovery; overall, substantial improvements require bolstering the model's intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction capabilities.
AI.conclusion: To realize reliable tool-augmented reasoning, focus on strengthening internal reasoning and robust tool interaction, rather than relying solely on the addition of tools; further work needed to design protocols and model capabilities that can resist semantic distractors.

title: GAFSV-Net: A Vision Framework for Online Signature Verification
summary: Online signature verification (OSV) requires distinguishing skilled forgeries from genuine samples under high intra-class variability and with very few enrollment samples. Existing deep learning methods operate directly on raw temporal sequences, restricting them to 1D architectures and preventing the use of pretrained 2D vision backbones. We bridge this gap with GAFSV-Net, which represents each signature as a six-channel asymmetric Gramian Angular Field image: three kinematic channels (pen speed, pressure derivative, direction angle) are each encoded into complementary GASF and GADF matrices that capture pairwise temporal co-occurrence and directional transition structure respectively. A dual-branch ConvNeXt-Tiny encoder processes GASF and GADF independently, with bidirectional cross-attention enabling each branch to query discriminative patterns from the other before metric-space projection. Training uses semi-hard triplet loss with skilled-forgery hard-negative injection; verification is performed via cosine similarity against a small enrollment prototype. We evaluate on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID, outperforming all sequence-based baselines trained under identical objectives, demonstrating that the representational gain of 2D temporal encoding is consistent and independent of training procedure, with ablations characterising each design choice's contribution.

AI.tldr: Transforms online signatures into six-channel GAF images (three kinematic channels encoded as GASF and GADF) and uses a dual-branch ConvNeXt-Tiny with bidirectional cross-attention and semi-hard triplet loss; achieves state-of-the-art OSV results on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID with cosine-similarity enrollment.
AI.motivation: OSV faces high intra-class variability and very few enrollment samples. 1D sequence models limit to 1D architectures and can't leverage 2D pretrained backbones; a 2D representation could improve discriminative power and transferability.
AI.method: Represent each signature by six-channel Gramian Angular Field images: three kinematic channels (pen speed, pressure derivative, direction angle) encoded into complementary GASF and GADF matrices. Use a dual-branch ConvNeXt-Tiny encoder for GASF and GADF with bidirectional cross-attention to exchange discriminative patterns, followed by a metric-space projection. Train with semi-hard triplet loss and skilled-forgery hard-negative injection; verification via cosine similarity against a small enrollment prototype.
AI.result: Outperforms all sequence-based baselines trained under identical objectives on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID. Demonstrates that 2D temporal encoding yields consistent representational gains, with ablations validating each design choice.
AI.conclusion: 2D temporal encoding via Gramian Angular Fields provides robust representations for OSV that are largely independent of the training procedure; the proposed cross-attentive dual-branch GAFSV-Net effectively leverages 2D backbones for few-shot enrollment scenarios.

title: Task-Conditioned Uncertainty Costmaps for Legged Locomotion
summary: Legged robots maintain dynamic feasibility through multicontact interactions with terrain. Learned foothold prediction can provide feasibility-aware costs for motion planning and path selection, but accurately predicting future contacts from perceptual inputs such as height scans remains challenging on highly unstructured terrain, even with a repetitive gait cycle. In this work, we show that modeling epistemic uncertainty in predicted footholds, conditioned on terrain observations and commanded motion, distinguishes in-distribution from out-of-distribution operating regimes in simulation and real-world settings. This allows a single learned model, trained on limited data distributions, to express uncertainty caused by missing training coverage. We use this learned uncertainty to detect OOD regions and incorporate them into a unified costmap-generation framework for uncertainty-aware path planning. Using these uncertainty-aware costmaps, we evaluate feasibility error across in-distribution and OOD terrains in simulation and real-world settings. The results show improved OOD detection, up to a 37% reduction in simulation feasibility error, and more reliable planning behavior than geometry-only baselines.

AI.tldr: Model epistemic uncertainty in learned foothold predictions to detect out-of-distribution terrain and generate uncertainty-aware costmaps for planning, improving feasibility detection and planning reliability on unstructured terrains.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable legged locomotion on highly unstructured terrain by distinguishing what the model cannot reliably predict from what it can, and by integrating that uncertainty into planning costs.
AI.method: Train a single foothold predictor conditioned on terrain observations and commanded motion that models epistemic uncertainty; use the uncertainty to identify OOD regions; incorporate this into a unified costmap-generation framework for uncertainty-aware path planning; evaluate feasibility across in-distribution and OOD terrains in simulation and real-world tests.
AI.result: Improved OOD detection; up to 37% reduction in simulation feasibility error; more reliable planning than geometry-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware foothold prediction and planning yield more robust and reliable legged locomotion across diverse terrains; a single model with epistemic uncertainty can express missing training coverage and guide planning accordingly.

title: Learning physically grounded traffic accident reconstruction from public accident reports
summary: Traffic accidents are routinely documented in textual reports, yet physically grounded accident reconstruction remains difficult because detailed scene measurements and expert reconstructions are scarce, costly and hard to scale. Here we formulate accident reconstruction from publicly accessible reports and scene measurements as a parameterized multimodal learning problem. We construct CISS-REC, a dataset of 6,217 real-world accident cases curated from the NHTSA Crash Investigation Sampling System, and develop a reconstruction framework that grounds report semantics to road topology and participant attributes, reconstructs lane consistent pre-impact motion, and refines collision relevant interactions through localized geometric reasoning and temporal allocation. Our method outperforms representative baselines on CISS-REC, achieving the strongest overall reconstruction fidelity, including improved accident point accuracy and collision consistency. These results show that public accident reports can serve as scalable computational substrates for quantitatively verifiable accident reconstruction, with potential value for traffic safety analysis, simulation and autonomous driving research.

AI.tldr: Introduces CISS-REC dataset (6,217 real-world accidents) and a multimodal reconstruction framework that grounds textual reports to road topology and vehicle/participant attributes to infer lane-consistent pre-impact motion and refine interactions; achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity on the dataset.
AI.motivation: Public accident reports and sparse scene measurements hinder scalable, verifiable reconstruction; a data-efficient method is needed to enable quantitative traffic safety analysis, simulation, and autonomous driving research.
AI.method: Parameterized multimodal learning that grounds report semantics to road topology and participant attributes, reconstructs lane-consistent pre-impact motion, and refines collision-relevant interactions via localized geometric reasoning and temporal allocation; dataset derived from NHTSA CISS (6,217 cases).
AI.result: Outperforms representative baselines on CISS-REC, achieving the strongest overall reconstruction fidelity, with improved accident point accuracy and collision consistency.
AI.conclusion: Public accident reports can serve as scalable, quantitatively verifiable substrates for accident reconstruction, offering value for traffic safety analysis, simulation, and autonomous driving research.

title: TUR-DPO: Topology- and Uncertainty-Aware Direct Preference Optimization
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences is commonly done via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) or, more simply, via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). While DPO is stable and RL-free, it treats preferences as flat winner vs. loser signals and is sensitive to noisy or brittle preferences arising from fragile chains of thought. We propose TUR-DPO, a topology- and uncertainty-aware variant of DPO that rewards how answers are derived, not only what they say, by eliciting lightweight reasoning topologies and combining semantic faithfulness, utility, and topology quality into a calibrated uncertainty signal. A small learnable reward is factorized over these signals and incorporated into an uncertainty-weighted DPO objective that remains RL-free and relies only on a fixed or moving reference policy. Empirically, across open 7-8B models and benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, factual question answering, summarization, and helpful/harmless dialogue, TUR-DPO improves judge win-rates, faithfulness, and calibration relative to DPO while preserving training simplicity and avoiding online rollouts. We further observe consistent gains in multimodal and long-context settings, and show that TUR-DPO matches or exceeds PPO on reasoning-centric tasks while maintaining operational simplicity.

AI.tldr: TUR-DPO extends Direct Preference Optimization with topology- and uncertainty-aware rewards, yielding an RL-free, calibrated preference objective that improves faithfulness and reasoning performance relative to DPO and can match or beat PPO on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: DPO treats preferences as flat winner/loser signals and is vulnerable to noisy or brittle preferences from fragile chains of thought. Incorporating lightweight reasoning topologies and calibrated uncertainty aims to reward not just what answers say, but how they are derived, improving robustness and faithfulness.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable, factorized reward over semantic faithfulness, utility, and topology quality. Combine these into an uncertainty-weighted DPO objective that is RL-free and uses a fixed or moving reference policy. Elicit lightweight reasoning topologies during training and factorize a small reward to these signals.
AI.result: Across open 7–8B models and multiple benchmarks (math reasoning, factual QA, summarization, dialogue), TUR-DPO increases judge win-rates, improves faithfulness and calibration, and shows gains in multimodal and long-context settings. On reasoning-centric tasks, it matches or exceeds PPO while preserving simplicity and avoiding online rollouts.
AI.conclusion: TUR-DPO demonstrates that combining topology-aware reasoning signals with calibrated uncertainty can improve preference optimization without RL, maintaining simplicity and scalability, and offering competitive performance to PPO on reasoning tasks while enhancing faithfulness and calibration.

authors: Udayanga G. W. K. N. Gamage
title: Real-Time Frame- and Event-based Object Detection with Spiking Neural Networks on Edge Neuromorphic Hardware: Design, Deployment and Benchmark
summary: Real-time object detection on energy-constrained platforms is critical for applications such as UAV-based inspection, autonomous navigation, and mobile robotics. Spiking neural networks (SNNs) on neuromorphic hardware are believed to be significantly more energy-efficient than conventional artificial neural networks (ANNs). In this work, we present a comprehensive methodology for designing general SNN detection architectures targeting neuromorphic platforms, along with the engineering adaptations required to deploy them on the state-of-the-art Neuromorphic processor, Intel Loihi 2. We benchmark SNN-based object detection on Loihi 2 using both frame-based and event-based datasets, comparing performance with ANN-based detection on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, NVIDIA Jetson Nano B01, and the Apple M2 CPU. Our results show that SNNs on Loihi 2 can perform real-time detection while achieving the lowest per-inference dynamic energy among all platforms. Also, Loihi 2 outperforms the other platforms in terms of power consumption, though ANNs on Jetson Orin Nano achieve higher inference rates. Furthermore, our ANN-to-SNN distillation-aware training enables SNNs to recover 87-100% of the detection accuracy of their ANN counterparts while maintaining lower inference latency; without distillation, SNNs exhibit an 11-27% accuracy drop. These results highlight the potential of neuromorphic systems for energy-efficient, real-time object detection at the edge.

AI.tldr: A methodology for designing general SNN detection architectures on Loihi 2 and benchmarking against ANN baselines; demonstrates real-time, energy-efficient object detection on neuromorphic hardware with distillation-based accuracy recovery.
AI.motivation: Energy-constrained edge platforms require real-time object detection; SNNs on neuromorphic hardware offer potential energy savings over conventional ANNs.
AI.method: Develop general SNN detection architectures; adapt them to Intel Loihi 2 with engineering adjustments; implement ANN-to-SNN distillation-aware training; benchmark on frame-based and event-based datasets; compare against ANN baselines on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson Nano B01, and Apple M2; assess energy, latency, and detection accuracy.
AI.result: Loihi 2 achieves real-time detection with the lowest per-inference dynamic energy among all platforms; Loihi 2 offers superior power efficiency; ANN-based detection on Jetson Orin Nano achieves higher inference rates; ANN-to-SNN distillation recovers 87-100% of ANN accuracy while maintaining low latency; without distillation, SNNs incur 11-27% accuracy drop.
AI.conclusion: Neuromorphic systems (Loihi 2) enable energy-efficient, real-time edge object detection; distillation-based training effectively closes the accuracy gap with ANNs; results highlight the viability of neuromorphic edge deployment and motivate further optimization of SNN architectures and hardware mappings.

title: A Model-based Visual Contact Localization and Force Sensing System for Compliant Robotic Grippers
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
summary: Grasp force estimation can help prevent robots from damaging delicate objects during manipulation and improve learning-based robotic control. Integrating force sensing into deformable grippers negotiates trade-offs in cost, complexity, mechanical robustness, and performance. With the growing integration of RGB-D wrist cameras into robotic systems for control purposes, camera-based techniques are a promising solution for indirect visual force estimation. Current approaches mostly utilize end-to-end deep learning, which can be brittle when generalizing to new scenarios, while existing model-based approaches are unsuited to grasping and modern grasper geometries. To address these challenges, we developed a model-based visual force sensing approach integrating an iterative contact localization with generalization to unseen objects. The system extracts structural key points from wrist camera RGB-D images of deforming fin-ray-shaped soft grippers, and uses these key points to define parameters of an inverse finite element analysis simulation in Simulation Open Framework Architecture. The iterative contact localization sub-system utilizes a deep learning-based online 3D reconstruction and pose estimation pipeline to dynamically update contact location, and is robust to visual occlusion and unseen objects. Our system demonstrated an average root mean square error of 0.23 N and normalized root mean square deviation of 2.11% during the load phase, and 0.48 N and 4.34% over the entire grasping process when interacting with different objects under various conditions, showcasing its potential for real-time model-based indirect force sensing of soft grippers.

AI.tldr: A model-based visual force sensing approach for deformable, fin-ray-shaped soft grippers using RGB-D wrist cameras. It integrates iterative contact localization with inverse finite element analysis (in SOFA) and a deep-learning online 3D reconstruction/pose estimation to update contact points, enabling real-time indirect force sensing with robust generalization to unseen objects. Achieves RMSE ~0.23 N (load phase) and ~0.48 N (full grasp) with NRMSD ~2.11% and ~4.34%.
AI.motivation: To enable safe, accurate grasping by estimating interaction forces without brittle end-to-end learning or poorly-suited model-based methods for deformable grippers. Leverages vision to infer forces through physics-based simulation, improving robustness to object variability and occlusion.
AI.method: Extract structural key points from RGB-D images of fin-ray-shaped soft grippers; use these to parameterize an inverse finite element analysis in the SOFA framework. An iterative contact localization subsystem employs a deep-learning-based online 3D reconstruction and pose estimation pipeline to update contact locations dynamically, handling occlusion and unseen objects.
AI.result: Quantitative: RMSE of 0.23 N (load phase) and 0.48 N (entire grasp) with NRMSD of 2.11% and 4.34%, across varied objects and conditions. Demonstrates accuracy and potential for real-time model-based indirect force sensing.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of real-time, model-based indirect force sensing for soft grippers by combining vision-based key-point extraction, iterative contact localization, and inverse FEA in SOFA, with robust generalization to unseen objects.

title: Smart Ensemble Learning Framework for Predicting Groundwater Heavy Metal Pollution
comment: 53 pages, 16 figures, accepted for publication in Earth Systems and Environment (2026)
summary: Groundwater in the Densu Basin is increasingly threatened by heavy metal contamination, but conventional methods fail to capture the statistical complexity and spatial heterogeneity of pollution indicators. A key challenge is modelling the Heavy Metal Pollution Index (HPI), which is typically skewed and affected by correlated contaminants, leading to biased predictions without transformation. This study develops a predictive framework integrating response transformations with nested cross-validated ensemble machine learning. Three transformations (raw, log, and Gaussian copula) were applied to HPI and evaluated across six learners: support vector regression (SVM), $k$-nearest neighbours (k-NN), CART, Elastic Net, kernel ridge regression, and a stacked Lasso ensemble. Raw-scale models produced deceptively high fits (Elastic Net and stacked ensemble $R^2 \approx 1.0$), suggesting over-optimism. The log transformation stabilised variance (SVM: $R^2 = 0.93$, RMSE $= 0.18$; k-NN: $R^2 = 0.92$, RMSE $= 0.20$). The Gaussian copula gave the most reliable results: stacked ensemble $R^2 = 0.96$ (RMSE $= 0.19$), with other learners maintaining high accuracy. Copula-based models improved residuals and produced spatially plausible maps. DBSCAN clustering revealed Fe and Mn as primary HPI contributors, consistent with regional hydrogeochemistry. Limitations include reliance on random (not spatial) cross-validation and basin-specific scope. Future work should explore spatial validation and other geological settings. Overall, distribution-aware ensembles with clustering diagnostics offer robust, interpretable assessments of groundwater contamination.

AI.tldr: A framework combining distribution-aware response transformations with nested cross-validated ensemble ML improves HPI prediction in groundwater; Gaussian copula transformation with stacked ensemble provides most reliable results and spatial maps, with Fe and Mn as key contributors; limitations include non-spatial CV and basin-specific scope.
AI.motivation: Address skewness, correlation among heavy metals, and spatial heterogeneity in HPI prediction; overcome overfitting from raw-scale models; leverage spatially informed transformations and clustering to enhance reliability and interpretability.
AI.method: Apply three response transformations (raw, log, Gaussian copula) to HPI. Train six learners (SVM, k-NN, CART, Elastic Net, kernel ridge, stacked Lasso ensemble) under nested CV. Evaluate with R^2 and RMSE. Use DBSCAN to identify primary contributors and assess spatial plausibility; compare residuals and maps across transformations.
AI.result: Raw-scale models show over-optimistic fit (R^2 ~ 1.0). Log transformation yields robust performance (SVM R^2 0.93, RMSE 0.18; k-NN R^2 0.92, RMSE 0.20). Gaussian copula with stacked ensemble yields best performance (R^2 0.96, RMSE 0.19) and superior residuals; other learners also accurate. Copula-based models produce spatially plausible contamination maps. Fe and Mn identified as primary HPI drivers via DBSCAN. Limitations: use of random cross-validation; basin-limited scope. Future: spatial validation and application to other basins.
AI.conclusion: Distribution-aware ensembles combined with clustering diagnostics provide robust, interpretable groundwater contamination assessments and mitigate overfitting; the Gaussian copula transformation is particularly effective for skewed, correlated HPI data.

title: ARMOR 2025: A Military-Aligned Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Model Safety Beyond Civilian Contexts
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are now being explored for defense applications that require reliable and legally compliant decision support. They also hold significant potential to enhance decision making, coordination, and operational efficiency in military contexts. These uses demand evaluation methods that reflect the doctrinal standards that guide real military operations. Existing safety benchmarks focus on general social risks and do not test whether models follow the legal and ethical rules that govern real military operations. To address this gap, we introduce ARMOR 2025, a military aligned safety benchmark grounded in three core military doctrines the Law of War, the Rules of Engagement, and the Joint Ethics Regulation. We extract doctrinal text from these sources and generate multiple choice questions that preserve the intended meaning of each rule. The benchmark is organized through a taxonomy informed by the Observe Orient Decide Act (OODA) decision making framework. This structure enables systematic testing of accuracy and refusal across military relevant decision types. This benchmark features a structured 12-category taxonomy, 519 doctrinally grounded prompts, and rigorous evaluation procedures applied to 21 commercial LLMs. Evaluation results reveal critical gaps in safety alignment for military applications.

AI.tldr: ARMOR 2025 introduces a military-aligned safety benchmark for LLMs based on Law of War, Rules of Engagement, and Joint Ethics Regulation; 12-category taxonomy, 519 prompts, evaluated on 21 commercial LLMs; reveals safety gaps.
AI.motivation: There is a need for evaluation methods that reflect doctrinal standards guiding real military operations; existing safety benchmarks target general social risks and do not test adherence to legal/ethical military rules.
AI.method: Extract doctrinal text from Law of War, Rules of Engagement, and Joint Ethics Regulation; generate multiple-choice questions preserving rule meaning; organize through an OODA-informed 12-category taxonomy; assemble 519 prompts and implement rigorous evaluation across 21 commercial LLMs focusing on accuracy and refusal.
AI.result: Evaluation shows critical gaps in safety alignment for military applications across tested models.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables systematic testing against military doctrine, highlighting gaps and guiding future alignment efforts; potential limitations include paraphrase fidelity of doctrinal content, MCQ evaluation constraints, and generalizability across settings.

title: From Images2Mesh: A 3D Surface Reconstruction Pipeline for Non-Cooperative Space Objects
summary: On-orbit inspection imagery is crucial as it enables characterization of non-cooperative resident space objects, providing the geometry and structural condition essential for active debris removal and on-orbit servicing mission planning. However, most existing neural implicit surface reconstruction methods have been confined to synthetic or hardware-in-the-loop data with known camera poses and controlled illumination. In this work, we present a pipeline for neural implicit surface reconstruction of non-cooperative space objects from monocular inspection imagery. We demonstrate it on publicly released ISS inspection footage from the STS-119 mission and publicly released on-orbit inspection footage of an H-IIA rocket upper stage. We find that segmentation-based background removal is essential for successful camera pose estimation from real on-orbit footage, where background variation between frames caused direct processing to fail entirely. We further incorporate photometric correction of per-frame exposure variations and analyze its behavior across datasets, finding that performance in shadowed regions varies with the illumination characteristics of the input footage.
