title: SIMON: Saliency-aware Integrative Multi-view Object-centric Neural Decoding
summary: Recent EEG-to-image retrieval methods leverage pretrained vision encoders and foveation-inspired priors, but typically assume a fixed, center-focused view. This center bias conflicts with content-driven human attention, creating a geometric-semantic dissociation between visual features and EEG responses. We propose SIMON, a saliency-aware multi-view framework for zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval. SIMON combines foreground segmentation and saliency prediction to select fixation centers via Saliency-Aware Sampling (SAS), then generates foveated views that emphasize informative object regions while suppressing background clutter. On THINGS-EEG, SIMON achieves state-of-the-art performance in both intra-subject and inter-subject settings, reaching an average Top-1 accuracy of 69.7% and 19.6%, respectively, consistently outperforming recent competitive baselines. Analyses across sampling granularity, EEG channel topology, and visual/brain encoder backbones further support the robustness of saliency-aware multi-view integration. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/simonlink666/SIMON.

AI.tldr: SIMON: a saliency-aware multi-view framework for zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval that uses saliency-guided foveation to align EEG with informative image regions, achieving state-of-the-art results on THINGS-EEG.
AI.motivation: Addresses the center-focused bias and geometry-semantic dissociation between EEG responses and visual features by incorporating content-driven saliency and multi-view representations to better map EEG signals to relevant image regions.
AI.method: Integrates foreground segmentation and saliency prediction to perform Saliency-Aware Sampling (SAS) that selects fixation centers. Generates foveated views emphasizing informative object regions while suppressing background, and fuses multiple views with a vision/brain encoder backbone to perform zero-shot retrieval.
AI.result: On THINGS-EEG, SIMON achieves state-of-the-art intra-subject Top-1 accuracy of 69.7% and inter-subject 19.6%, outperforming recent baselines. Robustness shown across sampling granularity, EEG channel topology, and encoder backbones.
AI.conclusion: Saliency-aware multi-view fusion effectively aligns EEG responses with content-relevant image regions, reducing geometric-semantic dissociation and enabling robust zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval. Code and models are publicly available.

title: Diversity in Large Language Models under Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is essential for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with user intent, yet it is believed to suppress generative diversity. Although this reduction is frequently referenced, formal empirical testing of the phenomenon remains limited. The expressiveness of LLMs by itself was addressed by multiple prior methods. Their varying perspectives suggest that deeper analysis could yield further improvements. In this study, we attribute the decline to two primary drivers: the neglect of low-frequency patterns within fine-tuning datasets and the forgetting of preexisting knowledge. Motivated by our theoretical analysis, we develop Tempered Focal (TOFU) loss, a novel objective that addresses both stated challenges simultaneously. Our extensive evaluation confirms at scale that generation breadth narrows after SFT and strengthens the hypothesis explaining this effect. Across multiple models and benchmarks, we demonstrate that TOFU enhances output diversity while preserving high response quality, offering a principled approach to SFT.

AI.tldr: TOFU loss improves output diversity after supervised fine-tuning by addressing long-tail data neglect and knowledge forgetting, achieving broader generation without sacrificing quality.
AI.motivation: SFT is known to narrow generation breadth; there is limited formal empirical testing of this phenomenon and a need to understand the underlying causes and potential remedies.
AI.method: The authors provide theoretical analysis identifying two drivers of reduced diversity (neglect of low-frequency patterns and forgetting preexisting knowledge) and introduce Tempered Focal (TOFU) loss to address both; they validate the approach with extensive large-scale experiments across models and benchmarks.
AI.result: TOFU increases output diversity while preserving or improving response quality across models and datasets, supporting the proposed explanation for diversity loss and demonstrating the method's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: TOFU offers a principled objective to mitigate diversity loss in SFT, enabling aligned yet more diverse LLM outputs.

title: BOLT: Online Lightweight Adaptation for Preparation-Free Heterogeneous Cooperative Perception
summary: Most existing heterogeneous cooperative perception methods depend on prior preparation like offline joint training or tailored collaborator-model adaptation. Such preprocessing is, however, generally impractical in real scenarios, as agents are usually independently trained by different developers and meet occasionally online. This work investigates \emph{preparation-free heterogeneous cooperative perception}, where agents use independently trained single-agent detectors without any pre-deployment coordination. We find direct cross-agent fusion under this setting greatly underperforms ego-only perception. We present BOLT, a lightweight plug-and-play module that adapts neighboring features online via ego-as-teacher distillation, requiring only ego predictions without ground-truth labels. BOLT leverages high-confidence ego perception features to guide cross-agent feature-domain alignment, while enabling neighbors to contribute features in the ego's low-confidence regions. With only 0.9M trainable parameters, BOLT improves AP@50 by up to 32.3 points over vanilla unadapted fusion in the preparation-free setting. It consistently outperforms ego-only results on DAIR-V2X and OPV2V, across different encoder pairs and fusion strategies. Code: https://github.com/sidiangongyuan/BOLT.

AI.tldr: BOLT enables preparation-free cross-agent fusion for heterogeneous cooperative perception by ego-teacher distillation, achieving large AP@50 gains with minimal parameters.
AI.motivation: Many existing methods require offline joint training or bespoke collaborator-model adaptation, which is impractical when agents are independently trained and only meet online. A plug-in solution is needed to enable cross-agent fusion without pre-deployment coordination or ground-truth data.
AI.method: Introduce BOLT, a lightweight plug-and-play module that uses ego predictions as a teacher to align cross-agent features online (ego-as-teacher distillation). High-confidence ego features guide feature-domain alignment; neighbors’ features are allowed to contribute in ego’s low-confidence regions. Only ego predictions are used for supervision; 0.9M trainable parameters.
AI.result: BOLT improves AP@50 by up to 32.3 points over vanilla unadapted fusion in the preparation-free setting. It consistently outperforms ego-only baselines on DAIR-V2X and OPV2V across different encoder pairs and fusion strategies, with a small parameter footprint (0.9M). Code is provided.
AI.conclusion: Preparation-free heterogeneous cooperative perception is feasible; BOLT provides an effective, low-cost plug-in to boost cross-agent fusion without pre-deployment coordination or ground-truth labels, evidenced by substantial AP gains across benchmarks.

title: Beyond Heuristics: Learnable Density Control for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary:
While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated impressive real-time rendering performance, its efficacy remains constrained by a reliance on heuristic density control. Despite numerous refinements to these handcrafted rules, such methods inherently lack the flexibility to adapt to diverse scenes with complex geometries.
  In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift for density control from rigid heuristics to fully learnable policies. Specifically, we introduce \textbf{LeGS}, a framework that reformulates density control as a parameterized policy network optimized via Reinforcement Learning (RL). Central to our approach is the tailored effective reward function grounded in sensitivity analysis, which precisely quantifies the marginal contribution of individual Gaussians to reconstruction quality. To maintain computational tractability, we derive a closed-form solution that reduces the complexity of reward calculation from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$. Extensive experiments on the Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks \& Temples, and Deep Blending datasets demonstrate that \textbf{LeGS} significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, striking a superior balance between reconstruction quality and efficiency. The code will be released at https://github.com/AaronNZH/LeGS

AI.tldr: LeGS reframes density control in 3D Gaussian Splatting as a learnable policy optimized by reinforcement learning, using a sensitivity-analysis–based reward and a closed-form O(N) reward computation to outperform state-of-the-art methods on several benchmarks while balancing quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Rigid handcrafted density heuristics in 3D Gaussian Splatting limit adaptability to diverse scenes with complex geometries. A learnable, data-driven policy can adapt density control to varying scenes, potentially improving reconstruction quality and efficiency.
AI.method: A parameterized policy network outputs density-control actions; reinforcement learning optimizes the policy with a reward function grounded in sensitivity analysis that measures each Gaussian’s marginal contribution to reconstruction quality; the reward computation is made tractable by a closed-form solution reducing complexity from O(N^2) to O(N).
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks & Temples, and Deep Blending datasets show that LeGS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving a better balance between reconstruction quality and rendering efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Learning-based density control via reinforcement learning can surpass handcrafted heuristics in 3D Gaussian Splatting. The approach scales well thanks to the O(N) reward computation, and the authors provide code for reproducibility.

title: CompleteRXN: Toward Completing Open Chemical Reaction Databases
summary: Chemical reaction datasets such as USPTO suffer from substantial incompleteness, frequently missing byproducts, co-reactants, and stoichiometric coefficients. This limits their applicability and reliability in downstream applications. Here, we introduce CompleteRXN, a large-scale supervised benchmark for reaction completion under realistic missing-data conditions. We construct a dataset of aligned incomplete and atom-balanced reactions by mapping USPTO records to curated mechanistic reactions. We evaluate representative baselines, including a novel encoder-decoder reaction completion model with constrained decoding, the Constrained Reaction Balancer (CRB), and a recent algorithmic method, SynRBL. On our CompleteRXN benchmark, the CRB achieves high performance across splits of increasing difficulty, reaching 99.20% equivalence accuracy on the random split and 91.12% on the extreme out-of-distribution split. SynRBL produces many balanced and chemically plausible completions, but with lower accuracy on the benchmark test splits. Across all methods, performance degrades with increasing incompleteness. We observe a substantial drop when evaluating on reactions outside the benchmark (full uncurated USPTO), highlighting the gap between benchmark performance and practical robustness and motivating future work.

AI.tldr: CompleteRXN provides a large-scale benchmark for reaction completion under realistic missing-data conditions, showing Constrained Reaction Balancer (CRB) achieves top equivalence accuracy, while SynRBL yields plausible but less accurate completions; overall performance degrades with more incompleteness and gaps exist between benchmark and uncurated USPTO data.
AI.motivation: Address substantial incompleteness in chemical reaction datasets (missing byproducts, co-reactants, stoichiometric coefficients) and enable robust evaluation of completion methods under realistic scenarios.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of aligned incomplete and atom-balanced reactions by mapping USPTO records to curated mechanistic reactions. Evaluate representative baselines, including an encoder-decoder completion model with constrained decoding, CRB, and SynRBL on CompleteRXN.
AI.result: CRB achieves high performance across increasing difficulty, up to 99.20% equivalence accuracy on the random split and 91.12% on the extreme out-of-distribution split. SynRBL produces many balanced and chemically plausible completions but with lower test-split accuracy. All methods degrade as incompleteness increases. A substantial drop occurs when evaluating on full uncurated USPTO, highlighting limited practical robustness and a need for future work.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark exposes a gap between benchmark-level performance and real-world robustness, motivating the development of more resilient reaction completion methods and broader evaluation on uncurated data.

title: LIMSSR: LLM-Driven Sequence-to-Score Reasoning under Training-Time Incomplete Multimodal Observations
summary: Real-world multimodal learning is often hindered by missing modalities. While Incomplete Multimodal Learning (IML) has gained traction, existing methods typically rely on the unrealistic assumption of full-modal availability during training to provide reconstruction supervision or cross-modal priors. This paper tackles the more challenging setting of IML under training-time incomplete observations, which precludes reliance on a ``God's eye view'' of complete data. We propose LIMSSR (LLM-Driven Incomplete Multimodal Sequence-to-Score Reasoning), a framework that reformulates this challenge as a conditional sequence reasoning task. LIMSSR leverages the semantic reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models via Prompt-Guided Context-Aware Modality Imputation and Multidimensional Representation Fusion to infer latent semantics from available contexts without direct reconstruction. To mitigate hallucinations, we introduce a Mask-Aware Dual-Path Aggregation to dynamically calibrate inference uncertainty. Extensive experiments on three Action Quality Assessment datasets demonstrate that LIMSSR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines without relying on complete training data, establishing a new paradigm for data-efficient multimodal learning. Code is available at https://github.com/XuHuangbiao/LIMSSR.

AI.tldr: LIMSSR introduces a data-efficient incomplete multimodal learning framework that uses LLM-driven conditional sequence reasoning to infer latent semantics from partial modalities, with prompt-guided imputation and mask-aware dual-path fusion to reduce hallucinations, achieving state-of-the-art results on Action Quality Assessment without full training data.
AI.motivation: Real-world multimodal data often arrive with missing modalities. Existing IML approaches assume access to complete data during training for supervision or priors, which is unrealistic. There is a need for methods that perform well under training-time incompleteness and without reconstruction-based supervision.
AI.method: Recast the problem as a conditional sequence reasoning task. Leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) through Prompt-Guided Context-Aware Modality Imputation and Multidimensional Representation Fusion to infer latent semantics from available contexts without explicit reconstruction. Introduce Mask-Aware Dual-Path Aggregation to calibrate inference uncertainty and mitigate hallucinations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three Action Quality Assessment datasets show LIMSSR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines without access to complete training data, validating data-efficient multimodal learning under training-time incompleteness. Code is released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: LIMSSR establishes a new paradigm for data-efficient multimodal learning under training-time incomplete observations by utilizing LLM-driven reasoning and uncertainty-aware fusion, reducing reliance on complete data and avoiding explicit reconstruction.

title: Bayesian Optimization in Linear Time
comment: 25 pages, 25 figures; code available at https://github.com/jsa378/bo_partition
summary: Bayesian optimization is a sequential method for minimizing objective functions that are expensive to evaluate and about which few assumptions can be made. By using all gathered data to train a Gaussian process model for the function and adaptively employing a mixture of global exploration and local exploitation, this method has been used for optimization in many fields including machine learning, automotive engineering and reinforcement learning. However, the standard method suffers from two problems: 1) with cubic computational complexity in the training-set size it eventually becomes computationally infeasible to train the model, and 2) globally modeling the objective function is not necessarily optimal given the local nature of minimization. Using flexible and recursive binary partitioning of the search space, we adapt both the modeling and acquisitive aspects of standard Bayesian optimization to work harmoniously with the partitioning scheme, thereby ameliorating both standard shortcomings. We compare our method against a commonly used Bayesian optimization library on seven challenging test functions, ranging in dimensionality from $6$ to $124$, and show that our method achieves superior optimization performance in all tests. In addition our method has linear computational complexity.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian optimization method that uses recursive binary space partitioning to adapt both the surrogate modeling and the acquisition function, achieving superior optimization performance with linear time complexity across 6–124-dimensional problems.
AI.motivation: Standard BO with Gaussian processes incurs cubic training complexity and global modeling may be suboptimal for locally driven minimization; a scalable approach that respects local structure is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce flexible recursive binary partitioning of the search space to guide both the GP-based modeling and the acquisition strategy, enabling harmonious integration and linear-time computation.
AI.result: Against a common BO library, on seven challenging test functions (dimensions 6–124), the proposed method achieved superior optimization performance in all cases.
AI.conclusion: Partition-aware BO yields both improved optimization performance and linear computational complexity, addressing key drawbacks of standard BO.

title: Scaling Video Understanding via Compact Latent Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) advance vision language understanding but face inherent limitations in long-video tasks due to bounded perception context budgets. Existing agentic methods mitigate this via rule-based preprocessing, yet often suffer from information loss, high cost, and reliance on textual intermediates. We propose MACF, an end-to-end Multi-Agent Collaboration Framework that decouples per-agent perception budgets from global video complexity, enabling scalable video understanding while preserving visual fidelity. MACF partitions videos into segments for locally budgeted agents and enables holistic reasoning via an agent-native latent communication protocol. Each agent encodes partial observations into compact, task-sufficient tokens in a shared embedding space, allowing efficient and information-preserving collaboration by a central coordinator. We introduce a curriculum training strategy that progressively enforces semantic alignment, evidence summarization, and cross-agent coordination. Extensive experiments on diverse video understanding benchmarks show that MACF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and multi-agent systems under identical budget constraints, demonstrating the effectiveness of our latent collaboration for scalable video understanding.

AI.tldr: MACF presents an end-to-end, multi-agent latent collaboration framework for long-video understanding in MLLMs, decoupling per-agent perception budgets from global video complexity using tokenized latent observations and a central coordinator, trained with curriculum to align semantics and coordination, achieving state-of-the-art performance under budget constraints.
AI.motivation: Address inherent limits of perception context budgets in long-video MLLMs and the shortcomings of rule-based preprocessing (information loss, high cost, reliance on textual intermediates).
AI.method: Partition videos into segments for locally budgeted agents that encode partial observations into compact tokens in a shared embedding space. A central coordinator performs holistic reasoning via an agent-native latent communication protocol. Agents are trained end-to-end with a curriculum that enforces semantic alignment, evidence summarization, and cross-agent coordination.
AI.result: MACF outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and multi-agent systems under identical budget constraints across diverse video understanding benchmarks, demonstrating effective latent collaboration for scalable video understanding.
AI.conclusion: Latent collaboration with decoupled per-agent budgets and a central coordinator provides scalable, information-preserving long-video understanding for MLLMs, reducing reliance on costly preprocessing while maintaining fidelity.

title: NLPOpt-Net: A Learning Method for Nonlinear Optimization with Feasibility Guarantees
summary: Nonlinear Parametric Optimization Network (NLPOpt-Net) is an unsupervised learning architecture to solve constrained nonlinear programs (NLP). Given the structure of an NLP, it learns the parametric solution maps with guaranteed constraint satisfaction. The architecture consists of a backbone neural network (NN) followed by a multilayer ($k$-layered) projection. While the NN drives toward optimality through a loss function consisting of a modified Lagrangian augmented with a consistency loss, the projection ensures feasibility by projecting the NN predictions in the original constraint manifold. Instead of typical distance minimization, our projection exploits local quadratic approximations of the original NLP. Under certain conditions (such as convexity), the projection has a descent property, which improves the NN predictions further. NLPOpt-Net deploys an inversion-free, modified Chambolle-Pock algorithm to solve the constrained quadratic projections during the forward pass and uses the implicit function theorem for efficient backpropagation. The fixed structure of the projection further allows decoupling of the NN and the projection once the training is complete. NLPOpt-Net solves large-scale convex QP, QCQP, NLP, and nonconvex problems with near zero optimality gap and constraint violations reduced to machine precision. Additionally, it provides near accurate prediction of the active sets and corresponding dual variables, thereby enabling a scalable approach for multiparametric programming. Compiling the projection in C provides order of magnitude improvement in inference time compared to JAX. We provide the codes and NLPOpt-Net as a ready to use package that includes GPU support.

AI.tldr: NLPOpt-Net is an unsupervised architecture for solving constrained NLPs by combining a backbone NN with a constraint-projection module that enforces feasibility via local quadratic approximations; trained with a modified Lagrangian loss and consistency term, enabling near-optimality and precise constraint satisfaction, fast inference through a C-compiled projection and Chambolle-Pock iterations.
AI.motivation: To learn parametric solution mappings for constrained NLPs with guarantees of constraint satisfaction and scalability to large-scale problems, while enabling fast inference and accurate dual/active-set estimates.
AI.method: Backbone NN plus multi-layer projection; projection uses local quadratic models of the NLP; forward pass solves projection via an inversion-free, modified Chambolle-Pock; training with modified Lagrangian plus consistency loss; backprop via implicit function theorem; decoupling after training; implementation in C for speed; GPU-ready.
AI.result: Achieves near-zero optimality gap and machine-precision constraint violations on large-scale convex QP, QCQP, NLP, and certain nonconvex problems; provides near-accurate active-set and dual variables; significant inference speedups when projection compiled in C; ready-to-use package with GPU support.
AI.conclusion: NLPOpt-Net offers a scalable, efficient, and reliable framework for parametric optimization in constrained NLPs, combining learning with principled projection to guarantee feasibility and high-quality solutions; suitable for multiparametric programming and large-scale deployments.

title: Learning from Compressed CT: Feature Attention Style Transfer and Structured Factorized Projections for Resource-Efficient Medical Image Analysis
summary: The deployment of artificial intelligence in medical imaging is hindered by high computational complexity and resource-intensive processing of volumetric data. Although chest computed tomography (CT) volumes offer richer diagnostic information than projection radiography, their use in AI-based diagnosis remains limited due to the computational burden of processing uncompressed volumetric images (typically stored in NIfTI or DICOM format). Addressing the growing need for low-resource deployment and efficient electronic data transfer, we investigate the utilization of JPEG-compressed chest CT volumes for thoracic abnormality detection. We propose Feature Attention Style Transfer (FAST), a novel distillation framework that transfers both activation patterns and structural relationships from high-fidelity CT representations to a spatiotemporal visual encoder operating on compressed inputs. By combining Gram-matrix-based attention style preservation with dual-attention feature alignment, FAST enables robust feature extraction from degraded volumes. Furthermore, we introduce Structured Factorized Projection (SFP), leveraging Block Tensor Train decomposition as a parameter-efficient alternative to dense projection layers, reducing projection-head parameters by almost half. Our contrastive learning pipeline, CT-Lite, integrates these components with a SigLIP-based multimodal alignment objective. Experiments on CT-RATE, NIDCH, and Rad-ChestCT demonstrate that CT-Lite achieves AUROC within 5-7\% of the uncompressed-input baseline across all three datasets, despite operating on compressed inputs with significantly fewer parameters, paving the way for AI-based clinical evaluation under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: Proposes CT-Lite for compressed chest CT volumes using FAST distillation and SFP with Block Tensor Train to achieve near-uncompressed AUROC with fewer parameters on JPEG-compressed inputs.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost and data transfer burden of volumetric CT AI by leveraging JPEG-compressed inputs while maintaining diagnostic performance.
AI.method: FAST distillation transfers activation patterns and structural relations from high-fidelity CT representations to a spatiotemporal encoder operating on compressed inputs, using Gram-matrix activation-style preservation and dual-attention feature alignment. Structured Factorized Projection (SFP) employs Block Tensor Train decomposition to replace dense projection layers, lowering projection-head parameters. CT-Lite uses a SigLIP-based multimodal alignment objective in a contrastive learning framework.
AI.result: CT-Lite achieves AUROC within 5–7% of the uncompressed-input baseline across CT-RATE, NIDCH, and Rad-ChestCT, while operating on compressed inputs and with substantially fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that near-baseline diagnostic performance is achievable with compressed CT data via specialized distillation, attention-preserving representations, and parameter-efficient projections, enabling resource-constrained clinical evaluation.

title: Pessimism-Free Offline Learning in General-Sum Games via KL Regularization
summary: Offline multi-agent reinforcement learning in general-sum settings is challenged by the distribution shift between logged datasets and target equilibrium policies. While standard methods rely on manual pessimistic penalties, we demonstrate that KL regularization suffices to stabilize learning and achieve equilibrium recovery. We propose General-sum Anchored Nash Equilibrium (GANE), which recovers regularized Nash equilibria at an accelerated statistical rate of $\widetilde{O}(1/n)$. For computational tractability, we develop General-sum Anchored Mirror Descent (GAMD), an iterative algorithm converging to a Coarse Correlated Equilibrium at the standard rate of $\widetilde{O}(1/\sqrt{n}+1/T)$. These results establish KL regularization as a standalone mechanism for pessimism-free offline learning that achieves equivalent or accelerated rates in multi-player general-sum games.

AI.tldr: KL regularization alone suffices for offline multi-agent learning in general-sum games, enabling stable learning and equilibrium recovery via two algorithms: GANE for accelerated recovery of regularized Nash equilibria (~O~(1/n)) and GAMD for convergence to Coarse Correlated Equilibria at (~O~(1/√n + 1/T)).
AI.motivation: Offline MARL in general-sum settings faces distribution shift between logged data and target equilibria; traditional pessimistic penalties are manual and potentially brittle. The work argues that KL regularization can serve as a standalone pessimism mechanism, enabling stable learning without ad hoc penalties and enabling theoretical convergence rates.
AI.method: Introduce GANE (General-sum Anchored Nash Equilibrium) to recover regularized Nash equilibria with accelerated statistical rate ~O~(1/n). Develop GAMD (General-sum Anchored Mirror Descent), an iterative algorithm that converges to a Coarse Correlated Equilibrium at the standard rate ~O~(1/√n + 1/T). Both leverage KL regularization as the core mechanism, replacing explicit pessimism penalties.
AI.result: Theoretically establishes that KL regularization alone can provide pessimism-like stability in offline multi-agent general-sum learning, achieving competitive or faster convergence rates compared to explicit pessimism methods. GANE attains accelerated convergence to regularized Nash equilibria; GAMD ensures convergence to CCE with standard sample- and iteration-based rates.
AI.conclusion: KL regularization serves as a standalone pessimism mechanism for offline learning in multi-player general-sum games, enabling stable equilibrium recovery with provable rates. The proposed algorithms offer practical pathways to implement pessimism-free offline MARL in general-sum settings.

title: From Local to Global to Mechanistic: An iERF-Centered Unified Framework for Interpreting Vision Models
comment: Accepted to IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI), 2026
summary: Modern vision models achieve remarkable accuracy, but explaining where evidence arises, what the model encodes, and how internal computations assemble that evidence remains fragmented. We introduce an iERF-centric framework that unifies local, global, and mechanistic interpretability around a single analysis unit: the pointwise feature vector (PFV) paired with its instance-specific Effective Receptive Field (iERF). On the local side, Sharing Ratio Decomposition (SRD) expresses each PFV as a mixture of upstream PFVs via sharing ratios and propagates iERFs to construct class-discriminative saliency maps. SRD yields high-resolution, activation-faithful explanations, is robust to targeted manipulation and noise, and remains activation-agnostic across common nonlinearities. For the global view, we introduce Concept-Anchored Feature Explanation (CAFE), which utilizes the iERF as a semantic label, grounding abstract latent vectors in verifiable pixel-level evidence. With CAFE, we address the challenge of non-localized sparse autoencoder latents--especially in Transformers, where early self-attention mixes distant context. To answer how representations are composed through depth, we propose the Interlayer Concept Graph with Interlayer Concept Attribution (ICAT), which quantifies concept-to-concept influence while isolating layer pairs; an interlayer insertion, deletion protocol identifies Integrated Gradients as the most faithful instantiation. Empirically, across ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs, our framework outperforms baselines in both fidelity and robustness, successfully interprets dispersed SAE features, and exposes dominant concept routes in correct, misclassified, and adversarial cases. Grounded in iERFs, our approach provides a coherent, evidence-backed map from pixels to concepts to decisions.

AI.tldr: An iERF-centric framework unifies local, global, and mechanistic interpretability around pointwise feature vectors (PFV) and their effective receptive fields (iERFs). It introduces SRD (local), CAFE (global), and ICAT (interlayer mechanistic) to provide high-fidelity, robust explanations across CNNs and ViTs, with validation on ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs.
AI.motivation: Interpretability of vision models remains fragmented across local (saliency), global (latent semantics), and mechanistic (how concepts propagate across layers). The paper proposes a unified unit (PFV + iERF) to bridge these scales and makes explanations activation-agnostic and robust, addressing non-local latent structures in Transformers and sparse Autoencoders.
AI.method: - Local: Sharing Ratio Decomposition (SRD) expresses each PFV as a mixture of upstream PFVs via sharing ratios, propagating iERFs to generate class-discriminative, high-resolution saliency maps that are robust to noise and targeted manipulation and activation-agnostic across common nonlinearities. - Global: Concept-Anchored Feature Explanation (CAFE) uses iERF as a semantic label to ground latent vectors in verifiable pixel-level evidence, addressing non-localized sparse autoencoder latents, especially in Transformers. - Interlayer (Mechanistic): Interlayer Concept Graph with Interlayer Concept Attribution (ICAT) quantifies concept-to-concept influence across layer pairs; an interlayer insertion/deletion protocol identifies Integrated Gradients as the most faithful instantiation. - Evaluation: Across ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs, compare to baselines on fidelity and robustness; interpret dispersed SAE features; reveal dominant concept routes in correct, misclassified, and adversarial cases.
AI.result: The framework achieves higher fidelity and robustness than baselines, provides activation-agnostic explanations, successfully interprets dispersed SAE features, and exposes dominant concept routes across model families (CNNs and Transformers). It yields a coherent, evidence-backed map from pixels to concepts to decisions grounded in iERFs.
AI.conclusion: A unified, evidence-backed interpretability paradigm that connects pixels, concepts, and decisions via iERFs, enabling high-fidelity local explanations, verifiable global grounding of latent features, and mechanistic interlayer attribution across CNNs and Transformers.

title: Polaris: Coupled Orbital Polar Embeddings for Hierarchical Concept Learning
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Real-world knowledge is often organized as hierarchies such as product taxonomies, medical ontologies, and label trees, yet learning hierarchical representations is challenging due to asymmetric structure and noisy semantics. We introduce Polaris, a polar hyperspherical embedding framework that separates semanticity from hierarchy using angular geometry and radius, enabling the learning of meaning and structure without interference. To map latent representation onto the sphere, we project it to the tangent space at the north pole, apply the exponential map, and learn unit-norm representations using spherical linear layers. Polaris then combines robust local constraints, global regularization that prevents geometric collapse, and uncertainty-aware asymmetric objectives that encourage directional containment. At inference time, Polaris uses structure-guided retrieval to efficiently narrow down candidate parents before final ranking. We evaluate Polaris on different settings of taxonomy expansion - spanning trees, multi-parent DAGs, and multimodal hierarchies, showing consistent improvements of up to ~19 points in top-K retrieval and up to ~60% reduction in mean rank over fourteen strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Polaris is a polar hyperspherical embedding framework that decouples semantic meaning from hierarchical structure using angular geometry, tangent-space projection, and unit-norm spherical layers; combines local constraints, global regularization, and uncertainty-aware asymmetric objectives; and uses structure-guided retrieval for efficient inference, achieving substantial gains in taxonomy expansion tasks across various hierarchy types.
AI.motivation: Real-world knowledge is organized in hierarchies (e.g., taxonomies, ontologies) but learning such representations is challenging due to asymmetric structure and noisy semantics. There is a need to separate semantic meaning from hierarchical structure to improve retrieval and reasoning over hierarchies.
AI.method: Polaris projects latent representations to the tangent space at the north pole, applies the exponential map to obtain unit-norm sphere embeddings, and learns using spherical linear layers. It combines robust local constraints with global regularization to prevent geometric collapse and introduces uncertainty-aware asymmetric objectives that promote directional containment. Inference uses structure-guided retrieval to prune candidate parents before ranking.
AI.result: Experiments on taxonomy expansion tasks across spanning trees, multi-parent DAGs, and multimodal hierarchies show consistent improvements, achieving up to ~19 points gains in top-K retrieval and up to ~60% reductions in mean rank relative to fourteen strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semanticity from hierarchy via angular geometry and radius, along with tangent-space projection and uncertainty-aware objectives, yields robust, scalable hierarchical representations and accurate structure-guided retrieval for diverse hierarchical settings.

title: Leveraging Vision-Language Models as Weak Annotators in Active Learning
summary: Active learning aims to reduce annotation cost by selectively querying informative samples for supervision under a limited labeling budget. In this work, we investigate how vision-language models (VLMs) can be leveraged to further reduce the reliance on costly human annotation within the active learning paradigm. To this end, we find that the reliability of VLMs varies significantly with label granularity in fine-grained recognition tasks: they perform poorly on fine-grained labels but can provide accurate coarse-grained labels. Leveraging this property, we propose an active learning framework that combines fine-grained human annotations with coarse-grained VLM-generated weak labels through instance-wise label assignment. We further model the systematic noise in VLM-generated labels using a small set of trusted full labels. Experiments on CUB200 and FGVC-Aircraft show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing active learning methods under the same annotation budget.

AI.tldr: An active learning framework leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to reduce annotation cost by using coarse-grained VLM labels alongside costly fine-grained human labels, with noise modeling via a small trusted label set; it improves AL performance on fine-grained tasks under a fixed budget.
AI.motivation: To address annotation bottlenecks in fine-grained recognition by exploiting VLMs' stronger performance on coarse-grained labels, reducing reliance on expensive fine-grained human annotations.
AI.method: Use instance-wise label assignment that combines fine-grained human annotations with coarse-grained VLM-generated labels. Model the systematic noise in VLM labels with a small set of trusted full labels to calibrate and correct weak labels.
AI.result: The proposed framework consistently outperforms existing active learning methods on CUB200 and FGVC-Aircraft under the same annotation budget.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language models can effectively reduce annotation costs in active learning for fine-grained tasks by providing reliable coarse-grained supervision, with noise-aware calibration from a limited set of trusted labels.

title: Jailbroken Frontier Models Retain Their Capabilities
summary: As language model safeguards become more robust, attackers are pushed toward developing increasingly complex jailbreaks. Prior work has found that this complexity imposes a "jailbreak tax" that degrades the target model's task performance. We show that this tax scales inversely with model capability and that the most advanced jailbreaks effectively yield no reduction in model capabilities. Evaluating 28 jailbreaks on five benchmarks across Claude models ranging in capability from Haiku 4.5 to Opus 4.6, we find Haiku 4.5 loses an average of 33.1% on benchmark performance when jailbroken, while Opus 4.6 at max thinking effort loses only 7.7%. We also observe that across all models, reasoning-heavy tasks display considerably more degradation than knowledge-recall tasks. Finally, Boundary Point Jailbreaking, currently the strongest jailbreak against deployed classifiers, achieves near-perfect classifier evasion with near-zero degradation across safeguarded models. We recommend that safety cases for frontier models should not rely on a meaningful capability degradation from jailbreaks.

AI.tldr: Jailbreaks impose a 'jailbreak tax' that degrades model performance, but the tax declines with increasing model capability; the most advanced jailbreaks yield little to no degradation in capabilities.
AI.motivation: Quantify how jailbreaks affect model performance across capabilities, examine whether safety improvements reduce harm, and assess the strength of leading jailbreaks (e.g., Boundary Point).
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of 28 jailbreaks across five benchmarks on Claude models ranging from Haiku 4.5 to Opus 4.6; measure performance degradation, compare reasoning-heavy vs knowledge-recall tasks, and assess the Boundary Point Jailbreak.
AI.result: Jailbreak tax scales inversely with model capability; the strongest jailbreaks yield near-zero degradation in capable models. Haiku 4.5 shows an average benchmark performance loss of 33.1% when jailbroken, while Opus 4.6 (max thinking) shows 7.7% loss. Reasoning-heavy tasks degrade more than knowledge-recall tasks. Boundary Point Jailbreaking achieves near-perfect classifier evasion with near-zero degradation across safeguarded models.
AI.conclusion: Safety evaluations of frontier models should not rely on meaningful capability degradation from jailbreaks; safeguards must account for the fact that strong jailbreaks can be deployed with minimal impact on model performance.

title: High-Probability Convergence in Decentralized Stochastic Optimization with Gradient Tracking
comment: 49 pages, 4 figures. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2510.06141
summary: We study high-probability (HP) convergence guarantees in decentralized stochastic optimization, where multiple agents collaborate to jointly train a model over a network. Existing HP results in decentralized settings almost exclusively focus on the Decentralized Stochastic Gradient Descent ($\mathtt{DSGD}$) algorithm, which requires strong assumptions, such as bounded data heterogeneity, or strong convexity of each agent's cost. This is contrary to the mean-squared error (MSE) results, where methods incorporating bias-correction techniques are known to converge under relaxed assumptions and achieve better practical performance. In this paper we provide the first step toward bridging the gap, by studying HP convergence of $\mathtt{DSGD}$ incorporating the gradient tracking technique, in the presence of noise satisfying a relaxed sub-Gaussian condition. We show that the resulting method, dubbed $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$, achieves order-optimal HP convergence rates for both non-convex and Polyak-Łojasiewicz costs, of order $\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\log(1/δ)}{\sqrt{nT}}\Big)$ and $\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\log(1/δ)}{nT}\Big)$, respectively, where $n$ is the number of agents, $T$ is the time horizon and $δ\in (0,1)$ is the confidence parameter. Our results establish that $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$ converges in the HP sense under the same conditions on the cost as in the MSE sense, while achieving comparable transient times. To the best of our knowledge, these are the first HP guarantees for decentralized optimization methods incorporating bias-correction. Numerical experiments on real and synthetic data verify our theoretical findings, underlining the superior performance of $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$ and highlighting that the benefits of incorporating bias-correction are also maintained in the HP sense.

AI.tldr: GT-DSGD achieves high-probability convergence guarantees for decentralized optimization with gradient-tracking bias correction under relaxed sub-Gaussian noise, delivering rates matching MSE under the same assumptions: non-convex O(log(1/δ)/√(nT)); PL O(log(1/δ)/(nT)); first HP results for bias-corrected decentralized methods; empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between HP guarantees in decentralized methods (often requiring strong assumptions) and MSE results (where bias-corrections enable weaker conditions).
AI.method: Apply gradient-tracking DSGD (GT-DSGD) with stochastic gradients under relaxed sub-Gaussian noise; analyze HP convergence for non-convex and PL; derive rates O(log(1/δ)/√(nT)) and O(log(1/δ)/(nT)).
AI.result: HP convergence rates achieved; convergence under same cost assumptions as MSE; rates are order-optimal; experiments validate theory.
AI.conclusion: Confirms feasibility of HP guarantees for bias-corrected decentralized methods; shows benefits extend to HP sense; sets stage for further explorations.

title: GOR-IS: 3D Gaussian Object Removal in the Intrinsic Space
summary: Recent advances in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have made it standard practice to reconstruct 3D scenes from multi-view images. Removing objects from such 3D representations is a fundamental editing task that requires complete and seamless inpainting of occluded regions, ensuring consistency in geometry and appearance. Although existing methods have made notable progress in improving inpainting consistency, they often neglect global lighting effects, leading to physically implausible results. Moreover, these methods struggle with view-dependent non-Lambertian surfaces, where appearance varies across viewpoints, leading to unreliable inpainting. In this paper, we present 3D Gaussian Object Removal in the Intrinsic Space (GOR-IS), a novel framework for physically consistent and visually coherent 3D object removal. Our approach decomposes the scene into intrinsic components and explicitly models light transport to maintain global lighting effects consistency. Furthermore, we introduce an intrinsic-space inpainting module that operates directly in the material and lighting domains, effectively addressing the challenges posed by non-Lambertian surfaces. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our framework substantially improves the physical consistency and visual coherence of object removal, outperforming existing methods by 13% in perceptual similarity (LPIPS) and 2dB in peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR). Code is publicly available at https://applezyh.github.io/GOR-IS-project-page/

AI.tldr: Introduces GOR-IS, a physics-informed 3D object removal framework in intrinsic space for NeRF/3DGS scenes, achieving consistent lighting and handling non-Lambertian surfaces via intrinsic-space inpainting; shows substantial gains in LPIPS and PSNR over prior methods and provides public code.
AI.motivation: To enable physically consistent and visually coherent removal of objects from 3D scenes while preserving global lighting and handling view-dependent appearance, which prior inpainting approaches struggle with.
AI.method: Decomposes scenes into intrinsic components (material, lighting, geometry) and explicitly models light transport to preserve global lighting effects. Proposes an intrinsic-space inpainting module that operates in material and lighting domains to address non-Lambertian surfaces.
AI.result: Significant improvements in physical consistency and visual coherence for object removal. Quantitatively, 13% improvement in perceptual similarity (LPIPS) and 2 dB PSNR over baselines on synthetic and real-world datasets. Code released publicly.
AI.conclusion: GOR-IS provides a principled framework for 3D object removal that integrates intrinsic decomposition with light-transport-aware inpainting, addressing non-Lambertian uncertainties and maintaining global illumination, outperforming existing methods.

title: A Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager principle: Instantaneous residual minimization with gauge momentum for nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions
summary:
Dirac-Frenkel instantaneous residual minimization evolves nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions in time, but ill-conditioning can render the parameter dynamics non-unique.
  We interpret this non-uniqueness as a gauge freedom: nullspace directions that leave the
  time derivative unchanged can be used to select better-conditioned parameter velocities.
  Building on Onsager's minimum-dissipation principle, we introduce a history variable -- interpretable as momentum -- and inject it only along the nullspace directions.
  The resulting Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics preserve instantaneous residual minimization, in contrast to standard regularization that can introduce bias, while promoting temporally smooth parameter evolutions.
  Examples demonstrate that the approach leads to increased robustness in singular and near-singular regimes.

AI.tldr: A gauge-based augmentation via Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics uses a history/momentum variable injected along residual nullspaces to achieve stable, minimally dissipative, time-smooth evolution of nonlinear PDE parametrizations in ill-conditioned regimes.
AI.motivation: Instantaneous residual minimization in Dirac-Frenkel formulations is ill-conditioned and non-unique due to nullspace directions that do not change the time derivative, leading to unstable parameter velocities.
AI.method: Interpret non-uniqueness as a gauge freedom and introduce a history variable (momentum) that is injected only along nullspace directions. This yields Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics that preserve instantaneous residual minimization while promoting temporal smoothness and avoiding bias from standard regularization.
AI.result: The approach increases robustness in singular and near-singular regimes, maintaining residual minimization without the bias associated with conventional regularization.
AI.conclusion: The history-variable Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager framework provides a principled, bias-free way to obtain well-conditioned, smooth time evolution of PDE-parametrizations in challenging regimes, with potential broader applicability to reduced-order models and time-dependent variational methods.

title: End-to-End Autoregressive Image Generation with 1D Semantic Tokenizer
summary: Autoregressive image modeling relies on visual tokenizers to compress images into compact latent representations. We design an end-to-end training pipeline that jointly optimizes reconstruction and generation, enabling direct supervision from generation results to the tokenizer. This contrasts with prior two-stage approaches that train tokenizers and generative models separately. We further investigate leveraging vision foundation models to improve 1D tokenizers for autoregressive modeling. Our autoregressive generative model achieves strong empirical results, including a state-of-the-art FID score of 1.48 without guidance on ImageNet 256x256 generation.

AI.tldr: End-to-end optimization of visual tokenizers within an autoregressive image model, supervised by generation results, with foundation-model-enhanced 1D tokenizers, achieving state-of-the-art FID 1.48 on ImageNet 256x256 without guidance.
AI.motivation: Two-stage tokenizer and model training can limit joint optimization; integrating tokenizer and generator enables direct feedback to the tokenizer; leveraging vision foundation models may improve token quality and modeling efficiency.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline jointly optimizing reconstruction and generation; direct supervision from generation to tokenizer; investigation of vision foundation models to improve 1D tokenizers; autoregressive modeling on the token space with end-to-end learning.
AI.result: State-of-the-art FID of 1.48 on ImageNet 256x256 generation without guidance; empirical demonstration of the approach's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end tokenizer-gen model training with foundation-model-enhanced tokenizers yields strong generation performance, suggesting practical advantages over two-stage approaches and potential for improved tokenization strategies.

title: PhysiGen: Integrating Collision-Aware Physical Constraints for High-Fidelity Human-Human Interaction Generation
summary: Despite substantial progress in text-driven 3D human motion synthesis, generating realistic multi-person interaction sequences remains challenging. Notably, body inter-penetration is a pervasive issue from both data acquisition to the generated results, which significantly undermines the realism and usability. Previous generative models either ignored this issue or introduced computationally expensive mesh-level loss functions to alleviate inter-body collisions. In this paper, we propose a general-purpose and computationally efficient optimization strategy named PhysiGen to explicitly integrate collision-aware physical constraints for human-human interaction generation. Specifically, we simplify the high-resolution human body mesh into geometric primitives to greatly reduce the cost of inter-person collision detection. Moreover, we identify the collision regions as the guidance of the optimization directions. PhysiGen is plug-and-play and can be readily integrated into existing human interaction generation models. Extensive cross-dataset and cross-model experiments show that our method can effectively reduce interpenetration and significantly improve visual coherence and physical plausibility compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A collision-aware optimization, PhysiGen, enforces physical plausibility in multi-person text-driven 3D motion by simplifying bodies to primitives and guiding optimization with collision regions, achieving efficient, plug-and-play integration and reduced interpenetration.
AI.motivation: Inter-penetration degrades realism in text-driven 3D human motion; prior methods either ignore collisions or rely on expensive mesh-level losses; a general, efficient collision-aware strategy for human-human interactions is needed.
AI.method: Replace high-resolution body meshes with geometric primitives to enable fast collision detection; identify collision regions and use them to steer optimization directions; design as a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with existing human interaction generation models; optimize under collision-aware physical constraints.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets and models, PhysiGen reduces inter-penetration and improves visual coherence and physical plausibility compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: PhysiGen provides a general, efficient, and easily integrable collision-aware constraint module for multi-person motion generation, enhancing realism without expensive mesh-level losses.

title: Data Deletion Can Help in Adaptive RL
summary: Deploying reinforcement learning policies in the real world requires adapting to time-varying environments. We study this problem in the contextual Markov Decision Process (cMDP) framework, where a family of environments is indexed by a low-dimensional context unknown at test time. The standard approach decomposes the problem: train a so-called "universal policy" which assumes knowledge of the true context, then pair it with a context estimator which approximates context using the observed trajectory. We identify a simple, counterintuitive trick that substantially improves the estimator: randomly delete a fraction of the training buffer after each round. This works because data is collected across multiple rounds using progressively better policies, and older trajectories come from a different distribution than what the estimator will face at deployment time; random deletion creates an implicit exponential decay on older data while preserving diversity without requiring any explicit identification of which samples are stale. This reduces robustness gap by 30% for MLPs and by 6% on average for recurrent networks. Strikingly, it allows a narrow MLP with 5x fewer parameters to outperform a wide MLP trained without deletion. To understand when and why deletion helps, we analyze regularized empirical risk minimization with a mismatch between the train distribution and the distribution at deployment; in this idealized setting, we prove that removing a single uniformly random training point decreases expected test loss in expectation under mild conditions. For ridge regression we make this quantitative: deletion helps when the regularization coefficient is moderate and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is sufficiently low, and, crucially, this SNR threshold gives a direct measure of how large the distribution mismatch between training and deployment must be for deletion to be beneficial.

AI.tldr: A simple data-deletion trick during training—randomly deleting a fraction of past training data after each round—substantially reduces the distribution shift between training and deployment in contextual MDPs, boosting robustness. It yields large gains for MLPs, modest gains for RNNs, and enables smaller networks to outperform larger ones; theory shows that even deleting a single random point can reduce expected test loss under mild conditions, with ridge-regression insights tying deletion benefits to regularization and SNR.
AI.motivation: Deploying reinforcement learning policies in time-varying environments where the context changes across rounds; in cMDPs, a model must cope with distributions that shift between training and deployment. The work seeks robust context estimation and policy deployment without explicit context identification.
AI.method: Contextual MDP framework with universal policy and a context estimator. Introduce a simple trick: after each training round, randomly delete a fraction of the training buffer to induce an implicit exponential decay of older data. The approach is analyzed via regularized empirical risk minimization under train–deployment distribution mismatch; complementary theoretical results are presented for ridge regression.
AI.result: Robustness gap reduced by about 30% for MLPs and 6% on average for recurrent networks. A narrow 5x smaller MLP can outperform a wide MLP trained without deletion. Theoretical analysis shows that removing a single uniformly random training point reduces expected test loss under mild conditions; in ridge regression, deletion helps when regularization is moderate and the SNR is sufficiently low, quantifying the required distribution mismatch.
AI.conclusion: A simple, distribution-shift-aware training trick—random data deletion—significantly improves deployment robustness in cMDPs. It works without identifying stale samples, generalizes across model types, and is supported by both empirical gains and a theory that links benefits to regularization strength and SNR.

title: IdentiFace: Multi-Modal Iterative Diffusion Framework for Identifiable Suspect Face Generation in Crime Investigations
summary: Suspect face generation remains a technical challenge in crime investigations. Traditional sketch-drawing workflows suffer from low efficiency and quality, while diffusion-based approaches still face intrinsic limitations on conditional ambiguity for text-to-image models and sampling variance for one-shot generation. We proposed IdentiFace, a novel diffusion-based framework for identifiable suspect face generation, which addressed these issues through (1) multi-modal input design to strengthen conditional control, and (2) an iterative generation pipeline enabling identifiable feature adjustment. We additionally contributed a facial identity loss and two task-specific datasets. Comprehensive experiments on synthetic datasets and in real-world scenarios indicate that IdentiFace achieves superior performance over existing methods, especially in terms of identity retrieval, and shows strong potential for practical applications.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework (IdentiFace) for identifiable suspect face generation using multi-modal conditioning and iterative feature adjustment, achieving improved identity retrieval; includes a facial identity loss and two task-specific datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome inefficiencies and quality limitations of traditional sketch workflows and the conditional ambiguity/sampling variance of diffusion models in one-shot suspect face generation, enabling controllable and identifiable outputs.
AI.method: Introduces IdentiFace: (1) multi-modal input design to strengthen conditional control; (2) an iterative generation pipeline allowing identifiable feature adjustment; plus a facial identity loss and two task-specific datasets.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show superior performance to existing methods, particularly in identity retrieval, indicating strong practical potential.
AI.conclusion: IdentiFace offers improved controllability and identifiable output for suspect face generation, with novel identity loss and datasets, demonstrating promise for real-world crime investigation applications.

title: Federated Weather Modeling on Sensor Data
comment: Accepted by Encyclopedia of GIS, this is an unedited version. Published version: https://link.springer.com/rwe/10.1007/978-3-319-23519-6_1719-1
summary: Federated weather modeling on sensor data is a distributed system underpinned by federated learning, enabling multiple sensor data sources, including ground weather stations, satellites and IoT devices, to collaboratively train deep learning models without sharing raw data. This method safeguards data privacy and security while leverages diverse, geographically distributed datasets to improve the accuracy and robustness of global/regional weather modeling tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Federated learning applied to distributed weather data to train deep learning models without sharing raw data, improving forecasting and anomaly detection while preserving privacy across sensors.
AI.motivation: Need to leverage diverse, geographically distributed sensor data (ground stations, satellites, IoT) while protecting data privacy and security; improve accuracy/robustness of weather models.
AI.method: Federated learning framework coordinating training across multiple data sources, with model aggregation on the server (e.g., FedAvg); deep learning models trained locally on sensor data; privacy-preserving and security-focused aspects; addresses data heterogeneity and communication constraints.
AI.result: Privacy-preserving, collaborative training yields improved accuracy and robustness in global/regional weather modeling tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Federated weather modeling is a promising approach for privacy-preserving collaboration across sensor networks. Practical deployment should address data heterogeneity (non-i.i.d. data) and communication efficiency, but the framework enables training across diverse, geographically distributed datasets without sharing raw data.

title: Vesselpose: Vessel Graph Reconstruction from Learned Voxel-wise Direction Vectors in 3D Vascular Images
summary: Blood vessel segmentation and -tracing are essential tasks in many medical imaging applications. Although numerous methods exist, the prevailing segment-then-fix paradigm is fundamentally limited regarding its suitability for modeling the task of complete and topologically accurate vascular network reconstruction. Here, we propose an approach to extract topologically more accurate vascular graphs from 3D image data, building upon highly successful ideas from the related biomedical tasks of cell segmentation and -tracking. Our approach first predicts voxel-wise vessel direction vectors joint with standard vessel segmentation masks. Second, to extract the vascular graph from these predictions, we introduce a direction-vector-guided extension of the TEASAR algorithm. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmark datasets, spanning both synthetic and real imagery. We further demonstrate the applicability of our approach to challenging 3D micro-CT scans of rat heart vasculature. Finally, we propose meaningful and interpretable measures of topological error, namely false splits and false merges for graphs. Overall, our approach substantially improves the topological accuracy of reconstructed vascular graphs, being able to separate closely apposed vessel segments and handle multiple vascular trees within a single volume.

AI.tldr: A method to reconstruct topologically accurate vascular graphs from 3D images by predicting voxel-wise vessel directions and performing a direction-vector-guided TEASAR graph extraction, achieving state-of-the-art results and introducing interpretable topological errors (false splits/false merges).
AI.motivation: The segment-then-fix paradigm struggles to produce complete, topologically correct vascular networks; there is a need for methods that accurately capture topology, separate closely spaced vessels, and handle multiple vascular trees in a volume.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) predict voxel-wise vessel direction vectors jointly with standard vessel segmentation masks; (2) extract the vascular graph using a direction-vector-guided extension of the TEASAR algorithm; evaluate on three benchmark datasets (synthetic and real) and demonstrate applicability to 3D micro-CT rat heart vasculature; propose interpretable topological measures (false splits/false merges).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmark datasets across synthetic and real imagery; demonstrates applicability to challenging 3D micro-CT rat heart vasculature; improves topological accuracy, enabling separation of closely apposed vessel segments and handling multiple vascular trees within a single volume.
AI.conclusion: Direction-vector-guided graph extraction substantially improves topological accuracy of reconstructed vascular graphs and provides interpretable metrics for topological errors, supporting robust reconstruction of complex vascular networks in 3D imaging.

title: Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles for Scalable Operator Learning with Distribution-Free Uncertainty
summary: Operator learning enables fast surrogate modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems, but existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: quadratic inference complexity and unreliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical settings. We propose Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles, a framework that addresses both challenges simultaneously. By leveraging Quantum Orthogonal Neural Networks (QOrthoNNs), we reduce operator inference complexity from O(n^2) to O(n), enabling scalable evaluation over fine discretizations. To provide rigorous uncertainty quantification, we combine ensemble-based epistemic modeling with adaptive conformal prediction, yielding distribution-free coverage guarantees. A key challenge in ensembling is that naive parallelism scales hardware resources linearly with the number of models. We resolve this by using Superposed Parameterized Quantum Circuits (SPQCs), which compress multiple ensemble members into a single circuit and enable simultaneous multi-model execution. Experiments on synthetic partial differential equations and real-world power system dynamics demonstrate that our approach achieves accurate predictions while maintaining calibrated uncertainty under realistic quantum noise. These results establish a practical pathway toward scalable, uncertainty-aware operator learning in quantum machine learning.

AI.tldr: Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles (CQDE) reduces operator inference complexity from quadratic to linear using Quantum Orthogonal Neural Networks (QOrthoNNs) and provides distribution-free uncertainty via ensemble-based conformal prediction. SPQCs compress ensemble members to enable single-circuit multi-model execution, demonstrated on synthetic PDEs and power-system dynamics with quantum noise-aware calibration.
AI.motivation: Address two core limitations in operator learning for high-dimensional dynamical systems: high inference complexity (O(n^2)) and unreliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical contexts.
AI.method: Introduce QOrthoNNs to lower inference complexity to O(n). Build an ensemble of models for epistemic uncertainty and apply adaptive conformal prediction for distribution-free coverage guarantees. Employ Superposed Parameterized Quantum Circuits (SPQCs) to pack multiple ensemble members into a single quantum circuit for simultaneous execution, mitigating hardware resource scaling. Evaluate on synthetic PDEs and real-world power dynamics under realistic quantum noise.
AI.result: Achieves accurate predictive performance with calibrated uncertainty under quantum noise. Demonstrates scalable, uncertainty-aware operator learning in a quantum ML setting across synthetic PDEs and real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: CQDE provides a practical path to scalable operator learning with rigorous uncertainty quantification in quantum ML, combining complexity reduction (O(n) via QOrthoNNs), ensemble epistemic uncertainty, adaptive conformal prediction, and efficient hardware through SPQCs.

title: Odysseus: Scaling VLMs to 100+ Turn Decision-Making in Games via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Given the rapidly growing capabilities of vision-language models (VLMs), extending them to interactive decision-making tasks such as video games has emerged as a promising frontier. However, existing approaches either rely on large-scale supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on human trajectories or apply reinforcement learning (RL) only in relatively short-horizon settings (typically around 20--30 turns). In this work, we study RL-based training of VLMs for long-horizon decision-making in Super Mario Land, a visually grounded environment requiring 100+ turns of interaction with coordinated perception, reasoning, and action. We begin with a systematic investigation of key algorithmic components and propose an adapted variant of PPO with a lightweight turn-level critic, which substantially improves training stability and sample efficiency over critic-free methods such as GRPO and Reinforce++. We further show that pretrained VLMs provide strong action priors, significantly improving sample efficiency during RL training and reducing the need for manual design choices such as action engineering, compared to classical deep RL trained from scratch. Building on these insights, we introduce Odysseus, an open training framework for VLM agents, achieving substantial gains across multiple levels of the game and at least 3 times average game progresses than frontier models. Moreover, the trained models exhibit consistent improvements under both in-game and cross-game generalization settings, while maintaining general-domain capabilities. Overall, our results identify key ingredients for making RL stable and effective in long-horizon, multi-modal settings, and provide practical guidance for developing VLMs as embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Odysseus: RL framework to train vision-language models for long-horizon embodied tasks (Super Mario Land). PPO variant with a turn-level critic; VLMs provide action priors; open framework yielding 3x progress and good generalization.
AI.motivation: Extend VLMs to long-horizon, visually grounded decision making; overcome limitations of supervised fine-tuning and short-horizon RL; harness VLM priors to improve efficiency.
AI.method: Propose adapted PPO with lightweight turn-level critic; compare to critic-free methods (GRPO, Reinforce++); leverage pretrained VLMs as action priors to improve sample efficiency; introduce Odysseus, an open RL framework for VLM agents; evaluate on multiple levels and cross-game generalization.
AI.result: Improved training stability and sample efficiency over critic-free baselines; VLM priors reduce need for action engineering; Odysseus achieves substantial gains across levels, at least 3x average game progress vs frontier models; consistent in-game and cross-game improvements; preserves general-domain capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Identifies key ingredients for stable, effective RL in long-horizon multi-modal settings; provides practical guidance for developing VLMs as embodied agents; Odysseus as a scalable open framework.

title: Colorful-Noise: Training-Free Low-Frequency Noise Manipulation for Color-Based Conditional Image Generation
comment: SIGGRAPH 2026 Conference Paper. Project Page at: https://nadavc220.github.io/colorful-noise/
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models generate images by gradually converting white Gaussian noise into a natural image. White Gaussian noise is well suited for producing diverse outputs from a single text prompt due to its absence of structure. However, this very property limits control over, and predictability of, specific visual attributes, as the noise is not human-interpretable. In this work, we investigate the characteristics of the input noise in diffusion models. We show that, although all frequencies in white Gaussian noise have comparable statistical energy, low-frequency components primarily determine the images global structure and color composition, while high-frequency components control finer details. Building on this observation, we demonstrate that simple manipulations of the low-frequency noise using low-frequency image priors can effectively condition the generation process to reconstruct these low-frequency visual cues. This allows us to define a simple, training-free method with minimal overhead that steers overall image structure and color, while letting high-frequency components freely emerge as fine details, enabling variability across generated outputs.

AI.tldr: Low-frequency noise manipulation via priors to steer diffusion model outputs without retraining; controls global structure and color while preserving high-frequency variability.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models produce diverse outputs from white noise but lack interpretable control over visual attributes; understanding input noise enables targeted steering.
AI.method: Analyze frequency distribution of Gaussian noise; show low frequencies govern global structure/color, high frequencies govern details; apply low-frequency image priors to input noise to condition generation; training-free approach with minimal overhead.
AI.result: Demonstrates that simple low-frequency noise manipulations can condition global structure and color; high-frequency content remains free to create variations; enables controllable, diverse outputs without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Low-frequency priors are effective, lightweight conditioning signals for diffusion models, offering predictable control over major image attributes while preserving detail variability.

title: Borrowed Geometry: Computational Reuse of Frozen Text-Pretrained Transformer Weights Across Modalities
summary: Frozen Gemma 4 31B weights pretrained exclusively on text tokens, unmodified, transfer across modality boundaries through a thin trainable interface. (1) OGBench scene-play-singletask-task1-v0: $+4.33$pt over published GCIQL at $n=3$ with std 0.74 -- a published-SOTA win on a robotic manipulation task the substrate has never seen. (2) D4RL Walker2d-medium-v2: Decision-Transformer parity ($76.2 \pm 0.8$, $n=3$) at $0.43\times$ DT's trainable count, with the frozen substrate compressing to a 5L slice ($+1.66$pt over the 6L baseline at $n=3$). (3) Associative recall as the cleanest pretraining-load-bearing case: the frozen slice + a 113K-parameter linear interface reaches L30 best-checkpoint per-bit error 0.0505 ($n=2$); a 6.36M-parameter from-scratch trained transformer at matched capacity ($1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling, two seeds, LR sweep) cannot solve the task at all under the protocol (best L30 = 0.4395), an $8.7\times$ advantage. Architecture-alone falsifications: a frozen random transformer with correct $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling stays at random-chance loss for 50k steps; a random-init Gemma slice fails OGBench cube-double-play-task1 entirely (0.89% across $n=3$ where pretrained reaches 60%). A dual-measurement protocol -- text-activation probing on 95 English sentences plus task-ablation on a non-language target -- names individual heads independently identifiable on both protocols: head L26.28 scores $3.7\times$ the slice mean for English token-copying and is the #2 most-critical head for binary copy ablation ($Δ$ L30 $= +0.221$); three further heads (L27.28, L27.2, L27.3) classify by the same protocol. The mechanism is single-model and the cross-modality results are single-task within their respective benchmarks; cross-model replication is structurally constrained because Gemma 4 31B is the only model on the small-scale Pareto frontier as of April 2026.

AI.tldr: Frozen text-pretrained Gemma 4 31B weights can transfer across modalities via a small trainable interface, delivering notable gains on robotic manipulation (OGBench), competitive performance with reduced trainable counts on D4RL Walker2d, and a strong advantage on associative recall. Interpretability analyses identify specific heads critical for cross-modal copying, while ablations highlight the limits of cross-model replication due to current Pareto frontier constraints.
AI.motivation: Test whether large, text-only pretrained models can serve as modular substrates for non-language tasks when coupled with a thin trainable interface, reducing task-specific training while maintaining performance across modalities.
AI.method: Freeze a large text-pretrained model (Gemma 4 31B) and attach a tiny trainable interface (e.g., 5-layer slice) to enable cross-modality outputs. Evaluate on multiple benchmarks (OGBench scene-play-singletask-task1-v0, D4RL Walker2d-medium-v2, and an associative recall task). Compare to task-specific, fully trainable baselines. Include architecture-level falsifications (frozen random transformer, random-init slice) and a dual-measurement interpretability protocol (text-activation probing on English sentences and non-language task ablations) to identify influential heads.
