AI.result: (1) OGBench scene task: +4.33 pt over published GCIQL at n=3 with std 0.74, a published SOTA on a substrate unseen during training. (2) D4RL Walker2d-medium-v2: Decision-Transformer parity with 76.2 ± 0.8 at 0.43× trainable parameters, using a 5-layer frozen slice. (3) Associative recall: a 113K-param linear interface achieves L30 best-checkpoint per-bit error 0.0505 (n=2); a 6.36M-param from-scratch transformer at matched capacity cannot solve the task (best L30 = 0.4395), an 8.7× advantage. Ablations: frozen random transformer with correct 1/√d_k scaling remains at random; random-init Gemma slice fails OGBench cube-double-play-task1 (0.89% accuracy). Interpretability: Head L26.28 is 3.7× the slice mean for English token-copying and is the #2 most-critical head for binary-copy ablation; other heads (L27.28, L27.2, L27.3) classify under the same protocol. Cross-modality findings are reported within their benchmarks; cross-model replication is limited since Gemma 4 31B is the sole model on the small-scale Pareto frontier as of Apr 2026.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal transfer from a frozen text-only substrate is feasible with a lightweight trainable interface, yielding competitive or superior performance in several tasks while dramatically reducing trainable parameters. The effect is task- and head-dependent, with some heads driving cross-modal copying and others showing negligible impact. Reproducibility across models is currently constrained by the lack of comparable small-scale models on the Pareto frontier.

title: Hypergraph and Latent ODE Learning for Multimodal Root Cause Localization in Microservices
summary: Root cause localization in cloud native microservice systems requires modeling complex service dependencies, irregular temporal dynamics, and heterogeneous observability data. We present HyperODE RCA, a unified framework that combines hypergraph attention learning, latent ordinary differential equations, and multimodal cross attention fusion for fine grained root cause analysis. The method learns higher order service interactions through differentiable hyperedge construction, captures continuous anomaly evolution from irregular observations with an ODE RNN encoder, and adaptively fuses logs, traces, metrics, entities, and events using context aware modality routing. We further improve robustness with a variational information bottleneck, temporal causal regularization, and invariant risk constraints. Experiments on the Tianchi AIOps benchmark show clear gains over strong baselines in ranking and classification performance, while preserving interpretability through learned hypergraph attention.

AI.tldr: Unified HyperODE RCA framework for root-cause analysis in cloud-native microservice systems, integrating hypergraph attention, latent ODEs, and multimodal fusion.
AI.motivation: Root-cause localization is challenged by complex service dependencies, irregular temporal dynamics, and heterogeneous observability data; a unified model is needed to capture higher-order interactions and continuous anomaly evolution while remaining robust and interpretable.
AI.method: 1) differentiable hyperedge construction for higher-order interactions via hypergraph attention; 2) ODE-RNN encoder to model continuous anomaly evolution under irregular observations; 3) context-aware modality routing to fuse logs, traces, metrics, entities, events; 4) robustness via variational information bottleneck, temporal causal regularization, invariant risk constraints.
AI.result: On the Tianchi AIOps benchmark, HyperODE RCA shows clear gains over strong baselines in ranking and classification while preserving interpretability through learned hypergraph attention.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust, interpretable, and scalable solution for fine-grained RCA in cloud-native microservice systems, effectively modeling complex dependencies and irregular dynamics.

title: Depth-Guided Privacy-Preserving Visual Localization Using 3D Sphere Clouds
summary: The emergence of deep neural networks capable of revealing high-fidelity scene details from sparse 3D point clouds has raised significant privacy concerns in visual localization involving private maps. Lifting map points to randomly oriented 3D lines is a well-known approach for obstructing undesired recovery of the scene images, but these lines are vulnerable to a density-based attack that can recover the point cloud geometry by observing the neighborhood statistics of lines. With the aim of nullifying this attack, we present a new privacy-preserving scene representation called \emph{sphere cloud}, which is constructed by lifting all points to 3D lines crossing the centroid of the map, resembling points on the unit sphere. Since lines are most dense at the map centroid, the sphere cloud mislead the density-based attack algorithm to incorrectly yield points at the centroid, effectively neutralizing the attack. Nevertheless, this advantage comes at the cost of i) a new type of attack that may directly recover images from this cloud representation and ii) unresolved translation scale for camera pose estimation. To address these issues, we introduce a simple yet effective cloud construction strategy to thwart new attack and propose an efficient localization framework to guide the translation scale by utilizing absolute depth maps acquired from on-device time-of-flight (ToF) sensors. Experimental results on public RGB-D datasets demonstrate sphere cloud achieves competitive privacy-preserving ability and localization runtime while not excessively compensating the pose estimation accuracy compared to other depth-guided localization methods.

AI.tldr: Sphere cloud is a privacy-preserving representation for 3D point clouds in visual localization, transforming points into lines crossing the map centroid (like points on a unit sphere) to disrupt density-based attacks, while enabling depth-guided localization with ToF depth maps; it trades some new attack vulnerability and translation-scale ambiguity for improved privacy and runtime.
AI.motivation: To mitigate privacy risks in visual localization that uses private maps by line-based obfuscation susceptible to density-based attacks, and to develop a robust, efficient representation that resists such attacks while enabling accurate localization.
AI.method: Construct a 'sphere cloud' by lifting all map points to 3D lines that cross the map centroid, effectively mapping to the unit sphere. To counter the new attack and translation-scale issues, propose a cloud-construction strategy and integrate an absolute depth-guided localization framework using on-device ToF depth maps to estimate scale. Evaluate on RGB-D datasets for privacy, localization speed, and pose accuracy.
AI.result: Sphere cloud reduces susceptibility to density-based point-recovery attacks and provides competitive privacy preservation and faster localization, with comparable pose accuracy to depth-guided methods. The approach introduces a new potential attack (image recovery) and translation-scale ambiguity, which the authors address via the proposed construction strategy and depth-guided localization.
AI.conclusion: Sphere cloud is a promising privacy-preserving representation for visual localization with private maps, balancing privacy, efficiency, and pose estimation performance, though additional defenses are needed against new image-recovery attacks and scale ambiguity.

title: Free Energy Surface Sampling via Reduced Flow Matching
summary: Sampling the free energy surface, namely, the distribution of collective variables (CVs), is a crucial problem in statistical physics, as it underpins a better understanding of chemical reactions and conformational transitions. Traditional methods for free energy surface sampling involve simulation in high-dimensional configuration space and projecting the resulting configurations onto the CV space. To reduce the computational costs of such sampling, we propose FES-FM, a reduced flow matching (FM) method for free energy sampling (FES). We train a dynamical transport map in the CV space, thereby enabling direct sampling of the free energy surface. For many-particle systems, we construct a prior distribution based on the Hessian at a local minimum of the potential, which ensures both rotation-translation invariance and physically meaningful configurations. We evaluate the proposed method across a variety of potential functions and collective variables. Comparative experiments demonstrate that our approach drastically reduces computational costs while delivering superior accuracy per unit sampling time.

AI.tldr: A reduced flow matching method (FES-FM) for free energy surface sampling in CV space, training a dynamical transport map to enable direct FES sampling. Uses a Hessian-based prior at a local minimum to enforce rotation–translation invariance and physically meaningful configurations; demonstrates substantial speedups and improved accuracy per sampling time across multiple potentials.
AI.motivation: To efficiently sample high-dimensional free energy surfaces by operating directly in collective variable space, avoiding expensive projections from full configurations while maintaining invariance properties and physical relevance.
AI.method: Train a dynamical transport map in CV space (reduced flow matching) to sample the free energy surface. Construct a Hessian-based prior at a local minimum to enforce rotation-translation invariance and generate physically meaningful CV configurations. Evaluate on a range of potential functions and CVs, comparing performance to baselines.
AI.result: Significant reductions in computational cost with superior accuracy per unit sampling time across tested scenarios, demonstrating the effectiveness of FES-FM in sampling FES.
AI.conclusion: FES-FM offers an efficient, scalable framework for free energy surface sampling by combining reduced flow matching with Hessian-informed priors, suitable for many-particle systems and diverse CV choices.

title: VQ-SAD: Vector Quantized Structure Aware Diffusion For Molecule Generation
summary: Many diffusion based molecule generation methods ignore the symbolic information of molecules and represent the atom and bond type as one hot representation. Methods based on Morgan fingerprints produce hash collisions and are hard to embed into a continuous space without information loss and random fingerprints correspond to no valid molecule. To circumvent this issue we use another paradigm and consider atom and bond codes as latent variables of VQ-VAE. We introduce VQ-SAD which first trains a VQ-VAE and uses the frozen pretrained VQ-VAE model and considers the codebooks for both atom and bond types as tokenizers for the downstream diffusion process. VQ-SAD is a neuro-symbolic model that utilizes both symbolic and neural structural information for a diffusion based model with learnable forward process. The large discrete code space provides a more balanced atom and bond types which enhances the denoising process. VQ-VAE slightly outperforms SOTA models for diffusion based molecule generation on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic diffusion model (VQ-SAD) using frozen VQ-VAE codebooks as discrete tokenizers for atom and bond types, enabling diffusion over a large discrete latent space and improving molecule generation; achieves slight SOTA on QM9 and ZINC250k.
AI.motivation: Overcome information loss and collisions from traditional one-hot or hash-based representations (Morgan fingerprints) by leveraging discrete latent codes that preserve symbolic structure while enabling neural diffusion.
AI.method: Train a VQ-VAE to encode atoms and bonds into discrete codebooks; freeze the pretrained VQ-VAE; use its atom and bond codebooks as tokenizers for a diffusion process with a learnable forward process; perform diffusion in the discrete latent space to generate valid molecules.
AI.result: VQ-VAE slightly outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion-based molecule generation models on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.
AI.conclusion: Integrating symbolic (discrete codes) and neural (VQ-VAE) representations in diffusion models yields improved molecule generation performance; the large discrete code space helps balance atom/bond types and aids denoising.

title: 2D-SuGaR: Surface-Aware Gaussian Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Mesh Reconstruction
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful technique for generating photorealistic renderings of a scene in real-time. However, the volumetric nature of 3DGS limits its ability to accurately capture surface geometry. To address this, 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) was proposed to enable view-consistent and geometrically accurate surface reconstruction from multi-view images. However, 2DGS can be sensitive to the initialization of the Gaussian primitives. Reliance on Structure-from-Motion (SfM) initializations, which can produce poor estimates on challenging image sets, may lead to subpar results. In this work, we enhance 2DGS by incorporating monocular depth and normal priors to improve both geometric accuracy and robustness. We propose a depth-guided initialization strategy for Gaussians and introduce a clustering-based technique for pruning degenerate Gaussians. We evaluate our method on the DTU dataset, where it achieves state-of-the-art results in mesh reconstruction while preserving high-quality novel view synthesis.

AI.tldr: Depth-guided 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) using monocular depth and surface normals to improve geometric accuracy and robustness, with depth-guided Gaussian initialization and clustering-based pruning of degenerate Gaussians; achieves state-of-the-art mesh reconstruction on the DTU dataset while preserving high-quality novel view synthesis.
AI.motivation: 2DGS can be highly sensitive to Gaussian initialization and SfM-derived priors, leading to suboptimal geometry, especially on challenging image sets. Incorporating monocular depth and normal priors aims to improve geometric fidelity and robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a depth-guided initialization strategy for Gaussian primitives and a clustering-based pruning mechanism to remove degenerate Gaussians. Leverage monocular depth and normal priors to guide both initialization and optimization within the 2D Gaussian Splatting framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the DTU dataset shows state-of-the-art mesh reconstruction quality while maintaining high-quality novel view synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Monocular depth and normal priors effectively improve 2DGS geometry and robustness, mitigating sensitivity to initializations and enabling superior surface reconstruction without sacrificing rendering quality.

title: AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies via Large Language Models with Deployment Guarantees
summary: We study how large language models can be used to evolve inventory policies in online, non-stationary environments. Our work is motivated by recent advances in LLM-based evolutionary search, such as AlphaEvolve, which demonstrates strong performance for static and highly structured problems such as mathematical discovery, but is not directly suited to online dynamic inventory settings. To this end, we propose AlphaInventory, an end-to-end inventory-policy evolution and inference framework grounded in confidence-interval-based certification. The framework trains a large language model using reinforcement learning, incorporates demand data as well as numerical and textual features beyond demand, and generates white-box inventory policy with statistical safety guarantees for deployment in future periods. We further introduce a unified theoretical interface that connects training, inference, and deployment. This allows us to characterize the probability that the AlphaInventory evolves a statistically safe and improved policy, and to quantify the deployment gap relative to the oracle-safe benchmark. Tested on both synthetic data and real-world retail data, AlphaInventory outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning based methods. In canonical inventory settings, it evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end framework, AlphaInventory, uses RL-trained LLMs to evolve and certify inventory policies with statistical safety guarantees in online non-stationary settings, outperforming classical policies and DL baselines.
AI.motivation: Address non-stationary demand and the need for auditable, safe inventory policies; extend LLM-based evolution (e.g., AlphaEvolve) to dynamic, online environments with deployment-time guarantees.
AI.method: Train a large language model using reinforcement learning, integrate demand data and numerical/text features, and evolve white-box inventory policies. Use confidence-interval-based certification to provide safety guarantees. Introduce a unified interface connecting training, inference, and deployment to quantify deployment gaps relative to an oracle-safe benchmark.
AI.result: AlphaInventory outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning-based methods on synthetic and real-world data. It evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks while providing probabilistic guarantees of safety and performance improvements.
AI.conclusion: AlphaInventory demonstrates a practical, certifiable, LLM-driven approach to evolving inventory policies in non-stationary environments, with potential for deployment in future periods and applicability to canonical inventory settings.

title: Jailbreaking Vision-Language Models Through the Visual Modality
summary: The visual modality of vision-language models (VLMs) is an underexplored attack surface for bypassing safety alignment. We introduce four jailbreak attacks exploiting the vision component: (1) encoding harmful instructions as visual symbol sequences with a decoding legend, (2) replacing harmful objects with benign substitutes (e.g., bomb -> banana) then prompting for harmful actions using the substitute term, (3) replacing harmful text in images (e.g., on book covers) with benign words while visual context preserves the original meaning, and (4) visual analogy puzzles whose solution requires inferring a prohibited concept. Evaluating across six frontier VLMs, our visual attacks bypass safety alignment and expose a cross-modality alignment gap: text-based safety training does not automatically generalize to harmful intent conveyed visually. For example, our visual cipher achieves 40.9% attack success on Claude-Haiku-4.5 versus 10.7% for an equivalent textual cipher. To further our insight into the attack mechanism, we present preliminary interpretability and mitigation results. These findings highlight that robust VLM alignment requires treating vision as a first-class target for safety post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates four visual-domain jailbreak attacks against vision-language models to bypass safety alignment, revealing a cross-modality alignment gap and highlighting the need to treat vision as a safety target in post-training.
AI.motivation: Text-based safety alignment does not automatically generalize to visual or cross-modal risks in vision-language models; understanding these gaps is essential for robust VLM safety.
AI.method: Four attack vectors exploiting the visual channel: (1) encoding instructions as visual symbol sequences with a decoding legend; (2) substituting harmful objects with benign substitutes and prompting with the substitute term; (3) replacing harmful text in images with benign words while preserving harmful meaning through visual context; (4) visual analogy puzzles requiring inference of prohibited concepts; evaluation across six frontier VLMs; preliminary interpretability and mitigation experiments.
AI.result: Attacks bypass safety alignment across multiple models, revealing a cross-modality alignment gap. The visual cipher achieves 40.9% attack success on Claude-Haiku-4.5 versus 10.7% for an equivalent textual cipher, with consistent vulnerability across six VLMs and initial mitigation insights.
AI.conclusion: Robust VLM alignment requires treating vision as a first-class safety target for post-training; relying solely on text-based safety training is insufficient for cross-modal alignment.

title: BWLA: Breaking the Barrier of W1AX Post-Training Quantization for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have driven major progress in NLP, yet their substantial memory and compute demands still hinder practical deployment. Binarization can compress weights to 1 bit, fundamentally lowering compute and bandwidth cost. However, existing methods cannot address activation heavy tails and thus must keep activations in high precision, preventing true end-to-end acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose BWLA (Binarized Weights and Low-bit Activations), the first post-training quantization framework that preserves high accuracy while achieving 1-bit weight quantization together with low-bit activations (e.g., 6 bits). The Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) learns an orthogonal mapping via EM minimization, converting unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal forms while suppressing activation tails and incoherence. The Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) then performs lightweight low-rank refinement through proximal SVD projection, further enhancing quantizability with minimal overhead. On Qwen3-32B, BWLA reaches a Wikitext2 perplexity of 11.92 under 6-bit activations (vs. 38 from SOTA), improves five zero-shot tasks by more than 70%, and delivers 3.26 times inference speedup, demonstrating strong potential for real-world LLM compression and acceleration.

AI.tldr: BWLA enables 1-bit weight quantization with low-bit activations via OKT and PSP, achieving substantial speedups with negligible accuracy loss on LLMs.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and compute demands of LLMs; activation heavy tails obstruct end-to-end quantization; need a post-training quantization framework.
AI.method: Two components: Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) to reshape unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal distributions by EM minimization; Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) for lightweight low-rank refinement; apply post-training quantization to achieve 1-bit weights and ~6-bit activations.
AI.result: On Qwen3-32B, perplexity 11.92 on WikiText-2 with 6-bit activations (vs 38 SOTA); >70% improvements on five zero-shot tasks; 3.26x inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: BWLA demonstrates effective end-to-end compression/acceleration for LLMs with minimal accuracy loss, indicating strong practical potential.

title: Intrinsic Gradient Suppression for Label-Noise Prompt Tuning in Vision-Language Models
summary: Contrastive vision-language models like CLIP exhibit remarkable zero-shot generalization. However, prompt tuning remains highly sensitive to label noise, as mislabeled samples generate disproportionately large gradients that can overwhelm pre-trained priors. We argue that because CLIP already provides a near-optimal initialization, adaptation should be inherently conservative, particularly against the extreme gradient updates common in noisy settings. To this end, we propose Double-Softmax Prompt Tuning (DSPT), a hyperparameter-free method for intrinsic gradient suppression. By applying a sequential probabilistic normalization, DSPT induces a self-adaptive saturation zone that suppresses gradients from high-error noisy samples while maintaining informative updates. We also provide both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence about how this mechanism achieves adaptive suppression. This design transforms ``gradient vanishing'', traditionally a training bottleneck, into a principled noise-filtering shield for label-noise prompt tuning. Extensive experiments confirm that this simple, drop-in design achieves state-of-the-art robustness across various noisy benchmarks, outperforming methods with complex architectures and handcrafted hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: DSPT is a hyperparameter-free prompt-tuning method for CLIP that uses double-softmax normalization to suppress gradients from mislabeled samples, achieving robust performance against label noise without extra hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: CLIP has near-optimal initialization, but prompt tuning is highly sensitive to label noise; need a conservative adaptation that prevents large updates from mislabeled data.
AI.method: Double-Softmax Prompt Tuning (DSPT): apply sequential probabilistic normalization (two softmax steps) to prompts to create a self-adaptive saturation zone that dampens gradients from high-error noisy samples while preserving informative updates; hyperparameter-free and theoretically analyzed.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art robustness on various noisy benchmarks; simple drop-in approach that outperforms methods with more complex architectures and handcrafted hyperparameters; provides theoretical analysis and empirical evidence for adaptive gradient suppression.
AI.conclusion: Gradient vanishing is reframed as a noise-filtering mechanism, enabling robust, efficient prompt tuning for CLIP under label noise; DSPT offers a practical, hyperparameter-free solution with strong empirical support.

title: Binomial flows: Denoising and flow matching for discrete ordinal data
summary: Flow-based generative modeling in continuous spaces exploit Tweedie's formula to express the denoiser (learned in training) as a score function (used in sampling). In contrast, this relation has been largely missing in the discrete setting where common approaches focus on learning discrete scores and rates. In this work we close this gap for discrete non-negative ordinal data by introducing Binomial flows. Our framework provides a simple recipe for training a discrete diffusion model which simultaneously denoises, samples, and estimates exact likelihoods. We verify our methodology on synthetic examples and obtain competitive results on real-world data sets.

AI.tldr: Binomial flows enable a discrete diffusion model for non-negative ordinal data, connecting Tweedie-style score-based denoising to discrete data, with training that denoises, samples, and yields exact likelihoods; validated on synthetic and real data with competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Discrete non-negative ordinal data have lacked a Tweedie/score-based diffusion framework. There is a need for discrete diffusion models that provide exact likelihoods and unify denoising, sampling, and likelihood estimation.
AI.method: Introduce Binomial flows: a diffusion process over discrete non-negative integers using binomial noise. Derive a training recipe where the learned denoiser corresponds to a score in the discrete setting (via Tweedie-type relations). The framework enables denoising, sampling, and exact likelihood computation for discrete data.
AI.result: Demonstrated on synthetic datasets and achieves competitive results on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The work closes the gap between continuous Tweedie-score diffusion and discrete non-negative ordinal data, offering a simple, effective recipe (Binomial flows) for training discrete diffusion models that denoise, sample, and provide exact likelihoods.

title: PAMod: Modeling Cyclical Shifts via Phase-Amplitude Modulation for Non-stationary Time Series Forecasting
summary: Real-world time series forecasting faces the fundamental challenge of non-stationary statistical properties, including shifts in mean and variance over time. While reversible instance normalization (RevIN) has shown promise by stationarizing inputs and denormalizing outputs, it relies on the strong assumption that historical and future distributions remain identical. We observe that in many practical applications, distribution shifts follow cyclical patterns that correlate with periodic positions (e.g., seasonal and holiday volatility). To this end, we propose PAMod, a lightweight yet powerful framework that models cyclical distribution shifts via Phase-Amplitude Modulation in the normalized feature space. PAMod learns periodic embeddings to modulate representations: phase modulation captures mean shifts, while amplitude modulation adapts to variance changes. Crucially, we prove mathematically that modulating in normalized space is equivalent to applying dynamic denormalization, offering an elegant unification of distribution adaptation and representation learning. Extensive experiments on twelve real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PAMod achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer computational resources. Furthermore, our modulation mechanism, as a novel plug-and-play technique, can improve existing time-series forecasting methods with simple integration.

AI.tldr: PAMod: Phase-Amplitude Modulation in normalized space to model cyclical distribution shifts; proves equivalence to dynamic denormalization; achieves state-of-the-art with less compute; plug-and-play for other methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series exhibit cyclical distribution shifts (mean/variance) correlated with periodic positions. RevIN assumes identical historical and future distributions, which is often violated. A cycle-aware mechanism is needed to adapt distributions without heavy normalization.
AI.method: PAMod learns periodic embeddings to modulate normalized features: phase modulation targets mean shifts, amplitude modulation targets variance changes. Demonstrates mathematically that modulatory in normalized space equates to dynamic denormalization. Lightweight and plug-and-play.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on twelve real-world benchmarks show PAMod achieving state-of-the-art performance with lower computational resources. The modulation mechanism can be integrated into existing forecasting methods as a simple enhancement.
AI.conclusion: PAMod provides an elegant unification of distribution adaptation and representation learning by operating in normalized space; it is effective, efficient, and broadly applicable as a plug-in for time-series forecasting.

title: Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization: Breaking RLVR's Indifference to Diversity
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved substantial gains in single-attempt accuracy (Pass@1) on reasoning tasks, yet often suffers from reduced multi-sample coverage (Pass@K), indicating diversity collapse. We identify a structural cause for this degradation: common RLVR objectives, such as GRPO, are indifferent to how probability mass is distributed among correct solutions. Combined with stochastic training dynamics, this indifference induces a self-reinforcing collapse, in which probability mass concentrates on a narrow subset of correct outputs while alternative valid solutions are suppressed. We formalize this collapse mechanism and further characterize the optimal policy structure under two complementary criteria: robustness and entropy-regularized optimality, which identify the Uniform-Correct Policy as uniquely optimal. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO), a modification to GRPO that adds a conditional uniformity penalty on the policy's distribution over correct solutions. The penalty redistributes gradient signal toward underrepresented correct responses, encouraging uniform allocation of probability mass within the correct set. Across three models (1.5B-7B parameters) and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, UCPO improves Pass@K and diversity while maintaining competitive Pass@1, achieving up to +10\% absolute improvement on AIME24 at Pass@64 and up to 45\% higher equation-level diversity within the correct set. The code is available at https://github.com/AnamikaLochab/UCPO.

AI.tldr: Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO) mitigates diversity collapse in RL with verifiable rewards by enforcing near-uniform distribution over correct outputs, improving Pass@K and diversity without harming Pass@1.
AI.motivation: RLVR often achieves high single-sample accuracy (Pass@1) but suffers from reduced multi-sample coverage (Pass@K) due to diversity collapse. The paper identifies a structural cause: GRPO-like objectives ignore how probability mass is allocated among correct solutions, which with stochastic training leads to a self-reinforcing collapse. It formalizes this and derives the Uniform-Correct Policy as uniquely optimal under robustness and entropy-regularized criteria.
AI.method: Introduce Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO), a modification to GRPO adding a conditional uniformity penalty on the policy's distribution over correct solutions. The penalty shifts gradients toward underrepresented correct responses, promoting uniform probability mass within the correct set. Evaluation across three models (1.5B–7B params) and five math reasoning benchmarks, comparing against GRPO; code provided.
AI.result: UCPO improves Pass@K and diversity while maintaining competitive Pass@1; up to +10 percentage points on AIME24 at Pass@64; up to 45% higher equation-level diversity within the correct set.
AI.conclusion: A Uniform-Correct Policy is uniquely optimal under robustness and entropy-regularized criteria; UCPO effectively alleviates diversity collapse, yielding more diverse correct outputs without sacrificing early accuracy. Code available.

title: Scalable Context-Aware Graph Attention for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Large-Scale Mobile Networks
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary:
Mobile network operators must monitor thousands of heterogeneous network elements across the radio access network and the packet core, each exposing high-dimensional KPI time series. The scale and cost of incident labelling make supervised approaches impractical, motivating unsupervised anomaly detection robust to context shifts and nonstationarity.
  We propose \textbf{C-MTAD-GAT} (\emph{Context-aware Multivariate Time-series Anomaly Detection with Graph Attention}), an anomaly detection framework designed to operate as a single shared model across large populations of network elements. The model combines temporal and feature-wise graph attention with lightweight static and dynamic context conditioning and a dual-head decoder for reconstruction and multi-step forecasting. It produces per-element, per-feature anomaly scores, converted to alerts via fully unsupervised thresholds calibrated from validation residuals.
  On the TELCO dataset released with DC-VAE \cite{garcia2023onemodel}, C-MTAD-GAT improves event-level affiliation and pointwise F1 while generating fewer alarms than prior graph-attention and VAE-based baselines. We then apply the same system to nation-scale radio access and evolved packet core control-plane counter data from a mobile network operator, where it is deployed. Operator feedback indicates the alerts are actionable and support daily monitoring, showing scalability across domains without relying on labelled incidents.

AI.tldr: Context-aware multivariate time-series anomaly detection for wide-scale telecom elements using graph attention; unsupervised, scalable, dual-head decoder; achieves better metrics and actionable alerts; deployed in production.
AI.motivation: Supervised labeling is impractical due to scale and nonstationarity of KPI time series across thousands of network elements; need robust unsupervised anomaly detection that generalizes across elements and domains.
AI.method: C-MTAD-GAT combines temporal and feature-wise graph attention; uses static and dynamic context conditioning; dual-head decoder for reconstruction and multi-step forecasting; per-element, per-feature anomaly scores; unsupervised thresholds calibrated from validation residuals.
AI.result: On TELCO dataset, improves event-level affiliation and per-point F1; fewer alarms compared to graph-attention and VAE baselines; deployed on operator data with actionable alerts; scalable across domains without labeled incidents.
AI.conclusion: Shows that a single shared model can effectively monitor large-scale heterogeneous networks; unsupervised, adaptable to context shifts; practical deployment demonstrated; potential for broader domain adaptation.

title: Faithful Extreme Image Rescaling with Learnable Reversible Transformation and Semantic Priors
summary: Most recent extreme rescaling methods struggle to preserve semantically consistent structures and produce realistic details, due to the severely ill-posed nature of low- to high-resolution mapping under scaling factors of $16\times$ or higher. To alleviate the above problems, we propose FaithEIR, a diffusion-based framework for extreme image rescaling. Inspired by singular value decomposition, we develop learnable reversible transformation that enables invertible downscaling and upscaling in the latent space. To compensate for information loss due to quantization, we propose an adaptive detail prior, a high-frequency dictionary that captures the empirical average of commonly occurring structures in the training data. Finally, we design a lightweight pixel semantic embedder to provide semantic conditioning for the pretrained diffusion model. We present extensive experimental results demonstrating that our FaithEIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity and perceptual quality. Our code, model weights, and detailed results are released at https://github.com/cshw2021/FaithEIR.

AI.tldr: FaithEIR: diffusion-based framework for extreme image rescaling (>=16x) with invertible latent-space down/upscaling inspired by SVD, adaptive detail prior, and semantic conditioning; claims superior reconstruction fidelity and perceptual quality with released code.
AI.motivation: Extreme resampling is severely ill-posed for high scaling factors; existing methods struggle to preserve semantic structures and details.
AI.method: Develop a learnable reversible transformation in latent space (SVD-inspired) enabling invertible downscaling/upscaling; introduce an adaptive detail prior (high-frequency dictionary) to compensate quantization-induced loss; implement a lightweight pixel semantic embedder to condition the diffusion model.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FaithEIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art in reconstruction fidelity and perceptual quality; code and weights released.
AI.conclusion: FaithEIR advances extreme rescaling by combining invertible latent-space transformation, data-driven detail priors, and semantic conditioning; promising for high-factor resampling.

title: Hierarchical Abstract Tree for Cross-Document Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances large language models with external knowledge, and tree-based RAG organizes documents into hierarchical indexes to support queries at multiple granularities. However, existing Tree-RAG methods designed for single-document retrieval face critical challenges in scaling to cross-document multi-hop questions: (1) poor distribution adaptability, where $k$-means clustering introduces noise due to rigid distribution assumptions; (2) structural isolation, as tree indexes lack explicit cross-document connections; and (3) coarse abstraction, which obscures fine-grained details. To address these limitations, we propose $Ψ$-RAG, a tree-RAG framework with two key components. First, a hierarchical abstract tree index built through an iterative "merging and collapse" process that adapts to data distributions without a priori assumption. Second, a multi-granular retrieval agent that intelligently interacts with the knowledge base with reorganized queries and an agent-powered hybrid retriever. $Ψ$-RAG supports diverse tasks from token-level question answering to document-level summarization. On cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, it outperforms RAPTOR by 25.9% and HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 score. Code is available at https://github.com/Newiz430/Psi-RAG.

AI.tldr: Ψ-RAG is a tree-based retrieval-augmented generation framework that builds a hierarchical abstract index via iterative merging/collapse and uses a multi-granular retrieval agent with an agent-powered hybrid retriever to scale cross-document multi-hop QA; it outperforms RAPTOR and HippoRAG 2 on benchmark F1 and is applicable from token-level QA to document-level summarization; code is released.
AI.motivation: Existing tree-based RAG methods for single-document retrieval struggle to scale to cross-document multi-hop questions due to rigid distributions (k-means noise), lack of cross-document connections, and coarse abstraction. There is a need for an adaptive, interconnected, and fine-grained retrieval mechanism.
AI.method: Introduce Ψ-RAG with (1) a hierarchical abstract tree index built via an iterative 'merging and collapse' process that adapts to data distributions without assuming a fixed distribution, and (2) a multi-granular retrieval agent that interacts with the knowledge base through reorganized queries and an agent-powered hybrid retriever.
AI.result: On cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, Ψ-RAG outperforms RAPTOR by 25.9% and HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 score.
AI.conclusion: Ψ-RAG demonstrates improved scaling and effectiveness for cross-document multi-hop QA, supporting a range of tasks from token-level QA to document-level summarization, with code available for replication.

title: CMTA: Leveraging Cross-Modal Temporal Artifacts for Generalizable AI-Generated Video Detection
summary: The proliferation of advanced AI video synthesis techniques poses an unprecedented challenge to digital video authenticity. Existing AI-generated video (AIGV) detection methods primarily focus on uni-modal or spatiotemporal artifacts, but they overlook the rich cues within the visual-textual cross-modal space, especially the temporal stability of semantic alignment. In this work, we identify a distinctive fingerprint in AIGVs, termed cross-modal temporal artifact (CMTA). Unlike real videos that exhibit natural temporal fluctuations in cross-modal alignment due to semantic variations, AIGVs display unnaturally stable semantic trajectories governed by given input prompts. To bridge this gap, we propose the CMTA framework, a cross-modal detection approach that captures these unique temporal artifacts through joint cross-modal embedding and multi-grained temporal modeling. Specifically, CMTA leverages BLIP to generate frame-level image captions and utilizes CLIP to extract corresponding visual-textual representations. A coarse-grained temporal modeling branch is then designed to characterize temporal fluctuations in cross-modal alignment with a GRU. In parallel, a fine-grained branch is constructed to capture intricate inter-frame variations from integrated visual-textual features with a Transformer encoder. Extensive experiments on 40 subsets across four large-scale datasets, including GenVideo, EvalCrafter, VideoPhy, and VidProM, validate that our approach sets a new state-of-the-art while exhibiting superior cross-generator generalization. Code and models of CMTA will be released at https://github.com/hwang-cs-ime/CMTA

AI.tldr: Introduces cross-modal temporal artifact (CMTA) as a fingerprint for AI-generated videos, detecting with a dual-branch architecture that models cross-modal temporal dynamics using BLIP captions and CLIP embeddings (GRU for coarse temporal trends and a Transformer for fine-grained inter-frame variations). Achieves state-of-the-art performance and strong cross-generator generalization on 40 subsets from GenVideo, EvalCrafter, VideoPhy, and VidProM; code released.
AI.motivation: AI video synthesis increasingly compromises digital authenticity. Existing detectors mainly target uni-modal or spatiotemporal cues and neglect cross-modal (visual-textual) alignment dynamics. Real videos exhibit natural temporal fluctuations in semantic alignment, whereas AI-generated ones can show unnaturally stable trajectories driven by prompts. Exploiting cross-modal temporal cues offers a new, robust detection signal.
AI.method: CMTA framework: (1) frame-level image captions via BLIP and corresponding visual-textual representations via CLIP; (2) coarse-grained branch with GRU to model temporal fluctuations in cross-modal alignment; (3) fine-grained branch with a Transformer encoder to capture detailed inter-frame variations from integrated visual-textual features; (4) joint cross-modal embedding and multi-grained temporal modeling for detection. Evaluation on 40 subsets across GenVideo, EvalCrafter, VideoPhy, VidProM; reported state-of-the-art performance and strong cross-generator generalization. Code and models to be released at the provided repository.
AI.result: Demonstrates that cross-modal temporal artifacts are a transferable fingerprint for detecting AIGVs. The proposed CMTA framework achieves state-of-the-art results and generalizes across multiple generators on large-scale benchmarks, indicating robustness of the cross-modal temporal cue and the effectiveness of the dual-branch temporal modeling.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal temporal dynamics offer a promising avenue for robust AIGV detection. The CMTA approach, combining BLIP-generated captions, CLIP representations, and hierarchical temporal modeling, effectively distinguishes AI-generated videos from real ones and generalizes across generators. The work paves the way for future exploration of cross-modal temporal fingerprints and multi-branch architectures in deepfake detection.

title: Group Cognition Learning: Making Everything Better Through Governed Two-Stage Agents Collaboration
comment: This study has been Accepted by ICML 2026. The current version is a manuscript, please refer to the official version released at ICML 2026 for the final published version
summary: Centralized multimodal learning commonly compresses language, acoustic, and visual signals into a single fused representation for prediction. While effective, this paradigm suffers from two limitations: modality dominance, where optimization gravitates towards the path of least resistance, ignoring weaker but informative modalities, and spurious modality coupling, where models overfit to incidental cross-modal correlations. To address these, we propose Group Cognition Learning (GCL), a governed collaboration paradigm that applies a two-stage protocol after modality-specific encoding. In Stage 1 (Selective Interaction), a Routing Agent proposes directed interaction routes, and an Auditing Agent assigns sample-wise gates to emphasize exchanges that yield positive marginal predictive gain while suppressing redundant coupling. In Stage 2 (Consensus Formation), a Public-Factor Agent maintains an explicit shared factor, and an Aggregation Agent produces the final prediction through contribution-aware weighting while keeping each modality representation as a specialization channel. Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec demonstrate that GCL mitigates dominance and coupling, establishing state-of-the-art results across both regression and classification benchmarks. Analysis experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of the design.

AI.tldr: A two-stage governance framework (GCL) for multimodal learning that mitigates modality dominance and spurious cross-modal coupling via routing/auditing in Stage 1 and consensus/shared-factor with contribution-aware aggregation in Stage 2, achieving state-of-the-art results on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec for both regression and classification.
AI.motivation: Centralized multimodal fusion often emphasizes dominant modalities and learns incidental cross-modal correlations. A governance mechanism is needed to regulate cross-modal interactions, preserve modality-specific information, and improve generalization.
AI.method: Stage 1 Selective Interaction: a Routing Agent proposes directed interaction routes; an Auditing Agent assigns sample-wise gates to emphasize interactions with positive marginal predictive gain and suppress redundant coupling. Stage 2 Consensus Formation: a Public-Factor Agent maintains an explicit shared factor; an Aggregation Agent produces the final prediction with contribution-aware weighting while keeping each modality representation as a specialization channel.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec show reduced modality dominance and spurious coupling, achieving state-of-the-art results across regression and classification benchmarks. Analyses validate the effectiveness of the proposed governance design.
AI.conclusion: GCL introduces a governance-based two-stage collaboration paradigm that balances cross-modal interactions and modality specialization, yielding robust, interpretable improvements and state-of-the-art performance in multimodal learning.

title: Beyond Continuity: Simulation-free Reconstruction of Discrete Branching Dynamics from Single-cell Snapshots
summary: Inferring cellular trajectories from destructive snapshots is complicated by the challenges of stochasticity and non-conservative mass dynamics such as cell proliferation and apoptosis. Existing unbalanced Optimal Transport (OT) methods treat mass as a continuous fluid, performing inference at the population level. However, this macroscopic view often fails to capture the discrete, jump-like nature of birth-death events at single-cell resolution, which is essential for understanding lineage branching and fate decisions. We present Unbalanced Schrödinger Bridge (USB), a simulation-free framework for learning underlying dynamics that effectively integrates both stochastic and unbalanced effects which also models the discrete, jump-like birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution. Theoretically, USB provides a tractable solution to the Branching Schrödinger Bridge (BSB) problem, offering a rigorous microscopic interpretation where individual cells undergo both Brownian motion and discrete birth-death jumps. Technically, the method implements an efficient solver by introducing a simulation-free training objective that effectively scales to high-dimensional omics data. Empirically, we demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that USB not only achieves trajectory reconstruction performance better than or comparable to deterministic baselines but also uniquely enables realistic discrete simulation of birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution.

AI.tldr: Unbalanced Schrödinger Bridge (USB) provides a simulation-free, microscopic framework for single-cell trajectory inference that jointly models stochastic diffusion and discrete birth-death events, addressing limitations of population-level unbalanced OT. It achieves competitive trajectory reconstruction and enables realistic birth-death simulations at single-cell resolution.
AI.motivation: Existing unbalanced OT approaches treat mass as a continuous fluid at the population level, failing to capture discrete birth-death events that drive lineage branching and fate decisions in single cells.
AI.method: USB formulates a Branching Schrödinger Bridge problem and provides a simulation-free training objective that integrates Brownian dynamics with discrete birth-death jumps, scalable to high-dimensional single-cell omics data and enabling microscopic interpretation of cell lineage dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on simulated and real datasets shows USB achieves trajectory reconstruction performance better than or comparable to deterministic baselines and uniquely enables realistic discrete birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution.
AI.conclusion: USB offers a tractable, microscopic, and scalable framework for learning birth-death–influenced cellular trajectories, bridging stochastic and unbalanced dynamics and facilitating realistic single-cell lineage simulations.

title: BlenderRAG: High-Fidelity 3D Object Generation via Retrieval-Augmented Code Synthesis
summary: Automatic generation of executable Blender code from natural language remains challenging, with state-of-the-art LLMs producing frequent syntactic errors and geometrically inconsistent objects. We present BlenderRAG, a retrieval-augmented generation system that operates on a curated multimodal dataset of 500 expert-validated examples (text, code, image) across 50 object categories. By retrieving semantically similar examples during generation, BlenderRAG improves compilation success rates from 40.8% to 70.0% and semantic normalized alignment from 0.41 to 0.77 (CLIP similarity) across four state-of-the-art LLMs, without requiring fine-tuning or specialized hardware, making it immediately accessible for deployment. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/MaxRondelli/BlenderRAG.

AI.tldr: BlenderRAG uses retrieval-augmented generation over a multimodal dataset to improve automatic generation of executable Blender code from natural language, boosting both compilation success and semantic alignment without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address the instability and geometric inconsistency of NL→Blender code produced by LLMs by grounding generation with semantically similar, expert-validated exemplars; leverage a multimodal dataset to improve reliability and alignment.
AI.method: A retrieval-augmented generation system that operates on a curated dataset of 500 expert-validated examples (text, code, image) across 50 object categories. During generation, semantically similar examples are retrieved and used to ground the LLM's outputs. Evaluation across four state-of-the-art LLMs shows improved compilation success and CLIP-based alignment without model fine-tuning or specialized hardware.
AI.result: Compilation success increases from 40.8% to 70.0%. Semantic alignment (CLIP similarity) increases from 0.41 to 0.77 across four LLMs. The approach does not require fine-tuning or specialized hardware, enabling immediate deployment.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented generation with a curated multimodal dataset effectively improves executable code generation for Blender NL prompts, enhancing both correctness and semantic alignment; the dataset and code are released for reproducibility and wider adoption.

title: Advancing Edge Classification through High-Dimensional Causal Modeling of Node-Edge Interplay
summary: Edge classification, a crucial task for graph applications, remains relatively under-explored compared to link prediction. Current methods often overlook the potential causal influences of node features on edge features, leading to a loss of relevant prior information. In this work, we present an empirical exploration using the Causal Edge Classification Framework (CECF). Unlike conventional causal inference methods, CECF is the first framework to apply causal inference principles to the edge classification task and to explore modeling edge features as a high-dimensional treatment within a causal framework. Based on the node embedding of Graph Neural Network (GNN), CECF seeks to learn a balanced representation of high-dimensional edge features by mitigating the potential influence of node features. Then, a cross-attention network captures the complex dependencies between node and edge features for final edge classification.Extensive experiments demonstrate that CECF not only achieves superior performance but also serves as a flexible, plug-and-play enhancement for existing methods.We also provide empirical analyses, offering insights into when and how this high-dimensional causal modeling framework works for the edge classification.

AI.tldr: CECF introduces a causal-edge-classification framework that treats edge features as high-dimensional treatments, learns balanced node-edge representations from GNN embeddings, and uses cross-attention to model node–edge dependencies, yielding improved performance and plug-and-play adaptability for existing methods.
AI.motivation: Edge classification has been relatively under-explored and may be affected by node features; incorporating causal reasoning about how node features influence edge features can preserve relevant prior information and potentially improve performance.
AI.method: Propose CECF, a causal inference-based framework that treats edge features as a high-dimensional treatment. It learns a balanced representation of edge features by mitigating node-feature influence using node embeddings from a GNN, and employs a cross-attention network to capture complex node–edge dependencies for final classification.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that CECF achieves superior performance and can serve as a flexible, plug-and-play enhancement for existing edge classification methods. Empirical analyses provide insights into when and how high-dimensional causal modeling benefits edge classification.
AI.conclusion: CECF offers a novel application of causal inference to edge classification, enabling better handling of high-dimensional edge features, improving performance, and providing practical guidance for integrating causal modeling with existing approaches.

title: UniVidX: A Unified Multimodal Framework for Versatile Video Generation via Diffusion Priors
comment: Project page: https://houyuanchen111.github.io/UniVidX.github.io/ Accepted to ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 2026)
summary: Recent progress has shown that video diffusion models (VDMs) can be repurposed for diverse multimodal graphics tasks. However, existing methods often train separate models for each problem setting, which fixes the input-output mapping and limits the modeling of correlations across modalities. We present UniVidX, a unified multimodal framework that leverages VDM priors for versatile video generation. UniVidX formulates pixel-aligned tasks as conditional generation in a shared multimodal space, adapts to modality-specific distributions while preserving the backbone's native priors, and promotes cross-modal consistency during synthesis. It is built on three key designs. Stochastic Condition Masking (SCM) randomly partitions modalities into clean conditions and noisy targets during training, enabling omni-directional conditional generation instead of fixed mappings. Decoupled Gated LoRA (DGL) introduces per-modality LoRAs that are activated when a modality serves as the generation target, preserving the strong priors of the VDM. Cross-Modal Self-Attention (CMSA) shares keys and values across modalities while keeping modality-specific queries, facilitating information exchange and inter-modal alignment. We instantiate UniVidX in two domains: UniVid-Intrinsic, for RGB videos and intrinsic maps including albedo, irradiance, and normal; and UniVid-Alpha, for blended RGB videos and their constituent RGBA layers. Experiments show that both models achieve performance competitive with state-of-the-art methods across distinct tasks and generalize robustly to in-the-wild scenarios, even when trained on fewer than 1,000 videos. Project page: https://houyuanchen111.github.io/UniVidX.github.io/

AI.tldr: UniVidX is a unified multimodal video diffusion framework that enables omni-directional conditional generation across multiple modalities by using SCM, DGL, and CMSA, instantiated as UniVid-Intrinsic and UniVid-Alpha, achieving competitive results and robust generalization with limited data.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal video generation methods train separate models for each input-output mapping, which restricts cross-modal correlations and scalability. A unified framework that preserves diffusion priors while enabling cross-modal conditioning is desirable.
AI.method: Three core designs: (1) Stochastic Condition Masking (SCM) partitions modalities into clean conditionals and noisy targets during training to enable flexible, omni-directional conditioning; (2) Decoupled Gated LoRA (DGL) provides per-modality LoRA adapters activated when a modality is the generation target, preserving VDM priors; (3) Cross-Modal Self-Attention (CMSA) shares keys/values across modalities while maintaining modality-specific queries to facilitate cross-modal exchange and alignment. Implementations include UniVid-Intrinsic (RGB videos + intrinsic maps) and UniVid-Alpha (RGB with RGBA layers).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows UniVidX achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art methods across diverse tasks, with strong cross-modal consistency and robust generalization to in-the-wild data, even when trained on fewer than 1,000 videos.
AI.conclusion: UniVidX provides a versatile, scalable framework for unified multimodal video generation that preserves diffusion priors, enables omni-directional conditioning, and supports cross-modal information exchange, with two concrete instantiations and strong empirical results.

title: ResRL: Boosting LLM Reasoning via Negative Sample Projection Residual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) enhances reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) but usually exhibits limited generation diversity due to the over-incentivization of positive rewards. Although methods like Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) mitigate this issue by upweighting penalty from negative samples, they may suppress the semantic distributions shared between positive and negative responses. To boost reasoning ability without losing diversity, this paper proposes negative sample projection Residual Reinforcement Learning (ResRL) that decouples similar semantic distributions among positive and negative responses. We theoretically link Lazy Likelihood Displacement (LLD) to negative-positive head-gradient interference and derive a single-forward proxy that upper-bounds representation alignment to guide conservative advantage reweighting. ResRL then projects negative-token hidden representations onto an SVD-based low-rank positive subspace and uses projection residuals to modulate negative gradients, improving reasoning while preserving diversity and outperforming strong baselines on average across twelve benchmarks spanning Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, and Function Calling. Notably, ResRL surpasses NSR on mathematical reasoning by 9.4\% in Avg@16 and 7.0\% in Pass@128. Code is available at https://github.com/1229095296/ResRL.git.

AI.tldr: ResRL introduces negative-sample projection with an SVD-based low-rank positive subspace to decouple similar semantic distributions of positive and negative responses, improving reasoning while preserving diversity and surpassing NSR on math and across 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) often overfits to positive rewards, reducing generation diversity. Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) mitigates this but can erode shared semantic distributions between positive and negative outputs. A method that preserves diversity while boosting reasoning is needed.
AI.method: ResRL projects negative-token hidden representations onto an SVD-based low-rank positive subspace and uses the projection residuals to adjust negative gradients. The approach is grounded by a theoretical link between Lazy Likelihood Displacement (LLD) and negative–positive head-gradient interference and a derived single-forward proxy to upper-bound representation alignment, guiding conservative advantage reweighting.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines on twelve benchmarks spanning Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, and Function Calling. Notably, ResRL surpasses NSR on mathematical reasoning by 9.4 percentage points in Avg@16 and 7.0 percentage points in Pass@128.
AI.conclusion: ResRL achieves higher reasoning performance while preserving generation diversity, offering a practical improvement over existing negative-sample methods. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Fairness of Classifiers in the Presence of Constraints between Features
comment: To be published in Proc. CP 2026
summary:
In Machine Learning, an accepted definition of fairness of a decision taken by a classifier is that it should not depend on protected features, such as gender. Unfortunately, when constraints exist between features, such dependencies can be obscured by the constraints.
  To avoid this problem, we propose that a decision be considered fair if it has a fair explanation. We define a fair explanation as a prime-implicant reason for the decision that does not contain any protected feature (where the constraints are taken into account in the definition of prime-implicant). Surprisingly, ignoring constraints can completely change the fairness of a decision (according to this definition) even in the absence of constraints between protected and unprotected features.
  Three possible definitions of fairness of a classifier are that for all its decisions (1) there are only fair explanations, (2) there is at least one fair explanation, or (3) changing protected features does not change the outcome. We identify the relationships between these different definitions of fairness and study the computational complexity of testing fairness of classifiers.

AI.tldr: Proposes fairness via fair explanations (prime-implicant) excluding protected features under constraints; analyzes three fairness notions and the complexity of testing them.
AI.motivation: Standard fairness definitions can be misled by feature dependencies and constraints. To obtain robust fairness, decide if a decision admits a fair explanation that excludes protected features while accounting for feature constraints.
AI.method: Define a fair explanation as a prime implicant for the decision that omits protected features, with constraints embedded in prime-implicant generation. Formalize three fairness notions: (1) all decisions have only fair explanations, (2) at least one fair explanation per decision, (3) changing protected features does not change the outcome. Explore relationships among them and study the computational complexity of testing fairness.
AI.result: The work identifies relationships among the three fairness definitions and provides complexity analyses for testing classifier fairness under these definitions.
AI.conclusion: Constraint-aware, prime-implicant–based explanations offer a robust fairness criterion; however, evaluating fairness under these notions can be computationally challenging, depending on the definition used.

title: InpaintSLat: Inpainting Structured 3D Latents via Initial Noise Optimization
summary: We present a training-free approach for controllable 3D inpainting based on initial noise optimization. In the structured 3D latent diffusion framework, we observe that the underlying geometric structure is established during the early stages of the diffusion process and exhibits high sensitivity to the initial noise. Such characteristics compromise stability in tasks like inpainting and editing, where the model must ensure strict alignment with the existing context while synthesizing a new structure. In this paper, we introduce a strategy to optimize the initial noise within the structured 3D latent diffusion framework, ensuring high-fidelity 3D inpainting. Specifically, we update the initial noise by leveraging a backpropagation approximation grounded in the rectified flow model, with the spectral parameterization specially designed for robust and efficient structured 3D latent optimization. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in contextual consistency and prompt alignment over representative training-free inpainting baselines, establishing initial noise control as an independent dimension for 3D inpainting, orthogonal to conventional sampling trajectory manipulation.

AI.tldr: A training-free method for 3D inpainting that optimizes the initial noise in a structured 3D latent diffusion model to improve fidelity and alignment with context, using a backpropagation approximation from a rectified flow model and a spectral parameterization.
AI.motivation: Address instability and misalignment in 3D inpainting caused by sensitivity of early diffusion stages to initial noise, by introducing an optimization over the initial noise without additional training.
AI.method: Optimize the initial noise via backpropagation-approximate updates inspired by rectified flow; employ spectral parameterization to enable robust and efficient structured 3D latent optimization within a diffusion framework.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent gains in contextual consistency and prompt alignment over training-free baselines; positions initial-noise control as a distinct axis for 3D inpainting, orthogonal to sampling-trajectory manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Initial noise control is a separate, orthogonal dimension for 3D inpainting within structured diffusion, enabling high-fidelity context-consistent results without training.

title: PILIR: Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks have become a powerful mesh-free method for solving partial differential equations, but their performance is often limited by spectral bias. Specifically, in standard MLPs used in PINNs, the global parameter coupling causes the model to prioritize learning low-frequency components, resulting in slow convergence for high-frequency details. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation (PILIR). Our approach separates the global physical domain into a discrete latent feature space and a continuous generative decoder. By using a learnable grid to encode explicit spatial locality, PILIR can capture high-frequency details locally, preventing dilution by global patterns. A generative neural operator then synthesizes these local latent features into continuous physical fields, allowing accurate reconstruction of fine-scale structures. Experiments on a range of challenging PDEs show that PILIR effectively mitigates spectral bias, thereby boosting the convergence of high-frequency details and achieving superior accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: PILIR tackles spectral bias in PINNs by separating the global domain into a discrete latent grid and a continuous generative decoder, enabling local high-frequency detail capture and improved convergence.
AI.motivation: Standard PINN-MLPs suffer from spectral bias, prioritizing low-frequency learning which hampers high-frequency PDE details. PILIR aims to decouple locality and global structure to preserve and reproduce fine-scale features.
AI.method: Split domain into a learnable spatial grid producing local latent features. Use a continuous generative decoder and a generative neural operator to synthesize local latents into fields. Physics-informed training guides the model to respect PDE constraints while mitigating global coupling that causes spectral bias.
AI.result: Experiments across challenging PDEs show reduced spectral bias, faster convergence for high-frequency details, and superior accuracy versus state-of-the-art PINN approaches.
AI.conclusion: PILIR effectively mitigates spectral bias in PINNs by leveraging local latent representations and a generative decoder, enabling accurate reconstruction of fine-scale PDE features and improved overall performance.

title: Possibilistic Predictive Uncertainty for Deep Learning
summary: Deep neural networks achieve impressive results across diverse applications, yet their overconfidence on unseen inputs necessitates reliable epistemic uncertainty modelling. Existing methods for uncertainty modelling face a fundamental dilemma: Bayesian approaches provide principled estimates but remain computationally prohibitive, while efficient second-order predictors lack rigorous derivations connecting their specific objectives to epistemic uncertainty quantification. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce Dirichlet-approximated possibilistic posterior predictions (DAPPr), a principled framework leveraging possibility theory. We define a possibilistic posterior over parameters, projects this posterior to the prediction space via supremum operators, and approximates the projected posterior using learnable Dirichlet possibility functions. This projection-and-approximation strategy yields a simple training objective with closed-form solutions. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive or superior uncertainty quantification performance compared to state-of-the-art evidential deep learning methods while maintaining both principled derivation and computational efficiency. Code will be available at https://github.com/MaxwellYaoNi/DAPPr.

AI.tldr: A principled, efficient uncertainty quantification framework (DAPPr) that uses a Dirichlet-possibilistic posterior to project parameter uncertainty into predictions via a supremum projection, achieving strong epistemic uncertainty estimates with a tractable objective.
AI.motivation: To resolve the dichotomy between Bayesian methods (principled but computationally heavy) and efficient second-order predictors (fast but lacking rigorous epistemic grounding) by embedding uncertainty in a possibilistic framework and rendering it tractable via Dirichlet approximations.
AI.method: Define a possibilistic posterior over model parameters, project it to prediction space with supremum operators, and approximate the projected posterior using learnable Dirichlet possibility functions. The resulting objective is simple and admits closed-form solutions.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse benchmarks show competitive or superior uncertainty quantification compared with state-of-the-art evidential deep learning methods, while maintaining principled derivation and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: DAPPr offers a principled, efficient pathway for epistemic uncertainty quantification in deep nets by marrying possibility theory with Dirichlet-based approximations, with promising scalability and applicability to real-world uncertainty estimation tasks.

title: Prediction of Alzheimer's Disease Risk Factors from Retinal Images via Deep Learning: Development and Validation of Biologically Relevant Morphological Associations in the UK Biobank
comment: Accepted to the "Journal of Alzheimer's Disease" for publication
summary: The systemic, metabolic, lifestyle factors have established associations with Alzheimer's Disease (AD) through epidemiologic and AD-specific biomarker studies. Whether colored fundus photography (CFP) contains retinal structural signatures corresponding to these AD-related risk domains remains unclear. To determine whether deep learning (DL) models can predict 12 AD-related risk factors from CFP and to characterize the retinal structures underlying these predictions, thereby assessing whether CFP reflects pathways to AD vulnerability. Using UK Biobank CFPs, DL models were trained using 62,876 images from 44,501 unique participants to predict 12 factors linked to AD incidence: 6 categorical (sex, smoking, sleeplessness, economic status, alcohol use, depression) and 6 continuous (age, age at completing education, BMI, systolic, diastolic blood pressure, HbA1c). Model performance, model saliency, and saliency-derived scores (CAM-Score) were evaluated and compared to retinal morphometry. The scores were also compared between incident-AD cases (average 8.55 years before onset) and matched controls. Performance of DL ranged from AUROC= 0.5654-0.9480 for categorical and R2=-0.0291-0.7620 for continuous factors, outperforming most of the morphometry-machine learning models. Saliency-based score consistently highlighted biologically meaningful regions, particularly the optic nerve head and retinal vasculature. It also aligned with present morphometric variations. Several saliency-based scores differed significantly between incident AD and matched controls, suggesting potential overlap between retinal correlates of risk factors and preclinical AD-associated changes. CFP encodes retinal signatures linked to AD risk factors. Although not diagnostic, DL-derived retinal representations may uncover biologically meaningful risk-related structural changes mirroring the potential AD vulnerability.

AI.tldr: DL models on colored fundus photographs predict 12 AD-related risk factors; performance varies, saliency highlights optic nerve head and vasculature; some signals differ between preclinical AD cases and controls; not diagnostic but may reflect AD vulnerability.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether CFP carries retinal structural signatures linked to AD-related risk domains and whether DL-derived retinal representations reflect pathways to AD vulnerability.
AI.method: Train DL models on 62,876 CFP images from 44,501 participants (UK Biobank) to predict 12 factors (6 categorical: sex, smoking, sleeplessness, economic status, alcohol use, depression; 6 continuous: age, education age, BMI, systolic/diastolic BP, HbA1c). Evaluate model performance (AUROC for categorical; R2 for continuous), saliency (CAM-Score), and compare to retinal morphometry. Compare saliency-derived scores between incident AD cases (avg 8.55 years to onset) and matched controls.
AI.result: DL performance ranged AUROC 0.5654–0.9480 for categorical factors and R2 −0.0291–0.7620 for continuous factors, outperforming most morphometry-based models. Saliency highlighted biologically meaningful regions, notably optic nerve head and retinal vasculature, aligning with morphometric variations. Several saliency-based scores differed significantly between incident AD cases and controls, suggesting overlap between retinal correlates of risk factors and preclinical AD changes. CFP encodes retinal signatures linked to AD risk factors; DL-derived retinal representations may reveal biologically meaningful risk-related structural changes related to AD vulnerability, though not diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: CFP contains retinal signatures associated with AD risk factors; DL-derived representations may reflect pathways to AD vulnerability and could aid risk stratification, but are not diagnostic for AD.

title: Towards Robust and Scalable Density-based Clustering via Graph Propagation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.02989
summary: We present \textit{CluProp}, a novel framework that reimagines varied-density clustering in high-dimensional spaces as a label propagation process over neighborhood graphs. Our approach formally bridges the gap between density-based clustering and graph connectivity, leveraging efficient propagation mechanisms from network science to mitigate the parameter sensitivity inherent in traditional density-based methods. Specifically, we introduce a deterministic density-based propagation strategy to ensure scalable neighborhood identification. The framework is agnostic to the choice of distance metric and exhibits superior performance on large-scale data, processing millions of points in minutes while consistently outperforming existing baselines in accuracy.

AI.tldr: CluProp reframes variable-density clustering as label propagation on neighborhood graphs, achieving parameter-insensitive, scalable, metric-agnostic clustering with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Density-based clustering in high dimensions suffers from parameter sensitivity and scalability; graph-based propagation can capture connectivity while reducing tuning; a framework that combines density cues with efficient propagation is desirable.
AI.method: Deterministic density-based propagation over a neighborhood graph; uses label propagation mechanisms from network science; framework is agnostic to the distance metric and supports scalable neighborhood identification.
AI.result: Scales to millions of points in minutes and consistently outperforms baselines in accuracy on large-scale data.
AI.conclusion: CluProp provides a principled bridge between density-based clustering and graph connectivity, improving robustness and scalability for high-dimensional clustering.
