title: DMDSC: A Dynamic-Margin Deep Simplex Classifier for Open-Set Recognition on Medical Image Datasets
summary:
Medical imaging datasets are often characterized by extreme class imbalances, where rare pathologies are significantly underrepresented compared to common conditions. This imbalance poses a dual challenge for Open-Set Recognition (OSR): models must maintain high classification accuracy on known classes while reliably rejecting unknown samples unseen during training in the clinical settings. While recently proposed Deep Simplex Classifier (DSC)~\cite{cevikalp2024reaching} and UnCertainty-aware Deep Simplex Classifier (UCDSC)~\cite{Aditya_2026_WACV} successfully leverage Neural Collapse to ensure maximal inter-class separation, they rely on a uniform margin that does not account for the varying densities of medical classes.
  In this paper, we propose DMDSC an enhanced framework featuring a dynamic margin approach. Our approach automatically adapts class-specific margins based on label frequency, enforcing a higher penalty and tighter feature clustering for rare pathologies to counteract the effects of data imbalance. Extensive experiments conducted on diverse medical benchmarks on BloodMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, OCTMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, DermaMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, and BreaKHis~\cite{spanhol2015dataset} datasets, demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DMDSC introduces a dynamic, class-aware margin for Neural Collapse-based OSR in imbalanced medical datasets, improving known-class accuracy and unknown rejection by prioritizing rare pathologies.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging often exhibits extreme class imbalance, causing uniform-margin OSR methods to underperform on rare pathologies; there is a need for class-specific margins that adapt to label frequencies to improve open-set recognition.
AI.method: Integrates a dynamic, class-conditioned margin into the Deep Simplex Classifier framework, adjusting margins based on label frequency to enforce tighter clustering for rare classes and better separation across the simplex, leveraging Neural Collapse principles.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on BloodMNIST, OCTMNIST, DermaMNIST, and BreaKHis shows that DMDSC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both known-class accuracy and open-set rejection, across diverse medical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, data-driven margins effectively counteract imbalance in medical OSR, improving robustness and generalization; the approach appears broadly applicable to other imbalanced domains beyond the tested medical datasets.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Double Oracle Efficiency in Policy Optimization and Offline Estimation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) in large environments often suffers from severe computational bottlenecks, as conventional regret minimization algorithms require repeated, costly calls to planning and statistical estimation oracles. While recent advances have explored offline oracle-efficient algorithms, their computational complexity typically scales with the cardinality of the state and action spaces, rendering them intractable for large-scale or continuous environments. In this paper, we address this fundamental limitation by studying offline oracle-efficient episodic RL through the lens of log-barrier and log-determinant regularization. Specifically, for tabular Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), we propose a novel algorithm that achieves the optimal $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound while requiring only $O(H\log\log T)$ calls to both the offline statistical estimation and planning oracles when $T$ is known and $O(H\log T)$ calls when $T$ is unknown. Crucially, this oracle complexity is entirely independent of the size of the state and action spaces. This strict independence drastically reduces the planning oracle complexity, representing a substantial improvement over existing offline oracle-efficient algorithms (Qian et al., 2024). Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of our framework by generalizing the algorithm to linear MDPs featuring infinite state spaces and arbitrary action spaces. We prove that this generalized approach successfully attains meaningful sub-linear regret. Consequently, our work yields the first doubly oracle-efficient (i.e., efficient with respect to both statistical estimation and policy optimization) regret minimization algorithm capable of solving MDPs with infinite state and action spaces, significantly expanding the boundaries of computationally tractable RL.

AI.tldr: Proposes a doubly oracle-efficient offline episodic RL algorithm for tabular and linear MDPs using log-barrier and log-determinant regularization. Achieves optimal sqrt(T) regret with oracle calls independent of state/action space size for tabular MDPs, and extends to linear MDPs with infinite spaces, achieving sublinear regret.
AI.motivation: Address computational bottlenecks in offline oracle-efficient RL where planning and estimation costs scale with large or continuous state/action spaces. Aim to decouple oracle complexity from environment size while preserving regret guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce log-barrier and log-determinant regularization within an offline oracle-efficient RL framework. Develop a tabular MDP algorithm achieving O~(sqrt(T)) regret with O(H log log T) (known T) or O(H log T) (unknown T) oracle calls, independent of state/action cardinality. Generalize to linear MDPs with infinite spaces while maintaining sublinear regret.
AI.result: First doubly oracle-efficient regret minimization algorithm for MDPs with infinite state/action spaces. For tabular MDPs: regret O~(sqrt(T)) with oracle calls independent of S,A; for linear MDPs: sublinear regret with similar oracle-efficiency guarantees.
AI.conclusion: This work substantially expands the computational tractability frontier for offline RL by achieving oracle-efficiency w.r.t. both estimation and planning, even in infinite-dimensional settings, and improves upon prior offline oracle-efficient methods that depend on state/action cardinality.

title: Learning Multimodal Energy-Based Model with Multimodal Variational Auto-Encoder via MCMC Revision
summary: Energy-based models (EBMs) are a flexible class of deep generative models and are well-suited to capture complex dependencies in multimodal data. However, learning multimodal EBM by maximum likelihood requires Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling in the joint data space, where noise-initialized Langevin dynamics often mixes poorly and fails to discover coherent inter-modal relationships. Multimodal VAEs have made progress in capturing such inter-modal dependencies by introducing a shared latent generator and a joint inference model. However, both the shared latent generator and joint inference model are parameterized as unimodal Gaussian (or Laplace), which severely limits their ability to approximate the complex structure induced by multimodal data. In this work, we study the learning problem of the multimodal EBM, shared latent generator, and joint inference model. We present a learning framework that effectively interweaves their MLE updates with corresponding MCMC refinements in both the data and latent spaces. Specifically, the generator is learned to produce coherent multimodal samples that serve as strong initial states for EBM sampling, while the inference model is learned to provide informative latent initializations for generator posterior sampling. Together, these two models serve as complementary models that enable effective EBM sampling and learning, yielding realistic and coherent multimodal EBM samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance for multimodal synthesis quality and coherence compared to various baselines. We conduct various analyses and ablation studies to validate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed multimodal framework.

AI.tldr: Interleaves MLE-based EBM learning with MCMC refinements in data and latent spaces by training a shared latent generator and a joint inference model to produce coherent multimodal samples, improving synthesis quality and coherence.
AI.motivation: To overcome MCMC mixing problems in training multimodal EBMs and the expressivity limits of unimodal priors in existing multimodal VAEs.
AI.method: Co-train EBM, shared latent generator, and joint inference model by alternating MLE updates with data- and latent-space MCMC refinements; generator provides strong initial states for EBM sampling while the inference model provides informative latent initializations for posterior sampling; these components are learned jointly as complementary models.
AI.result: Empirical results show superior multimodal synthesis quality and coherence across baselines; ablations validate the contributions and scalability.
AI.conclusion: A unified, scalable framework yields realistic and coherent multimodal EBM samples by effectively coupling data-space and latent-space inference with generation.

title: Foundation AI Models for Aerosol Optical Depth Estimation from PACE Satellite Data
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, to appear in 2026 IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
summary: Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD) retrieval is essential for Earth observation, supporting applications from air quality monitoring to climate studies. Conventional physics-based AOD retrieval methods formulate the problem as a pixel-wise inversion, relying on radiative transfer modeling, memory-intensive look-up tables, and auxiliary meteorological data. While recent data-driven approaches have shown promise, many fail to exploit the spatial-spectral coherence of hyperspectral imagery, leading to spatially inconsistent and noise-sensitive retrievals. We present the first study exploring Foundation AI models for AOD retrieval and propose ViTCG, a Vision Transformer with Channel-wise Grouping-based spatial regression framework that reduces retrieval bias and error. ViTCG uses hyperspectral top-of-atmosphere radiance as input and jointly models spatial context and spectral information. Validation with PACE radiance observations demonstrates a 62% reduction in mean squared error compared to state-of-the-art foundation models, including Prithvi, and produces spatially coherent AOD fields.

AI.tldr: ViTCG: A Vision Transformer with channel-wise grouping for AOD retrieval from hyperspectral radiance; achieves ~62% reduction in MSE vs state-of-the-art foundation models and yields spatially coherent AOD fields.
AI.motivation: Across traditional physics-based AOD retrieval, pixel-wise inversions rely on radiative transfer models and LUTs, which are memory-intensive and may be biased; recent data-driven approaches often miss spatial-spectral coherence, resulting in noisy, spatially inconsistent AOD maps. The study investigates Foundation AI models for AOD and aims to exploit spatial-spectral coherence to improve accuracy and coherence.
AI.method: Introduce ViTCG, a Vision Transformer with channel-wise grouping-based spatial regression. It takes hyperspectral top-of-atmosphere radiance as input and jointly models spatial context and spectral information to retrieve AOD. Validation uses PACE radiance observations as ground truth.
AI.result: ViTCG achieves a 62% reduction in mean squared error compared with state-of-the-art foundation models, including Prithvi, and produces spatially coherent AOD fields.
AI.conclusion: Foundation AI models like ViTCG can significantly improve AOD retrieval accuracy and spatial coherence, supporting improved air quality monitoring and climate studies.

title: Mesh Field Theory: Port-Hamiltonian Formulation of Mesh-Based Physics
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures, 10 tables. Accepted to ICML 2026
summary: We present Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) and its neural realization, MeshFT-Net: a structure-preserving framework for mesh-based continuum physics that cleanly separates the physics' topological structure from its metric structure. Imposing minimal physical principles (locality, permutation equivariance, orientation covariance, and energy balance/dissipation inequality), we prove a reduction theorem for mesh-based physics. Under these conditions, the physical dynamics admit a local factorization into a port-Hamiltonian form: the conservative interconnection is fixed uniquely by mesh topology, whereas metric effects enter only through constitutive relations and dissipation. This reduction clarifies what must be fixed and what should be learned, directly informing MeshFT-Net's design. Across evaluations on analytic and realistic datasets, physics-consistency tests, and out-of-distribution validation, MeshFT-Net achieves near-zero energy drift and strong physical fidelity (correct dispersion and momentum conservation) along with robust extrapolation and high data efficiency. By eliminating non-physical degrees of freedom and learning only metric-dependent structure, MeshFT provides a principled inductive bias for stable, faithful, and data-efficient learning-based physical simulation.

AI.tldr: MeshFT and MeshFT-Net propose a structure-preserving, topology-aware framework for mesh-based continuum physics, yielding a port-Hamiltonian factorization where topology fixes the conservative interconnection and learning handles metric-dependent constitutive relations, achieving energy stability and high data efficiency.
AI.motivation: To disentangle topological structure from metric structure in mesh-based physics, enabling stable, faithful, and data-efficient learning by providing a principled inductive bias.
AI.method: Impose minimal physical priors (locality, permutation equivariance, orientation covariance, energy balance/dissipation). Prove a local factorization of dynamics into a port-Hamiltonian form with topology-fixed conservative interconnection and metric-entering constitutive/dissipation terms. Implement MeshFT-Net to learn metric-dependent structure.
AI.result: Near-zero energy drift; accurate dispersion and momentum conservation; strong extrapolation and data efficiency; robust physics-consistency and OOD validation.
AI.conclusion: Offers a principled, inductive-bias-driven approach for stable, faithful learning-based simulation by separating topology-fixed interconnection from learnable metric effects.

title: AdaMeZO: Adam-style Zeroth-Order Optimizer for LLM Fine-tuning Without Maintaining the Moments
summary: Fine-tuning LLMs is necessary for various dedicated downstream tasks, but classic backpropagation-based fine-tuning methods require substantial GPU memory. To this end, a recent work, MeZO, which relies solely on forward passes to fine-tune LLMs, significantly reduces GPU requirements at the cost of slower convergence due to its indifference to loss landscapes. Standard solutions, such as Adam, explore loss landscapes by estimating the first- and second-order moments and storing them in memory to guide the model's movement through dimensions with lower curvature and vice versa. However, directly applying Adam negates MeZO's advantage as it will triple the memory requirement. In light of this, we propose AdaMeZO, a zeroth-order optimizer that leverages Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates without maintaining them in memory. We present a theoretical analysis of AdaMeZO, corroborated by extensive experiments demonstrating AdaMeZO's performance, showing that AdaMeZO can outperform MeZO while requiring up to $70\%$ fewer forward passes. Trajectory visualizations affirm AdaMeZO's ability to adapt to diverse loss landscapes.

AI.tldr: A memory-efficient zeroth-order optimizer AdaMeZO improves fine-tuning of LLMs by using Adam-style moment estimates without maintaining them, achieving faster convergence than MeZO with fewer forward passes.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs is memory-hungry; MeZO reduces memory but converges slowly; a method that exploits curvature information without large memory overhead is needed.
AI.method: AdaMeZO uses zeroth-order optimization with Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates but does not store full moment vectors; leverages forward-only training and on-the-fly moment updates to guide updates; includes theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis supports the approach; experiments show AdaMeZO outperforms MeZO and reduces forward passes by up to 70%; trajectory visualizations indicate adaptation to diverse loss landscapes.
AI.conclusion: AdaMeZO provides a memory-efficient, faster-converging optimization strategy for fine-tuning LLMs, combining MeZO's forward-only advantage with Adam-like adaptivity without extra memory overhead.

title: Static and Dynamic Graph Alignment Network for Temporal Video Grounding
summary: Temporal Video Grounding (TVG) aims to localize temporal moments in an untrimmed video that semantically correspond to given natural language queries. Recently, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) have been widely adopted in TVG to model temporal relations among video clips and enhance contextual reasoning by constructing clip-level graphs. Despite their effectiveness, existing GCN-based TVG methods encounter three critical bottlenecks: 1) Most methods construct graph nodes using either static or dynamic features alone, resulting in incomplete visual representation and overlooking complementary semantics, 2) Most methods construct temporal graphs in a query-agnostic manner, leading to inefficient feature interaction within the temporal graph representation, and 3) Most methods often suffer from a single-granularity semantic matching, while direct training on complex temporal localization task may lead to slow convergence and suboptimal precision. To address these challenges, we propose Static and Dynamic Graph Alignment Network (SDGAN). First, SDGAN jointly exploits static and dynamic visual features to construct two complementary temporal graphs and performs Position-wise Nodes Alignment, enabling more expressive and robust visual representation. Second, SDGAN introduces Query-Clip Contrastive Learning and Adaptive Graph Modeling to explicitly align visual clips with their corresponding textual queries, yielding query-aware visual representations. Third, SDGAN incorporates multi-granularity temporal proposals within Progressive Easy-to-Hard Training Strategy, effectively bridging coarse-grained semantic localization and fine-grained temporal boundary refinement. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that SDGAN achieves superior performance across complex TVG scenarios. Codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/ZhanJieHu/SDGAN.

AI.tldr: SDGAN introduces a dual-graph (static and dynamic features) approach for TVG, with position-wise node alignment, query-clip contrastive learning, adaptive graph modeling, and a progressive multi-granularity training strategy to bridge coarse-to-fine localization; achieves state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks with public code.
AI.motivation: Current GCN-based TVG methods suffer from: (1) incomplete visual representation due to single-feature graph nodes, (2) query-agnostic temporal graphs that hinder effective feature interaction, and (3) single-granularity matching leading to slow convergence and reduced precision. There is a need for richer, query-aware, and multi-granularity localization strategies.
AI.method: SDGAN constructs two complementary temporal graphs using static and dynamic visual features with Position-wise Nodes Alignment. It introduces Query-Clip Contrastive Learning and Adaptive Graph Modeling to align clips with textual queries, creating query-aware representations. It also employs a Progressive Easy-to-Hard Training Strategy with multi-granularity temporal proposals to connect coarse localization with fine boundary refinement.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmarks show SDGAN achieves superior performance under complex TVG scenarios, surpassing baselines. The authors provide code and datasets at the project link.
AI.conclusion: SDGAN effectively addresses the three identified bottlenecks by (i) enriching visual representation through dual graphs, (ii) making the temporal graph query-aware via contrastive alignment and adaptive modeling, and (iii) bridging coarse-to-fine localization with multi-granularity proposals and progressive training; yielding strong empirical gains and a public codebase.

title: M-CaStLe: Uncovering Local Causal Structures in Multivariate Space-Time Gridded Data
comment: 19 pages and 6 figures in the main text; 33 pages and 11 figures total
summary:
Causal graph discovery for space-time systems is challenging in high-dimensional gridded data, which often has many more grid cells than temporal observations per cell. The Causal Space-Time Stencil Learning (CaStLe) meta-algorithm was developed to address that niche under space-time locality and stationarity assumptions, but it is currently limited to univariate analyses. In this work, we present M-CaStLe. M-CaStLe generalizes the local embedding and parent-identification phases of CaStLe to jointly model local within-variable and cross-variable space-time causal structures in gridded data. Like CaStLe, by constraining candidate parents to a constant-size space-time neighborhood and pooling spatial replicates, M-CaStLe increases effective sample size to make discovery tractable in high-dimensional settings. We further decompose the resulting multivariate stencil graph into reaction and spatial graphs to aid interpretation in complex settings.
  We study M-CaStLe in four settings: a multivariate space-time vector autoregression benchmark with known ground truth, an advective-diffusive-reaction partial differential equation verification problem with derived physical reference structure, an atmospheric chemistry case study in a low-temporal-sample regime, and an El Niño Southern Oscillation study on reanalysis data, identifying phase-dependent ocean--atmosphere coupling. Across these settings, M-CaStLe more accurately recovers multivariate causal structure in controlled settings and identifies important physical dynamics in real-world case studies. Overall, M-CaStLe advances causal discovery for multivariate space-time systems while retaining interpretability at the grid level.

AI.tldr: M-CaStLe extends CaStLe to multivariate space-time data by modeling local within- and cross-variable causal structure in a gridded domain, using a fixed local space-time neighborhood and spatial pooling, and decomposing the causal stencil into reaction and spatial graphs; validated across synthetic and real data, showing improved multivariate causal discovery and interpretability.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional space-time data (more grid cells than temporal observations) complicates causal discovery. CaStLe is univariate; there is a need for a multivariate, interpretable approach that leverages space-time locality and limited samples.
AI.method: Generalizes CaStLe's local embedding and parent-identification to jointly model multivariate space-time causality. Candidate parents are constrained to a fixed-size space-time neighborhood; spatial replicates are pooled to increase effective sample size; the resulting multivariate stencil graph is decomposed into reaction and spatial graphs for interpretability.
AI.result: In four settings, M-CaStLe more accurately recovers multivariate causal structure in synthetic benchmarks and identifies important physical dynamics in PDE verification, atmospheric chemistry with limited temporal data, and ENSO studies, demonstrating practical utility and interpretability at the grid level.
AI.conclusion: M-CaStLe advances multivariate causal discovery for space-time systems while maintaining grid-level interpretability, extending CaStLe to high-dimensional gridded data and enabling physically meaningful insights across diverse domains.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Markov Risk Measures and Multipattern Risk Approximation
summary: For a risk-averse finite-horizon Markov Decision Problem, we introduce a special class of Markov coherent risk measures, called mini-batch measures. We also define the class of multipattern risk-averse problems that generalizes the class of linear systems. We use both concepts in a feature-based $Q$-learning method with multipattern $Q$-factor approximation and we prove a high-probability regret bound of $\mathcal{O}\big(H^2 N^H \sqrt{ K}\big)$, where $H$ is the horizon, $N$ is the mini-batch size, and $K$ is the number of episodes. We also propose an economical version of the $Q$-learning method that streamlines the policy evaluation (backward) step. The theoretical results are illustrated on a stochastic assignment problem and a short-horizon multi-armed bandit problem.

AI.tldr: Introduces mini-batch coherent risk measures for risk-averse finite-horizon MDPs and a multipattern risk-averse framework; develops a feature-based Q-learning with multipattern Q-factor approximation and proves a high-probability regret bound; proposes an efficient backward-policy evaluation; validates on stochastic assignment and short-horizon MAB.
AI.motivation: Address risk-averse decision making in finite-horizon MDPs and generalize to multipattern settings, combining coherent risk measures with learning-based control to obtain provable guarantees and practical efficiency.
AI.method: Define mini-batch Markov coherent risk measures and multipattern risk-averse problems; integrate these into a feature-based Q-learning algorithm with multipattern Q-factor approximation; derive a high-probability regret bound; propose an economical backward (policy evaluation) variant.
AI.result: High-probability regret bound of O(H^2 N^H sqrt(K)); an economical version of the Q-learning method for efficient backward evaluation; empirical illustrations on a stochastic assignment problem and a short-horizon multi-armed bandit problem.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides provable regret guarantees for risk-averse learning in finite-horizon settings, with a computationally efficient evaluation scheme and demonstrated applicability to practical risk-aware control tasks.

title: PhysEdit: Physically-Consistent Region-Aware Image Editing via Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reasoning
summary: Image editing instructions are heterogeneous: a color swap, an object insertion, and a physical-action edit all demand different spatial coverage and different reasoning depth, yet existing reasoning-based editors apply a single fixed inference recipe to every instruction. We argue that adaptivity along both the spatial and temporal axes is the missing degree of freedom, and we present PhysEdit, an editing framework built around this principle. PhysEdit introduces two inference-time modules that compose without retraining the backbone. At its core, (1) Complexity-Adaptive Reasoning Depth (CARD) predicts edit complexity directly from the instruction and reference image and allocates the reasoning step count N_r and reasoning-token length r per sample -- turning a previously fixed inference schedule into a conditional-computation problem. CARD is supported by (2) a Spatial Reasoning Mask (SRM) that extracts an instruction-conditioned spatial prior from cross-attention to confine reasoning to regions that semantically require it. On the full 737-case ImgEdit Basic-Edit Suite, PhysEdit delivers a 1.18x wall-clock speedup (64.3s vs. 76.1s per sample) over a strong reasoning baseline while slightly improving instruction adherence (CLIP-T 0.2283 vs. 0.2266, +0.7%) and matching identity preservation within noise (CLIP-I 0.8246 vs. 0.8280). The speedup is category-dependent and reaches 1.52x on appearance-level edits, validating CARD's adaptive allocation as the principal source of efficiency gain. A 30-sample pilot with full ablations isolates the contribution of each module.

AI.tldr: PhysEdit uses adaptive inference depth (CARD) and a spatial prior (SRM) to tailor editing reasoning per instruction without retraining, yielding speedups and competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reasoning-based editors need different spatial coverage and reasoning depth for diverse edits; a fixed inference schedule is inefficient.
AI.method: Introduce CARD to predict per-sample edit complexity and allocate reasoning steps/token length; SRM to confine reasoning to instruction-conditioned spatial regions; backbone remains frozen; no retraining.
AI.result: On 737-case ImgEdit Basic-Edit Suite, 1.18x wall-clock speedup (64.3s vs 76.1s); CLIP-T improves to 0.2283 vs 0.2266 (+0.7%); CLIP-I 0.8246 vs 0.8280 (within noise); speedup up to 1.52x for appearance edits; 30-sample pilot isolates contributions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive inference depth and spatial masking are effective, with adaptive allocation driving efficiency; the approach achieves faster editing with comparable or slightly better instruction adherence and preserved identity, without backbone retraining.

title: Learning Coarse-to-Fine Osteoarthritis Representations under Noisy Hierarchical Labels
summary: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) assessment involves a natural but often underused label hierarchy: a coarse binary OA decision and a fine-grained Kellgren--Lawrence (KL) severity grade. Existing deep learning studies commonly treat these targets as separate classification problems, either reducing OA assessment to disease presence or directly optimizing noisy ordinal KL labels. In this work, we ask whether this clinical hierarchy can serve as a representation-level supervisory prior. Rather than introducing a complex architecture, we use a deliberately simple dual-head model with a shared encoder and two task-specific heads as a probe of hierarchical supervision. We compare single-OA, single-KL, and dual-head training across multiple 3D backbones under the same test protocol. Beyond standard classification metrics, we perform paired statistical comparisons, analyze latent severity-axis geometry, and examine saliency overlap with cartilage regions. The results show that dual-head supervision produces backbone-dependent gains, with clear improvements in KL-related metrics for selected backbones. More importantly, the gains are accompanied by a more ordered coarse-to-fine latent organization and, for responsive backbones, stronger anatomical alignment of saliency with cartilage. These findings suggest that even simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can reshape disease representations under noisy coarse/fine labels, providing a useful inductive bias for OA diagnosis and severity grading.

AI.tldr: Dual-head hierarchical supervision (shared encoder, two task heads for binary OA and KL grade) can reshape representations and improve KL-related performance in certain 3D backbones, accompanied by a more ordered coarse-to-fine latent structure and better saliency alignment to cartilage; gains are backbone-dependent.
AI.motivation: To test whether a natural clinical label hierarchy (coarse OA presence followed by fine-grained Kellgren–Lawrence severity) can serve as a representation-level supervisory prior, without introducing a complex architecture.
AI.method: Train a simple dual-head model (shared encoder, separate heads for OA vs KL) and compare against single-OA and single-KL baselines across multiple 3D backbones using the same test protocol. Assess standard classification metrics, paired statistical tests, latent severity-axis geometry, and saliency overlap with cartilage regions.
AI.result: Dual-head supervision yields backbone-dependent gains, with clear improvements in KL-related metrics for selected backbones. Gains accompany a more ordered coarse-to-fine latent organization and, for responsive backbones, stronger anatomical alignment of saliency with cartilage.
AI.conclusion: Even simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can reshape disease representations under noisy coarse/fine labels, providing a useful inductive bias for OA diagnosis and severity grading.

title: Rethinking LLM Ensembling from the Perspective of Mixture Models
summary: Model ensembling is a well-established technique for improving the performance of machine learning models. Conventionally, this involves averaging the output distributions of multiple models and selecting the most probable label. This idea has been naturally extended to large language models (LLMs), yielding improved performance but incurring substantial computational cost. This inefficiency stems from directly applying conventional ensemble implementation to LLMs, which require a separate forward pass for each model to explicitly compute the ensemble distribution. In this paper, we propose the Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME). By reinterpreting the ensemble as a mixture model, ME stochastically selects a single model at each step to generate the next token, thereby avoiding the need to explicitly compute the full ensemble distribution. ME is mathematically equivalent to sampling from the ensemble distribution, but requires invoking only one model, making it 1.78x-2.68x faster than conventional ensemble. Furthermore, this perspective connects LLM ensembling and token-level routing methods, suggesting that LLM ensembling is a special case of routing methods. Our findings open new avenues for efficient LLM ensembling and motivate further exploration of token-level routing strategies for LLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/jialefu/Mixture-model-like-Ensemble/.

AI.tldr: Reframes LLM ensembling as a mixture-model; samples a single model per token to emulate the ensemble while avoiding full forward passes, yielding speedups.
AI.motivation: Ensembling boosts performance but incurs high computational cost for LLMs due to multiple forward passes. A more efficient ensemble method is needed.
AI.method: Interpret the ensemble as a mixture distribution over models. At each decoding step, stochastically select one model to generate the next token, effectively sampling from the ensemble distribution without computing it in full. This connects ensembling with token-level routing strategies.
AI.result: ME achieves 1.78x–2.68x faster decoding than conventional ensembles. It is mathematically equivalent to sampling from the ensemble distribution and reveals a link between LLM ensembling and routing methods. Code is provided.
AI.conclusion: The mixture-model viewpoint enables efficient LLM ensembling and motivates further exploration of token-level routing strategies for LLMs.

title: Augmented Lagrangian Multiplier Network for State-wise Safety in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safety is a primary challenge in real-world reinforcement learning (RL). Formulating safety requirements as state-wise constraints has become a prominent paradigm. Handling state-wise constraints with the Lagrangian method requires a distinct multiplier for every state, necessitating neural networks to approximate them as a multiplier network. However, applying standard dual gradient ascent to multiplier networks induces severe training oscillations. This is because the inherent instability of dual ascent is exacerbated by network generalization -- local overshoots and delayed updates propagate to adjacent states, further amplifying policy fluctuations. Existing stabilization techniques are designed for scalar multipliers, which are inadequate for state-dependent multiplier networks. To address this challenge, we propose an augmented Lagrangian multiplier network (ALaM) framework for stable learning of state-wise multipliers. ALaM consists of two key components. First, a quadratic penalty is introduced into the augmented Lagrangian to compensate for delayed multiplier updates and establish the local convexity near the optimum, thereby mitigating policy oscillations. Second, the multiplier network is trained via supervised regression toward a dual target, which stabilizes training and promotes convergence. Theoretically, we show that ALaM guarantees multiplier convergence and thus recovers the optimal policy of the constrained problem. Building on this framework, we integrate soft actor-critic (SAC) with ALaM to develop the SAC-ALaM algorithm. Experiments demonstrate that SAC-ALaM outperforms state-of-the-art safe RL baselines in both safety and return, while also stabilizing training dynamics and learning well-calibrated multipliers for risk identification.

AI.tldr: Augmented Lagrangian Multiplier Network (ALaM) for stable state-wise constraints in safe RL; adds quadratic penalty and dual-target supervised regression; integrated with SAC to yield SAC-ALaM; theory guarantees convergence; empirical results show improved safety, performance, and stability.
AI.motivation: To address instability of dual gradient ascent when learning many state-dependent multipliers; standard stabilization methods fail for vector-valued multipliers; need a method that ensures convergence and stable training for state-wise constraints.
AI.method: Introduce ALaM with two components: (1) quadratic penalty in augmented Lagrangian to counteract delayed multiplier updates and improve local convexity; (2) train multiplier network via supervised regression toward a dual target to stabilize updates. Combine with SAC to obtain SAC-ALaM.
AI.result: Prove multiplier convergence and recovery of optimal policy; experiments show SAC-ALaM outperforms safe RL baselines in safety and return, with stabilized training and well-calibrated multipliers for risk identification.
AI.conclusion: ALaM offers a principled and effective approach to learning state-wise multipliers, enabling safe RL with robust convergence; SAC-ALaM demonstrates practical gains in safety and performance.

title: Unpaired Image Deraining Using Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Strategy
summary: Unsupervised deraining has attracted attention for its ability to learn the real-world distribution of rain without paired supervision. However, the lack of strong constraints makes it difficult for the network to converge, especially with the complex diversity of rain degradation. A key motivation is that high-quality deraining results occasionally emerge during training, which can be leveraged to guide the optimization process. To overcome these challenges, we introduce RGSUD (Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Unsupervised Image Deraining), comprising two key stages: reward recycling and self-reinforcement (SR) training. For the former stage, we propose an Image Quality Assessment (IQA)-based dynamic reward recycling mechanism that selects optimal derained outputs during training and continuously collects high-quality deraining images. In latter stage, we incorporate these rewards into the model's optimization process, constraining the optimization space and improving alignment between derained outputs and clean images. By leveraging IQA-based self-reinforced loss and dynamically updated rewards, we enhance the quality of synthesized pseudo-paired data and stabilize the optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves SOTA performance across multiple datasets, including paired synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real images, outperforming existing unsupervised deraining approaches in both subjective and objective IQA metrics. Additionally, we show that the self-reinforcement strategy is adaptable to other unsupervised deraining methods and our deraining framework demonstrates strong generalization across existing supervised deraining networks.

AI.tldr: An unsupervised image deraining method (RGSUD) that uses IQA-based reward recycling and self-reinforcement training to stabilize training and achieve state-of-the-art results across synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real datasets.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised deraining lacks strong supervision, and rain degradation is highly diverse, making convergence difficult. Occasional high-quality deraining during training can serve as informative guidance; a mechanism is needed to leverage these signals to guide optimization.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) reward recycling: dynamic selection of top derained outputs using an Image Quality Assessment (IQA) score to curate high-quality pseudo-derained images; (2) self-reinforcement (SR) training: integrate IQA-derived rewards into the model optimization with a self-reinforced loss and dynamically updated rewards, constraining the optimization space and aligning outputs with clean images. This improves pseudo-paired data quality and stabilizes training. The SR framework is adaptable to other unsupervised deraining methods and generalizes to supervised networks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets, including paired synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real images. Outperforms existing unsupervised deraining methods in both subjective and objective IQA metrics. The self-reinforcement strategy is transferable to other methods and demonstrates strong generalization to supervised deraining networks.
AI.conclusion: Reward-guided self-reinforcement provides a stable, high-quality unsupervised deraining framework by leveraging IQA-based dynamic rewards to guide optimization, with potential applicability to other unsupervised restoration tasks and cross-method generalization.

title: Meritocratic Fairness in Budgeted Combinatorial Multi-armed Bandits via Shapley Values
summary: We propose a new framework for meritocratic fairness in budgeted combinatorial multi-armed bandits with full-bandit feedback (BCMAB-FBF). Unlike semi-bandit feedback, the contribution of individual arms is not received in full-bandit feedback, making the setting significantly more challenging. To compute arm contributions in BCMAB-FBF, we first extend the Shapley value, a classical solution concept from cooperative game theory, to the $K$-Shapley value, which captures the marginal contribution of an agent restricted to a set of size at most $K$. We show that $K$-Shapley value is a unique solution concept that satisfies Symmetry, Linearity, Null player, and efficiency properties. We next propose K-SVFair-FBF, a fairness-aware bandit algorithm that adaptively estimates $K$-Shapley value with unknown valuation function. Unlike standard bandit literature on full bandit feedback, K-SVFair-FBF not only learns the valuation function under full feedback setting but also mitigates the noise arising from Monte Carlo approximations. Theoretically, we prove that K-SVFair-FBF achieves $O(T^{3/4})$ regret bound on fairness regret. Through experiments on federated learning and social influence maximization datasets, we demonstrate that our approach achieves fairness and performs more effectively than existing baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces K-Shapley value for budgeted combinatorial multi-armed bandits with full-bandit feedback, and a fairness-oriented algorithm (K-SVFair-FBF) that adaptively estimates it under unknown valuation. Provides a theoretical regret bound and empirical validation showing improved fairness and performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Tackle meritocratic fairness in BCMAB-FBF where individual arm contributions are not directly observable; extend Shapley value to a K-cap to quantify marginal contributions under limited interaction; develop a learning algorithm that remains robust to Monte Carlo noise.
AI.method: Extend Shapley value to K-Shapley (restricting coalitions to size K) and prove its uniqueness under standard axioms (Symmetry, Linearity, Null player, Efficiency). Develop K-SVFair-FBF that adaptively estimates the K-Shapley values with unknown valuation function, addressing noise from Monte Carlo estimation in full-bandit feedback.
AI.result: Theoretical: K-Shapley is the unique solution satisfying key axioms. Algorithm achieves O(T^{3/4}) regret for fairness regret. Empirical: outperforms baselines on federated learning and social influence maximization datasets in terms of fairness and overall performance.
AI.conclusion: A practical, principled framework for meritocratic fairness in challenging BCMAB-FBF settings; K-Shapley provides a robust, unique measure of marginal contribution, and K-SVFair-FBF delivers provable fairness guarantees with empirical effectiveness.

title: Exploring the Limits of End-to-End Feature-Affinity Propagation for Single-Point Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection
summary:
Single-point supervised infrared small target detection (IRSTD) drastically reduces dense annotation costs. Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods achieve high precision by recovering mask supervision through explicit, offline pseudo-label construction, such as multi-stage active learning and physics-driven mask generation. In this paper, we study a minimalist alternative: generating point-to-mask supervision online through in-batch, point-anchored feature-affinity propagation. We instantiate this paradigm as GSACP, an end-to-end testbed that directly supervises the detector using hard-margin feature affinity gated by local image priors, entirely eliminating external label-evolution loops.
  This compact design, however, exposes an optimization bottleneck. Because the affinity target is generated from the same feature representation being optimized, training forms a self-referential loop. We theoretically formalize this as \emph{Self-Referential Propagation Drift}, a representation-supervision entanglement that can sharpen true boundaries or distort the feature space to satisfy its own targets. To systematically isolate these failure modes, we apply a protocolized single-variable ablation procedure spanning local EMA teacher decoupling, hard-background contrastive separation, and adaptive support geometry.
  On the SIRST3 dataset, GSACP-Final establishes a new ultra-low false-alarm operating regime, achieving a highly competitive $0.6674$ mIoU while demonstrating a $38\% relative reduction in false-positive artifacts ($\mathrm{Fa}$) compared with PAL. By systematically deconstructing the end-to-end paradigm, we map its performance boundaries and show that in-batch feature propagation provides a compact alternative for deployment scenarios where false-alarm suppression is paramount.

AI.tldr: GSACP presents online point-to-mask supervision for single-point IRSTD via in-batch point-anchored feature affinity propagation, addressing self-referential drift; achieves strong mIoU with ultra-low false alarms on SIRST3 and lower FP vs PAL; analyzed via ablations to map operational boundaries.
AI.motivation: Reduce labeling costs for infrared small target detection by moving from explicit mask supervision to online point-to-mask supervision, and to understand potential failure modes without external label-evolution loops.
AI.method: GSACP framework uses in-batch, point-anchored feature affinity propagation with hard-margin constraints gated by local image priors. End-to-end training eliminates external loops. The paper formalizes Self-Referential Propagation Drift and conducts a protocolized single-variable ablation over EMA teacher decoupling, hard-background contrastive separation, and adaptive support geometry.
AI.result: On SIRST3, GSACP-Final achieves 0.6674 m IoU with an ultra-low false-alarm regime, and a 38% relative reduction in false-positive artifacts compared to PAL. Ablation clarifies performance boundaries and shows in-batch propagation as a compact, deployment-friendly alternative in false-alarm–sensitive scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Online point-to-mask supervision via in-batch feature propagation is viable and can yield competitive IRSTD performance under stringent false-alarm constraints. Self-Referential Propagation Drift is a key failure mode that can be mitigated through targeted ablations; the paradigm offers a practical alternative when minimizing false alarms is critical, though further work is needed to generalize and further stabilize training.

title: GD4: Graph-based Discrete Denoising Diffusion for MIMO Detection
summary: In wireless communications, recovering the optimal solution to the multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) detection problem is NP-hard. Obtaining high-quality suboptimal solutions with a favorable performance-complexity trade-off is particularly challenging in under-determined systems with $N_t$ transmit antennas and $N_r < N_t$ receive antennas. Recent diffusion-based MIMO detectors have shown promise, but they require extensive sampling iterations at inference time, and their performance degrades in under-determined scenarios. We propose GD4, a graph-based discrete denoising diffusion method for MIMO detection. Unlike existing diffusion-based detectors that operate in a continuous relaxed space, GD4 performs denoising directly in the discrete symbol space and enables fast inference with one or a few denoising evaluations. Numerical results show that, under a similar inference-time compute budget, GD4 produces higher-quality suboptimal solutions than existing diffusion-based detectors and some widely used classical baseline including box-constrained Babai point and the $K$-best box-constrained randomized Klein-Babai point in both under-determined and overdetermined settings.

AI.tldr: GD4 is a graph-based discrete denoising diffusion detector for MIMO that denoises in the discrete symbol space, enabling fast inference with few evaluations and achieving higher-quality suboptimal solutions than diffusion-based and classical baselines in both under- and over-determined MIMO settings.
AI.motivation: MIMO detection is NP-hard, with under-determined systems (Nr < Nt) being especially challenging. Diffusion-based detectors typically require many iterations and degrade in under-determined regimes, prompting a need for faster, high-quality suboptimal detectors.
AI.method: Introduce GD4, a graph-based discrete denoising diffusion method that operates directly in the discrete symbol space (not a continuous relaxation). It uses a graph-structured denoiser to refine candidate symbol vectors, enabling fast inference with one or a few denoising steps across both under- and over-determined MIMO configurations.
AI.result: With a similar inference-time compute budget, GD4 achieves higher-quality suboptimal solutions than existing diffusion-based detectors and strong baselines—including the box-constrained Babai point and the K-best box-constrained randomized Klein-Babai point—in both under-determined and overdetermined settings.
AI.conclusion: Discrete-space diffusion via GD4 delivers a favorable performance–complexity trade-off for MIMO detection, mitigating limitations of continuous-space diffusion in under-determined regimes and enabling efficient, high-quality suboptimal solutions.

title: Make Your LVLM KV Cache More Lightweight
comment: Accepted to Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR), 2026
summary: Key-Value (KV) cache has become a de facto component of modern Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) for inference. While it enhances decoding efficiency in Large Language Models (LLMs), its direct adoption in LVLMs introduces substantial GPU memory overhead due to the large number of vision tokens processed during the prefill stage. To tackle this problem, we propose LightKV, a novel approach that reduces KV cache size by exploiting the redundancy among vision-token embeddings. Guided by text prompts, LightKV employs cross-modality message passing to aggregate informative messages across vision tokens and progressively compress them during prefill. This prompt-aware guidance distinguishes our method from prior vision-only compression strategies. We evaluate LightKV on eight open-source LVLMs across eight public benchmark datasets, e.g., MME and SeedBench. Experimental results demonstrate that with only 55% of the original vision tokens, LightKV (a) halves the vision-token KV cache size, (b) reduces computation by up to 40%, and (c) preserves general-purpose performance while significantly outperforming existing baselines.

AI.tldr: LightKV reduces KV cache size in LVLMs by exploiting redundancy among vision-token embeddings with cross-modality message passing guided by prompts, enabling compression during prefill.
AI.motivation: KV caches in LVLMs incur substantial GPU memory overhead due to large numbers of vision tokens in the prefill stage; a memory-efficient solution that preserves performance is needed.
AI.method: LightKV uses cross-modality message passing to aggregate informative messages across vision tokens and progressively compress them during prefill, guided by text prompts to focus on task-relevant information, thereby reducing the KV cache size.
AI.result: Evaluated on eight open-source LVLMs over eight public benchmarks (e.g., MME, SeedBench). With only 55% of original vision tokens, LightKV halves the vision-token KV cache size, reduces computation by up to 40%, and preserves general-purpose performance while outperforming existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: LightKV provides a practical, prompt-guided, cross-modality approach to KV-cache compression in LVLMs, delivering substantial memory and compute savings with negligible loss in performance and good generalizability across models and datasets.

title: Quantum Gradient-Based Approach for Edge and Corner Detection Using Sobel Kernels
summary: Edge detection refers to identifying points in a digital image where intensity changes sharply, indicating object boundaries or structural features. Corners are locations where gray-level intensity changes abruptly in multiple directions and are widely used in feature extraction, object tracking, and 3D modeling. In this study, we present a quantum implementation of Sobel-based edge detection and Harris-style corner detection. Two quantum image encoding methods - Flexible Representation of Quantum Images (FRQI) and Quantum Probability Image Encoding (QPIE) - are used to encode the input data and are comparatively analyzed. The proposed approach introduces a quantum gradient computation scheme based on lag-2 differences, enabling the evaluation of gradient-like features in superposition. To improve detection quality and reduce false positives, a classical post-processing step is applied to candidate corner points identified by the quantum circuit. Results show that the proposed quantum circuits produce outputs consistent with classical Sobel and Harris operators. Furthermore, the QPIE-based configuration yields more stable and coherent results than FRQI, especially under limited measurement shots. While gradient computation can be performed efficiently at the circuit level, the overall cost remains dominated by state preparation, measurement, and classical post-processing. All experiments are conducted under noiseless simulation, and performance on NISQ hardware may be affected by noise and measurement limitations. Therefore, this work demonstrates a functional and scalable quantum realization of classical edge and corner detection methods rather than an end-to-end speedup.

AI.tldr: Quantum realization of Sobel-based edge and Harris-style corner detection with FRQI and QPIE encodings; gradient in superposition via lag-2 differences; QPIE more stable under limited shots; classical post-processing used; no end-to-end speedup; demonstrated in noiseless simulation.
AI.motivation: Assess viability of quantum edge and corner detection; compare image encodings; quantify computational bottlenecks and practicality on NISQ hardware.
AI.method: Encode input images with FRQI and QPIE; implement a quantum lag-2 gradient computation to detect edges; identify corner candidates via quantum circuits; apply a classical post-processing stage to reduce false positives.
AI.result: Quantum outputs align with classical Sobel and Harris operators; QPIE provides more stable, coherent results under limited measurements; time/cost dominated by state preparation, measurement, and post-processing; gradient computation itself is circuit-efficient; experiments run in noiseless simulation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a functional, scalable quantum implementation of classical edge/corner detection rather than an end-to-end speedup; hardware noise and measurement limits could affect real-world performance; future work to optimize preparation/measurement and explore real hardware, aiming toward practical quantum image analysis.

title: Optimal Spatio-Temporal Decoupling for Bayesian Conformal Prediction
summary: Online Conformal Prediction (CP) struggles to balance temporal adaptability and structural stability. Feedback-driven methods (e.g., Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI)) suffer from systemic marginal under-coverage and high interval variance during abrupt shifts, while temporally discounted Bayesian CP suffers from severe structural lag and uncalibrated interval bloat. We propose State-Adaptive Bayesian Conformal Prediction (SA-BCP) to achieve optimal spatio-temporal decoupling. By gating long-term temporal inertia with spatial kernel-density evidence, SA-BCP proactively expands intervals for recognized historical regimes while maintaining tight efficiency during stable states. We rigorously prove this mechanism's optimality, identifying a minimax bias-variance tradeoff governed by an evidence threshold $K$. Extensive benchmarks on volatile financial datasets (2016--2026), including AMD, Gold, and GBP/USD, demonstrate that SA-BCP consistently minimizes the strictly proper Winkler score across diverse confidence levels. Specifically, SA-BCP resolves the systematic under-coverage inherent to ACI variants while simultaneously reducing the uncalibrated interval bloat of Bayesian CP by 10\% to 37\% under high-confidence requests. By elegantly navigating this tradeoff, SA-BCP achieves an optimal balance between conditional reliability and predictive efficiency.

AI.tldr: SA-BCP introduces state-adaptive Bayesian conformal prediction with a spatial gating mechanism that leverages kernel-density evidence to decouple temporal inertia from spatial context. It proves a minimax bias-variance tradeoff controlled by an evidence threshold K and demonstrates strong empirical gains on volatile financial data, reducing under-coverage and interval bloat relative to ACI and standard Bayesian CP.
AI.motivation: Online conformal prediction trades off temporal adaptability and structural stability. Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) suffers from systemic under-coverage and high interval variance during abrupt shifts; temporally discounted Bayesian CP incurs lag and uncalibrated interval bloating. A method that robustly decouples spatial context from temporal dynamics is needed to maintain calibration while preserving efficiency.
AI.method: Propose State-Adaptive Bayesian Conformal Prediction (SA-BCP) that gates long-term temporal inertia with spatial kernel-density evidence. By using an evidence threshold K, the approach expands intervals for recognized historical regimes and keeps tight intervals in stable states. The paper provides theoretical optimality results, establishing a minimax bias-variance tradeoff, and details the mechanism for spatio-temporal decoupling.
AI.result: Extensive benchmarks on volatile financial series (2016–2026) including AMD, Gold, and GBP/USD show SA-BCP minimizes the strictly proper Winkler score across confidence levels. It resolves ACI-style under-coverage and reduces Bayesian CP interval bloating by 10–37% under high-confidence requests.
AI.conclusion: SA-BCP achieves an optimal balance between conditional reliability and predictive efficiency, offering robust spatio-temporal decoupling that outperforms existing adaptive or Bayesian conformal predictions in volatile regimes.

title: Persistent Visual Memory: Sustaining Perception for Deep Generation in LVLMs
summary: While autoregressive Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) demonstrate remarkable proficiency in multimodal tasks, they face a "Visual Signal Dilution" phenomenon, where the accumulation of textual history expands the attention partition function, causing visual attention to decay inversely with generated sequence length. To counteract this, we propose Persistent Visual Memory (PVM), a lightweight learnable module designed to ensure sustained, on-demand visual perception. Integrated as a parallel branch alongside the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) in LVLMs, PVM establishes a distance-agnostic retrieval pathway that directly provides visual embeddings for precise visual perception, thereby structurally mitigating the signal suppression inherent to deep generation. Extensive experiments on Qwen3-VL models demonstrate that PVM brings notable improvements with negligible parameter overhead, delivering consistent average accuracy gains across both 4B and 8B scales, particularly in complex reasoning tasks that demand persistent visual perception. Furthermore, in-depth analysis reveals that PVM can resist length-induced signal decay and accelerate internal prediction convergence.

AI.tldr: PVM—Persistent Visual Memory—addresses Visual Signal Dilution in autoregressive LVLMs by adding a lightweight parallel visual memory module that retrieves visual embeddings on demand, providing persistent visual perception with minimal parameter overhead, yielding performance gains on Qwen3-VL 4B/8B models and faster convergence.
AI.motivation: As textual history grows during generation, the attention allocated to visual context diminishes (visual signal dilution). A persistent, on-demand visual pathway is needed to maintain visual perception throughout long generations.
AI.method: Introduce PVM as a parallel branch to the LVLM's FFN. PVM implements distance-agnostic retrieval to fetch visual embeddings and supply direct visual perception, mitigating signal suppression during deep generation.
AI.result: Empirical results on Qwen3-VL show notable accuracy gains at 4B and 8B scales with negligible parameter overhead, especially on complex reasoning tasks; PVM resists length-induced decay and accelerates convergence.
AI.conclusion: PVM effectively mitigates visual signal dilution in autoregressive LVLMs, enabling persistent visual perception with small overhead, improving multimodal reasoning stability and performance.

title: Modeling Subjective Urban Perception with Human Gaze
summary: Urban perception describes how people subjectively evaluate urban environments, shaping how cities are experienced and understood. Existing computational approaches primarily model urban perception directly from street view images, but largely ignore the human perceptual process through which such judgments are formed. In this paper, we introduce Place Pulse-Gaze, an urban perception dataset that augments street view images with synchronized eye-tracking recordings and individual perception labels. Based on this dataset, we propose a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework to study how gaze behavior contributes to the modeling of subjective urban perception. The framework systematically investigates three complementary settings: gaze-only modeling, gaze fusion with explicit semantic scene representations, and gaze fusion with implicit richer visual representations. Experiments show that gaze alone already carries useful predictive signals for subjective urban perception, and that integrating gaze with scene representations further improves prediction under both semantic and richer visual representations. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of incorporating human perceptual processes into urban scene understanding and open a direction for gaze-guided multimodal urban computing.

AI.tldr: Eye-tracking augmented urban perception dataset and a gaze-guided framework show that gaze signals carry predictive power for subjective urban perception and improve predictions when fused with semantic or richer visual representations.
AI.motivation: Current street-view based models largely ignore the human perceptual process; understanding how gaze and perception labels shape urban judgments can lead to more accurate, human-aligned urban scene understanding.
AI.method: Create Place Pulse-Gaze dataset by augmenting street view images with synchronized eye-tracking data and perception labels. Propose a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework exploring three settings: (1) gaze-only modeling, (2) gaze fusion with explicit semantic scene representations, (3) gaze fusion with implicit richer visual representations.
AI.result: Gaze alone provides useful predictive signals for subjective urban perception. Fusion of gaze with semantic or richer visual representations yields further predictive improvements across the tested settings.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the importance of incorporating human perceptual processes into urban scene understanding and invites gaze-guided multimodal urban computing as a research direction.

title: Adaptive Equilibrium: Dynamic Weighting Framework for Generalized Interruption of DeepFake Models
summary: The advancement of generalized deepfake disruption is constrained by the interruption imbalance, a fundamental bottleneck inherent to the generation of universal perturbations. We reveal that conventional static gradient normalization fundamentally struggles to resolve architectural conflicts, causing the optimization to bias towards susceptible models while neglecting resistant ones. We argue that achieving high and uniform effectiveness requires resolving this imbalance by reaching an adaptive equilibrium. We propose the Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF), which employs a dynamic weighting mechanism that utilizes real-time loss feedback to adaptively assign greater interruption weights to the most resistant models. This approach shifts the optimization from an average-case problem to finding a dynamic balance, driving the perturbation to a uniformly effective equilibrium state. Comprehensive experiments validate that AEF achieves a more balanced interruption performance, maintaining a consistent interruption success rate across the evaluated diverse architectures.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF) uses real-time loss feedback to dynamically weight the most resistant models, turning universal perturbation optimization into a dynamic balance etching a uniformly effective equilibrium.
AI.motivation: Generalized deepfake disruption suffers from interruption imbalance and static gradient normalization, which biases optimization toward susceptible models and neglects resistant ones; a uniform, robust perturbation across diverse architectures is highly desirable.
AI.method: Introduce Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF) with a dynamic weighting mechanism that uses real-time loss signals to assign higher interruption weights to resistant models, shifting optimization from average-case to a dynamic equilibrium.
AI.result: AEF achieves more balanced interruption performance and maintains a consistent interruption success rate across diverse architectures in comprehensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, loss-guided weighting fosters a uniformly effective equilibrium for universal perturbations, overcoming imbalance and architectural conflicts.

title: Map2World: Segment Map Conditioned Text to 3D World Generation
summary: 3D world generation is essential for applications such as immersive content creation or autonomous driving simulation. Recent advances in 3D world generation have shown promising results; however, these methods are constrained by grid layouts and suffer from inconsistencies in object scale throughout the entire world. In this work, we introduce a novel framework, Map2World, that first enables 3D world generation conditioned on user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes and scales, ensuring global-scale consistency and flexibility across expansive environments. To further enhance the quality, we propose a detail enhancer network that generates fine details of the world. The detail enhancer enables the addition of fine-grained details without compromising overall scene coherence by incorporating global structure information. We design the entire pipeline to leverage strong priors from asset generators, achieving robust generalization across diverse domains, even under limited training data for scene generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches in user-controllability, scale consistency, and content coherence, enabling users to generate 3D worlds under more complex conditions.

AI.tldr: Map2World enables 3D world generation conditioned on user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes and scales, achieving global-scale consistency and controllability; it adds a detail enhancer to generate fine details without breaking coherence and leverages asset priors for robustness with limited data; experiments show superior performance in controllability, scale consistency, and content coherence across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Current 3D world generation methods rely on grid layouts and exhibit scale inconsistencies across large environments, limiting controllability and realism. A framework that supports arbitrary-shaped segment maps, global-scale consistency, and detail refinement is needed, especially with limited training data.
AI.method: Proposes Map2World, a pipeline that conditions 3D world generation on user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes and scales, plus a detail enhancer network that adds fine details while preserving global structure. The pipeline leverages strong priors from asset generators to improve generalization and robustness under limited data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in user controllability, scale consistency, and content coherence in diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: Map2World enables flexible, coherent large-scale 3D world generation conditioned on arbitrary segment maps and enhanced by a detail network, with strong generalization and robustness under limited data, advancing immersive content creation and simulation applications.

title: The Power of Order: Fooling LLMs with Adversarial Table Permutations
summary: Large Language Models have achieved remarkable success and are increasingly deployed in critical applications involving tabular data, such as Table Question Answering. However, their robustness to the structure of this input remains a critical, unaddressed question. This paper demonstrates that modern LLMs exhibit a significant vulnerability to the layout of tabular data. Specifically, we show that semantically-invariant permutations of rows and columns - rearrangements that do not alter the table's underlying information - are sometimes sufficient to cause incorrect or inconsistent model outputs. To systematically probe this vulnerability, we introduce Adversarial Table Permutation, a novel, gradient-based attack that efficiently identifies worst-case permutations designed to maximally disrupt model performance. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that ATP significantly degrades the performance of a wide range of LLMs. This reveals a pervasive vulnerability across different model sizes and architectures, including the most recent and popular models. Our findings expose a fundamental weakness in how current LLMs process structured data, underscoring the urgent need to develop permutation-robust models for reliable, real-world applications.

AI.tldr: LLMs for table QA are vulnerable to semantically invariant table reordering; an adversarial gradient-based attack (ATP) finds worst-case permutations that degrade performance across models, revealing a widespread robustness issue and the need for permutation-robust architectures.
AI.motivation: Assess robustness of large language models to input structure in tabular data and determine whether layout permutations alter outputs, exposing a fundamental weakness in current table processing.
AI.method: Introduce Adversarial Table Permutation (ATP), a gradient-based search that identifies row/column permutations that maximally disrupt model performance; evaluate ATP across diverse LLMs and tabular tasks to demonstrate universality of the vulnerability.
AI.result: ATP significantly degrades performance across model sizes and architectures, revealing pervasive vulnerability to tabular layout permutations.
AI.conclusion: There is an urgent need to develop permutation-robust models or training strategies to ensure reliable performance on real-world tabular tasks.

title: Federated Learning with Hypergradient-based Online Update of Aggregation Weights
summary: Federated learning using mobile and Internet of Things devices requires not only the ability to handle heterogeneity of clients' data distributions but also high adaptability to varying communication environments. We propose FedHAW (Federated Learning with Hypergradient-based update of Aggregation Weights) that implements online updates of aggregation weights. FedHAW updates the aggregation weights by using hypergradient, the gradient of the objective function with respect to the weights, which can be calculated with low computational overhead. Simulation results show that the proposed method possesses high generalization performance in heterogeneous environments and high robustness to communication errors.

AI.tldr: Online hypergradient-based aggregation weight updates in federated learning to improve robustness and generalization in heterogeneous data and unreliable communication.
AI.motivation: Federated learning faces data heterogeneity across clients and variable communication environments. There is a need for adaptive, low-overhead aggregation schemes that can adjust the contribution of client models.
AI.method: FedHAW updates aggregation weights online by computing hypergradients (gradients of the objective w.r.t. the weights), enabling low computational overhead.
AI.result: Simulations indicate high generalization performance in heterogeneous settings and strong robustness to communication errors.
AI.conclusion: Hypergradient-based updates enable effective, low-overhead adaptive aggregation in federated learning, improving generalization and communication robustness.

title: GMGaze: MoE-Based Context-Aware Gaze Estimation with CLIP and Multiscale Transformer
summary: Gaze estimation methods commonly use facial appearances to predict the direction of a person gaze. However, previous studies show three major challenges with convolutional neural network (CNN)-based, transformer-based, and contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP)-based methods, including late fusion of image features, lack of factor-aware conditioning, and impractical capacity scaling. To address these challenges, we propose Globally-conditioned Multi-scale Gaze estimation (GMGaze), which leverages a multi-scale transformer architecture. Specifically, the model first introduces semantic prototype conditioning, which modulates the CLIP global image embedding using four learned prototype banks (i.e., illumination, background, head pose and appearance) to generate two complementary context-biased global tokens. These tokens, along with the CLIP patch and CNN tokens, are fused at the first layer. This early unified fusion prevents information loss common in late-stage merging. Finally, each token passes through sparse Mixture-of-Experts modules, providing conditional computational capacity without uniformly increasing dense parameters. For cross-domain adaptation, we incorporate an adversarial domain adaptation technique with a feature separation loss that encourages the two global tokens to remain de-correlated. Experiments using four public benchmarks (MPIIFaceGaze, EYEDIAP, Gaze360, and ETH-XGaze) show that GMGaze achieves mean angular errors of 2.49$^\circ$, 3.22$^\circ$, 10.16$^\circ$, and 1.44$^\circ$, respectively, outperforming previous baselines in all within-domain settings. In cross-domain evaluations, it provides state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on two standard transfer routes.

AI.tldr: GMGaze introduces a globally-conditioned, multi-scale gaze estimation architecture that fuses CLIP, CNN, and transformer tokens early, uses semantic prototype conditioning via learned banks, and employs sparse Mixture-of-Experts with adversarial domain adaptation to achieve state-of-the-art within- and cross-domain gaze estimation on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome three known challenges in gaze estimation with CNN/transformer/CLIP-based approaches: (1) late fusion of image features that risks information loss, (2) lack of factor-aware conditioning for factors like illumination, background, head pose, and appearance, and (3) impractical capacity scaling due to dense parameter growth.
AI.method: A multi-scale transformer framework with semantic prototype conditioning that modulates the CLIP global image embedding using four learned prototype banks (illumination, background, head pose, appearance) to produce two context-biased global tokens. These tokens, plus CLIP patch and CNN tokens, are fused at the first layer. Each token then passes through sparse Mixture-of-Experts modules for conditional computation. Adversarial domain adaptation with a feature separation loss encourages the two global tokens to remain decorrelated across domains.
AI.result: On MPIIFaceGaze, EYEDIAP, Gaze360, and ETH-XGaze, GMGaze achieves mean angular errors of 2.49°, 3.22°, 10.16°, and 1.44°, respectively, outperforming previous baselines within-domain. In cross-domain evaluations, it attains state-of-the-art results on two standard transfer routes.
AI.conclusion: GMGaze effectively addresses late fusion, factor-aware conditioning, and scalable capacity by early unified fusion and sparse MoE, with added domain-adaptive decorrelation to boost cross-domain performance; demonstrates strong within- and cross-domain gaze estimation performance across four public benchmarks.

title: Let ViT Speak: Generative Language-Image Pre-training
summary: In this paper, we present \textbf{Gen}erative \textbf{L}anguage-\textbf{I}mage \textbf{P}re-training (GenLIP), a minimalist generative pretraining framework for Vision Transformers (ViTs) designed for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To better align vision encoders with the autoregressive nature of LLMs, GenLIP trains a ViT to predict language tokens directly from visual tokens using a standard language modeling objective, without contrastive batch construction or an additional text decoder. This design offers three key advantages: (1) \textbf{Simplicity}: a single transformer jointly models visual and textual tokens; (2) \textbf{Scalability}: it scales effectively with both data and model size; and (3) \textbf{Performance}: it achieves competitive or superior results across diverse multimodal benchmarks. Trained on 8B samples from Recap-DataComp-1B, GenLIP matches or surpasses strong baselines despite using substantially less pretraining data. After continued pretraining on multi-resolution images at native aspect ratios, GenLIP further improves on detail-sensitive tasks such as OCR and chart understanding, making it a strong foundation for vision encoders in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: GenLIP is a minimalist ViT pretraining framework for multimodal LLMs that uses a single transformer to predict language tokens from visual tokens with a standard LM objective, achieving strong results with far less data; continued pretraining on multi-resolution images improves OCR/chart tasks.
AI.motivation: Align vision encoders with the autoregressive nature of LLMs; simplify multimodal pretraining by avoiding contrastive objectives and text decoders; enable scalable, high-performance MLLMs with minimal components.
