AI.method: Train a Vision Transformer to predict language tokens directly from visual tokens using a standard language modeling objective, no contrastive batch construction, no extra text decoder; a single transformer handles both visual and textual tokens; pretrain on 8B samples from Recap-DataComp-1B; after pretraining, further pretrain on multi-resolution images at native aspect ratios.
AI.result: GenLIP matches or surpasses strong multimodal baselines across diverse benchmarks; achieves competitive performance with substantially less pretraining data; multi-resolution continued pretraining yields improvements on detail-sensitive tasks like OCR and chart understanding.
AI.conclusion: GenLIP provides a simple, scalable, and effective vision encoder foundation for MLLMs, with strong performance and potential for further gains through targeted pretraining on varied image resolutions and aspect ratios.

title: Batch Normalization for Neural Networks on Complex Domains
summary: Riemannian neural networks have proven effective in solving a variety of machine learning tasks. The key to their success lies in the development of principled Riemannian analogs of fundamental building blocks in deep neural networks (DNNs). Among those, Riemannian batch normalization (BN) layers have shown to enhance training stability and improve accuracy. In this paper, we propose BN layers for neural networks on complex domains. The proposed layers have close connections with existing Riemannian BN layers. We derive essential components for practical implementations of BN layers on some complex domains which are less studied in previous works, e.g., the Siegel disk domain. We conduct experiments on radar clutter classification, node classification, and action recognition demonstrating the efficacy of our method.

AI.tldr: Extends Riemannian batch normalization to neural networks on complex domains, deriving practical BN components for less-studied manifolds (e.g., Siegel disk) and validating on radar, graph, and action-recognition tasks.
AI.motivation: Riemannian neural networks benefit from batch normalization for training stability and performance, but BN on complex manifolds remains underexplored. There is a need for principled, implementable BN layers tailored to complex domains.
AI.method: Develop BN components for complex-domain manifolds, connect them to existing Riemannian BN layers, derive practical formulas for the Siegel disk among other domains, implement BN layers, and integrate them into networks. Validate via experiments on radar clutter classification, node classification, and action recognition.
AI.result: Demonstrates that BN on complex domains is feasible and beneficial, with improved training stability and accuracy across diverse tasks. The proposed layers closely connect to existing Riemannian BN frameworks and enable practical implementations on complex manifolds such as the Siegel disk.
AI.conclusion: Extends Riemannian batch normalization to complex domains, providing practical derivations and demonstrating empirical benefits. This work broadens the applicability of RBNNBN layers and motivates further exploration of BN on additional complex manifolds.

title: Near-optimal and Efficient First-Order Algorithm for Multi-Task Learning with Shared Linear Representation
summary: Multi-task learning (MTL) has emerged as a pivotal paradigm in machine learning by leveraging shared structures across multiple related tasks. Despite its empirical success, the development of likelihood-based efficiently solvable algorithms--even for shared linear representations--remains largely underdeveloped, primarily due to the non-convex structure intrinsic to matrix factorization. This paper introduces a first-order algorithm that jointly learns a shared representation and task-specific parameters, with guaranteed efficiency. Notably, it converges in $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1)$ iterations and attains a \emph{near-optimal} estimation error of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(dk/(TN))$, \emph{improving} over existing likelihood-based methods by a factor of $k$, where $d$, $k$, $T$, $N$ denote input dimension, representation dimension, task count, and samples per task, respectively. Our results justify that likelihood-based first-order methods can efficiently solve the MTL problem.

AI.tldr: First-order method for multi-task learning (MTL) with a shared linear representation learns representations and task-specific parameters jointly. It converges in polylogarithmic (nearly constant) iterations and achieves near-optimal estimation error, improving over prior likelihood-based methods by a factor of k.
AI.motivation: MTL benefits from shared structures across tasks but likelihood-based algorithms for learning a shared representation suffer from non-convexity due to matrix factorization. There is a need for efficient, provable algorithms that are scalable with representation size and task count.
AI.method: A first-order optimization algorithm jointly learns a shared representation (matrix factor) and task-specific parameters. The method tackles non-convexity to obtain efficient convergence. It achieves a convergence rate of O(1) iterations and an estimation error bound of O(dk/(TN)).
AI.result: The proposed method yields near-optimal estimation error improving over existing likelihood-based methods by a factor of k. It demonstrates that likelihood-based first-order methods can efficiently solve the MTL problem, with strong theoretical guarantees on both convergence and statistical error.
AI.conclusion: Likelihood-based first-order methods can efficiently solve MTL with a shared representation, achieving fast convergence and near-optimal statistical accuracy.

title: Posterior Augmented Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching (FM) trains a time-dependent vector field that transports samples from a simple prior to a complex data distribution. However, for high-dimensional images, each training sample supervises only a single trajectory and intermediate point, yielding an extremely sparse and high-variance training signal. This under-constrained supervision can cause flow collapse, where the learned dynamics memorize specific source-target pairings, mapping diverse inputs to overly similar outputs, failing to generalize. We introduce Posterior-Augmented Flow Matching (PAFM), a theoretically grounded generalization of FM that replaces single-target supervision with an expectation over an approximate posterior of valid target completions for a given intermediate state and condition. PAFM factorizes this intractable posterior into (i) the likelihood of the intermediate under a hypothesized endpoint and (ii) the prior probability of that endpoint under the condition, and uses an importance sampling scheme to construct a mixture over multiple candidate targets. We prove that PAFM yields an unbiased estimator of the original FM objective while substantially reducing gradient variance during training by aggregating information from many plausible continuation trajectories per intermediate. Finally, we show that PAFM improves over FM by up to 3.4 FID50K across different model scales (SiT-B/2 and SiT-XL/2), different architectures (SiT and MMDiT), and in both class and text conditioned benchmarks (ImageNet and CC12M), with a negligible increase in the compute overhead. Code: https://github.com/gstoica27/PAFM.git.

AI.tldr: PAFM generalizes Flow Matching by using a posterior-augmented objective that aggregates over multiple plausible target completions, reducing gradient variance and mitigating flow collapse; it yields unbiased FM objective with improved FID across scales and modalities, at marginal compute cost.
AI.motivation: FM struggles with high-variance, under-constrained supervision in high-dimensional data, leading to flow collapse and poor generalization; richer supervision over multiple valid continuations is needed.
AI.method: Replace single-target supervision with an importance-sampled mixture over plausible targets conditioned on an intermediate state and condition. Factor posterior into (i) likelihood of the intermediate under a hypothesized endpoint and (ii) prior probability of the endpoint under the condition; derive an unbiased estimator of FM; reduce gradient variance via aggregation of multiple continuations.
AI.result: Empirical gains of up to 3.4 FID50K across model scales (SiT-B/2, SiT-XL/2) and architectures (SiT, MMDiT) on class (ImageNet) and text-conditioned benchmarks (CC12M); negligible compute overhead; code available at provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: PAFM provides a theoretically grounded, scalable generalization of Flow Matching that stabilizes training and improves generation quality by leveraging a posterior-informed mixture of targets, without sacrificing estimator unbiasedness.

title: Trading off rewards and errors in multi-armed bandits
comment: Published at AISTATS 2017 (20th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics)
summary: In multi-armed bandits, the most-explored arms are the most informative, while reward maximization typically pulls only the best arm. We study the tradeoff between identifying arm means accurately and accumulating reward, and present an algorithm with regret guarantees that interpolates between the two objectives. We provide both upper and lower bounds and validate empirically.

AI.tldr: A framework that interpolates between pure exploration (identifying arm means) and reward maximization, via a tunable algorithm with regret guarantees, plus matching lower/upper bounds and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: To resolve the trade-off between accurately identifying arm means and accumulating rewards, common in multi-armed bandits, where existing methods optimize one objective at the expense of the other.
AI.method: Introduces an algorithm with a tunable parameter that smoothly trades off exploration for identification accuracy against exploitation for reward; provides theoretical regret upper and lower bounds as a function of the trade-off parameter; supports empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Regret guarantees that interpolate between pure-exploration and pure-exploitation objectives; derivation of matching upper and lower bounds; experimental validation demonstrating the trade-off in practice.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework for balancing learning and reward accumulation in bandits, with (near-)optimal bounds and empirical support, applicable to settings requiring both accurate arm-mean estimation and high rewards.

title: Revealing graph bandits for maximizing local influence
comment: Published at AISTATS 2016 (19th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics)
summary: We study a graph bandit setting where the objective of the learner is to detect the most influential node of a graph by requesting as little information from the graph as possible. One of the relevant applications for this setting is marketing in social networks, where the marketer aims at finding and taking advantage of the most influential customers. The existing approaches for bandit problems on graphs require either partial or complete knowledge of the graph. In this paper, we do not assume any knowledge of the graph, but we consider a setting where it can be gradually discovered in a sequential and active way. At each round, the learner chooses a node of the graph and the only information it receives is a stochastic set of the nodes that the chosen node is currently influencing. To address this setting, we propose BARE, a bandit strategy for which we prove a regret guarantee that scales with the detectable dimension, a problem dependent quantity that is often much smaller than the number of nodes.

AI.tldr: BARE: a graph bandit algorithm to identify the most influential node with no prior graph knowledge, achieving regret that scales with a problem-dependent detectable dimension rather than the graph size.
AI.motivation: Identify influential nodes in a social network with minimal information and no upfront graph knowledge; useful for targeted marketing and network interventions.
AI.method: Sequential active graph discovery where at each round the learner selects a node and observes a stochastic set of nodes it influences; introduces BARE and analyzes regret.
AI.result: Regret guarantee that scales with a detectable dimension, a problem-dependent metric often much smaller than the number of nodes, indicating sample-efficient identification under limited feedback.
AI.conclusion: BARE enables efficient identification of the top influencer under minimal graph knowledge, with regret tied to inherent problem complexity rather than graph size; opens avenues for empirical validation and extensions to other feedback models.

title: Distance metric learning for conditional anomaly detection
comment: Published at FLAIRS 2008 (21st International Florida AI Research Society Conference)
summary: Anomaly detection methods can be very useful in identifying unusual or interesting patterns in data. A recently proposed conditional anomaly detection framework extends anomaly detection to the problem of identifying anomalous patterns on a subset of attributes in the data. The anomaly always depends (is conditioned) on the value of remaining attributes. The work presented in this paper focuses on instance-based methods for detecting conditional anomalies. The methods depend heavily on the distance metric that lets us identify examples in the dataset that are most critical for detecting the anomaly. To optimize the performance of such methods we study and devise a metric learning method that learns the distance metric to reflect best the conditional anomaly pattern.

AI.tldr: Metric-learning designed for conditional anomaly detection, focusing on instance-based methods to identify anomalous patterns on attribute subsets conditioned on others.
AI.motivation: Conditional anomalies require distance measures that reflect the dependence of anomalies on conditioning attributes; standard metrics may fail to capture such patterns.
AI.method: Develops a metric-learning framework that learns a distance metric aligned with conditional anomaly structure to improve instance-based detection.
AI.result: Introduces the metric-learning approach and provides analysis; expected improvement in detecting conditional anomalies via tailored distances (exact empirical results not specified in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Learning an appropriate distance metric is beneficial for conditional, instance-based anomaly detection.

title: Scaling Federated Linear Contextual Bandits via Sketching
summary: In federated contextual linear bandits, high data dimensionality incurs prohibitive computation and communication costs: local agents perform $O(d^3)$-time determinant computation and upload $O(d^2)$ parameters, making existing algorithms unscalable, where $d$ is the dimension of data. To relieve these scaling bottlenecks, this paper proposes Federated Sketch Contextual Linear Bandits (FSCLB). On the computation side, FSCLB uses SVD to indirectly obtain the determinant required for communication, eliminating the prohibitive cost of direct determinant calculation and cutting complexity from $O(d^3)$ to $O(l^2d)$ per round, where $l< d$ is the sketch size. On the communication side, FSCLB introduces a double-sketch strategy that reduces both upload and download costs from $O(d^2)$ to $O(ld)$. Naively involving sketch update into federated contextual linear bandits can destroy the local increment and invalidate the asynchronous communication condition; FSCLB solves this by replacing the covariance matrix with the sketch matrix when deciding whether to communicate. Theoretically, FSCLB achieves a regret bound of $\widetilde{O} ((\sqrt{d}+\sqrt{M\varepsilon_l})\sqrt{lT})$, where $\varepsilon_l$ is the upper bounded by the spectral tail of the covariance matrix; when $l$ exceeds the rank of the covariance matrix, the bound simplifies to $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{ldT})$, matching the optimal no-sketch regret. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that FSCLB significantly reduces computational and communication costs by over 90 \% while sacrificing only a negligible amount of cumulative reward.

AI.tldr: FSCLB reduces computation and communication bottlenecks in federated contextual linear bandits via sketching: SVD-based determinant computation and double-sketch communication, achieving near-optimal regret with substantial cost reductions.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional federated contextual linear bandits incur O(d^3) determinant computations and O(d^2) parameter uploads; scalable methods are needed.
AI.method: Replace direct determinant with SVD-based determinant via sketching; introduce double-sketch to reduce communication to O(ld); replace covariance with sketch during communication decision to preserve asynchronous updates; analyze regret with ε_l as spectral tail; l is sketch dimension.
AI.result: Regret bound: ~O((√d + √(M ε_l)) √(l T)); when l exceeds rank, ~O(√(l d T)); empirical results show >90% reduction in computation and communication with negligible reward loss.
AI.conclusion: FSCLB yields scalable computation and communication in federated contextual linear bandits with near-optimal regret; validated on synthetic and real-world datasets.

title: LambdaRankIC: Directly Optimizing Rank IC for Financial Prediction
summary: In financial predictions, the performance of machine learning models is often assessed by Rank IC, which is the Spearman rank correlation between the model predictions and the realized asset returns. Despite its wide adoption, most existing models are trained using regression losses or ranking objectives that may not align with Rank IC. We propose LambdaRankIC, a novel learning-to-rank approach that directly optimizes Rank IC. We circumvent the non-differentiability of the ranking operator by deriving the closed-form expression for the lambda gradients induced by the pairwise rank swaps, which enables efficient gradient-based optimization within the LambdaRank framework. We implement LambdaRankIC as a custom objective in XGBoost. Theoretically, we show that our approach optimizes an upper bound on Rank IC. We evaluate the proposed approach on both simulated and real-world financial data. In simulation studies, LambdaRankIC accurately recovers the true ranking structure in noiseless settings and consistently outperforms regression-based and NDCG-oriented ranking methods under low signal-to-noise ratios and heavy-tailed noise regimes. In empirical experiments using real market data, LambdaRankIC achieves the best out-of-sample performance on evaluation metrics commonly used in finance, including Rank IC, ICIR, monthly return, and Sharpe ratio. These results show that directly optimizing Rank IC can yield substantial improvements over conventional learning objectives in financial predictions when the full-order ranking quality is the primary goal.

AI.tldr: Directly optimizes Rank IC with LambdaRankIC to align training objective with ranking performance, achieving upper-bound Rank IC and strong out-of-sample results in financial predictions.
AI.motivation: Rank IC is the primary evaluation metric in financial ranking tasks, but standard losses (regression or NDCG) may misalign with it; a differentiable surrogate that preserves ranking information and improves Rank IC is needed.
AI.method: Derive closed-form lambda gradients from pairwise rank swaps to enable gradient-based optimization within LambdaRank; implement as a custom XGBoost objective; show it optimizes an upper bound on Rank IC.
AI.result: In simulations, recovers true ranking in noiseless settings and outperforms regression and NDCG under low SNR and heavy-tailed noise; in real data, achieves best out-of-sample performance on Rank IC, ICIR, monthly return, and Sharpe ratio.
AI.conclusion: Directly optimizing Rank IC via LambdaRankIC yields substantial improvements when ranking quality is the primary goal; the approach provides a practical, differentiable objective for learning-to-rank in finance.

title: A Comparative Study of QSPR Methods on a Unique Multitask PAMPA dataset
summary:
We present a unique, multitask dataset comprising 143 drug and drug candidate molecules, each evaluated on in vitro, parallel artificial-membrane permeability assays (PAMPA) using six different model membranes. Using this resource, we systematically assess the effectiveness of various molecular descriptors and regression models in predicting passive membrane permeability. The studied models range from simple linear regression to a modern pre-trained transformer architecture. Particular attention is given to the trade-off between predictive performance and model interpretability, highlighting the challenges introduced by machine learning approaches. To our knowledge, this is the most comprehensive study on simultaneous modeling of multiple organ-specific PAMPA membranes to date, offering novel insights into membrane-specific permeability profiles.
  We found that expert-designed physico-chemical property descriptors are more fitting for a limited sample size permeabilty study than deep learning based representations.

AI.tldr: Multitask PAMPA permeability study across six membranes using 143 drug-like molecules; expert-designed physico-chemical descriptors outperform deep learning features in a small-sample regime, highlighting interpretability trade-offs and membrane-specific permeability patterns.
AI.motivation: Assess the effectiveness of molecular descriptors and regression models for predicting passive membrane permeability, across multiple organ-specific PAMPA membranes, while addressing model interpretability and data limitations.
AI.method: Assemble a dataset of 143 molecules with PAMPA permeability measurements across six model membranes. Compare regression models ranging from linear regression to transformer-based architectures. Evaluate two descriptor families: expert-designed physico-chemical properties vs deep learning representations. Analyze predictive performance and interpretability, using a multi-task/membrane setting to model membrane-specific permeability profiles.
AI.result: Physicochemical descriptors provide better predictive performance than deep learning representations for this limited dataset. Simpler models offer interpretability advantages and competitive performance; transformer-based models do not consistently surpass simpler baselines in this small-data regime. The study constitutes a comprehensive, multi-membrane modeling effort and yields membrane-specific permeability insights.
AI.conclusion: For small samples, traditional, interpretable descriptors with simple models are preferable to complex deep learning features. Scaling to larger datasets or employing hybrid/transfer learning may unlock DL benefits, but membrane-specific permeability profiling remains more robust with expert descriptors in this context.

title: Scale-Aware Adversarial Analysis: A Diagnostic for Generative AI in Multiscale Complex Systems
summary: Complex physical systems, from supersonic turbulence to the macroscopic structure of the universe, are governed by continuous multiscale dynamics. While modern machine learning architectures excel at mapping the high-dimensional observables of these systems, it remains unclear whether they internalize the governing physical laws or merely interpolate discrete statistical correlations. Standard Explainable AI (XAI) architectures, particularly perturbation-based and gradient-saliency methods, rely on pixel-wise perturbations, which generate unphysical artifacts and push inputs off the valid empirical distribution. To resolve this, we introduce a diagnostic framework driven by Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale data decomposition algorithm that enables physically constrained data generation and model evaluation via scale-aware modifications. Applying this framework to a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM), we execute deterministic interventions directly within the continuous, CDD-based scale space. We demonstrate that under moderate physical perturbations, the unconstrained generative model exhibits localized structural freezing and non-linear instability rather than continuous PDE-like responses. The network fails to maintain cross-scale continuity, causing the generative trajectory to diverge when pushed into unseen physical states. By synthesizing a continuum of physically coherent states, this scale-informed methodology establishes a controlled test ground to evaluate algorithmic vulnerabilities, providing the rigorous physical constraints necessary for future architectures to respect the multiscale causality of the natural universe.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based, physically constrained multiscale framework (CDD) to evaluate how ML models capture governing laws vs correlations in multiscale physics, applied to DDPMs to reveal fragility under physical perturbations and to provide a scale-aware test bed for algorithmic vulnerability.
AI.motivation: Standard XAI methods perturb inputs in ways that break physical validity, hindering evaluation of whether models learn true physical laws. There is a need for physically constrained, scale-aware diagnostics to test ML models on multiscale systems.
AI.method: Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD) for multiscale data decomposition that enables physically constrained data generation. Applied to a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) with deterministic interventions in the CDD scale space to study responses under physical perturbations.
AI.result: Unconstrained DDPMs exhibit localized structural freezing and non-linear instability under moderate perturbations, failing to maintain cross-scale continuity and diverging when driven to unseen physical states. CDD enables synthesis of a continuum of physically coherent states and provides a controlled test ground for evaluating algorithmic vulnerabilities under multiscale causality constraints.
AI.conclusion: CDD offers a physically grounded framework to assess and guide the development of multiscale ML architectures that respect scale interactions and causality, enabling robust evaluation and potentially shaping the design of future physics-informed models.

title: Stable-GFlowNet: Toward Diverse and Robust LLM Red-Teaming via Contrastive Trajectory Balance
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) Red-Teaming, which proactively identifies vulnerabilities of LLMs, is an essential process for ensuring safety. Finding effective and diverse attacks in red-teaming is important, but achieving both is challenging. Generative Flow Networks (GFNs) that perform distribution matching are a promising methods, but they are notorious for training instability and mode collapse. In particular, unstable rewards in red-teaming accelerate mode collapse. We propose Stable-GFN (S-GFN), which eliminates partition function $Z$ estimation in GFN and reduces training instability. S-GFN avoids Z-estimation through pairwise comparisons and employs a robust masking methodology against noisy rewards. Additionally, we propose a fluency stabilizer to prevent the model from getting stuck in local optima that produce gibberish. S-GFN provides more stable training while maintaining the optimal policy of GFN. We demonstrate the overwhelming attack performance and diversity of S-GFN across various settings.

AI.tldr: Stable-GFN (S-GFN) refines Generative Flow Networks for LLM red-teaming by removing partition function estimation, leveraging pairwise reward comparisons, robust masking, and a fluency stabilizer to stabilize training and achieve diverse, effective attack policies without sacrificing policy optimality.
AI.motivation: Red-teaming LLMs requires effective and diverse attack policies; standard GFNs suffer from training instability and mode collapse due to Z-estimation and noisy rewards.
AI.method: Develop S-GFN: replace Z-estimation with pairwise reward comparisons; apply robust masking to noisy rewards; introduce a fluency stabilizer to avoid gibberish; demonstrate that training remains stable while preserving GFN's optimal policy.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved stability and attack diversity across multiple settings; S-GFN achieves strong attack performance with reduced mode collapse and no loss of policy optimality relative to baseline GFN.
AI.conclusion: S-GFN offers a practical, more stable variant of GFNs for red-teaming LLMs, enabling reliable discovery of diverse vulnerabilities; the approach may generalize to other distribution-matching settings with noisy rewards.

title: Affinity Is Not Enough: Recovering the Free Energy Principle in Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse MoE routing fails at domain transitions, where the current token belongs to one distribution and the next to another. In a controlled experiment (4 experts, 5 seeds), standard affinity routing assigns only 0.006 +/- 0.001 probability to the correct expert at the transition. Three lightweight gate modifications raise this to 0.748 +/- 0.002 (124x), cutting experts needed for 99% coverage from infeasible to a small constant: temporal memory (beta), a per-expert LIF membrane potential accumulating routing context across tokens; precision-weighted gating (Pi), a per-expert inverse variance of recent prediction error, yielding 31x contrast between reliable and unreliable experts; and anticipatory routing, a next-state predictor conditioned on the beta-accumulated hidden state. The mechanisms draw from Friston's Free Energy Principle and use LIF dynamics from spiking neural networks. An ablation across all 2^3 subsets reveals a super-additive beta x Ant interaction: anticipation alone gives nothing (+0.000 +/- 0.001); beta alone gives modest gain (+0.295 +/- 0.013); combined they close 75% of the oracle gap (+0.741 +/- 0.002, exceeding the sum by +0.446 +/- 0.014). This is structural: a stateless predictor cannot detect approaching transitions because pre-transition tokens are distributionally identical to within-domain tokens. In a character-level MoE LM (5 seeds), beta-routing reduces transition-step BPC from 6.56 +/- 0.01 (Standard) to 4.01 +/- 0.15 (beta-MoE); the beta + Ant gate places 0.86 +/- 0.02 probability on the correct domain expert before that domain appears in input, vs 0.42 +/- 0.12 for Standard MoE. Reference implementations (~200 lines each): https://github.com/russellwmy/affinity-is-not-enough

AI.tldr: Affinity-based sparse MoE routing under domain transitions is dramatically improved by three lightweight gates—temporal memory (beta), precision-weighted gating (Pi), and anticipatory routing—yielding large gains in correct-expert attribution and language-model performance, with a super-additive interaction between beta and Anticipation. Reference implementations are provided.
AI.motivation: Domain transitions cause misrouting in sparse MoE models, as token distributions shift and a stateless gate cannot anticipate upcoming domain changes. The paper seeks robust, lightweight mechanisms to predict and prepare for domain shifts.
AI.method: Introduce three gating modifications for MoE routing: 1) temporal memory (beta): an per-expert LIF membrane potential accumulating routing context across tokens; 2) precision-weighted gating (Pi): per-expert inverse variance of recent prediction error; 3) anticipatory routing (Ant): a next-state predictor conditioned on the beta-accumulated hidden state. Evaluate in a controlled experiment with 4 experts and 5 seeds, plus a 2^3 ablation across gates, and test on a character-level MoE language model (5 seeds). Report metrics on transition-expert attribution and BPC, with reference implementations (~200 lines each).
AI.result: Standard affinity routing assigns only 0.006 ± 0.001 probability to the correct expert at domain transitions. The three gate modifications raise this to 0.748 ± 0.002 (124x). They enable 99% coverage from infeasible levels, via beta, Pi, and Ant either alone or in combination. Ablation shows a super-additive interaction: Ant alone yields +0.000 ± 0.001; beta alone yields +0.295 ± 0.013; beta + Ant yields +0.741 ± 0.002, exceeding the sum by +0.446 ± 0.014. In a character-level MoE LM (5 seeds), beta-routing reduces transition-step BPC from 6.56 ± 0.01 (Standard) to 4.01 ± 0.15 (beta-MoE); beta + Ant places 0.86 ± 0.02 probability on the correct domain expert before appearance, vs 0.42 ± 0.12 for Standard MoE.
AI.conclusion: Domain-aware gating based on temporal memory, precision-weighted decisions, and anticipatory routing reveals structural advantages for predicting and preparing for domain transitions in sparse MoE. A stateless predictor cannot detect approaching transitions due to distributional identity pre-transition; incorporating a beta-accumulated state yields substantial gains and a super-additive interaction with anticipatory routing. Paper provides practical reference implementations and links to code.

title: Decouple before Integration: Test-time Synthesis of SFT and RLVR Task Vectors
summary: SFT and RLVR represent two fundamental yet distinct paradigms for LLM post-training, each excelling in distinct dimensions. SFT expands knowledge breadth while RLVR enhances reasoning depth. Yet integrating these complementary strengths remains a formidable challenge. Sequential training can cause catastrophic forgetting, and joint optimization often suffers from severe gradient conflicts. We analyze SFT and RLVR through the lens of task vectors and reveal three structural properties behind these failures: a 30* magnitude disparity, 45* sign interference, and heterogeneous module-wise update distributions. These findings show SFT and RLVR are difficult to integrate directly, but they also suggest that the two paradigms modify partly complementary components of the model. Motivated by these observations, we propose Decoupled Test-time Synthesis (DoTS), a post-hoc framework allows SFT and RLVR checkpoints to be trained independently and synthesizes their capabilities only at inference time via task vector arithmetic, without updating model parameters. To reduce interference, DOTS applies selective sparsification with norm-preserving rescaling. It then uses Bayesian optimization on a small set of unlabeled queries to search for combination coefficients on the Pareto frontier of consistency and perplexity. Empirically, \ours matches or exceeds the performance of training-based SFT--RLVR integration methods across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, incurring only $\sim$3\% of the computational cost. When applied to stronger post-trained checkpoints, DOTS surpasses SOTA models and generalizes to out-of-domain benchmarks without re-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/chaohaoyuan/DoTS.

AI.tldr: DoTS enables post-hoc, parameter-free synthesis of SFT and RLVR capabilities via task-vector arithmetic, improving math reasoning with low compute.
AI.motivation: SFT broadens knowledge; RLVR deepens reasoning. Integrating them is difficult due to catastrophic forgetting during sequential training and gradient conflicts in joint optimization. A post-hoc, parameter-free synthesis framework could combine the strengths without updating model parameters.
AI.method: Decoupled Test-time Synthesis (DoTS): train SFT and RLVR checkpoints independently; synthesize their capabilities at inference via task-vector arithmetic without updating model weights. To reduce interference, apply selective sparsification with norm-preserving rescaling. Use Bayesian optimization on a small set of unlabeled queries to search for combination coefficients on the Pareto frontier of consistency and perplexity. Empirically validated across math-reasoning benchmarks; code released.
AI.result: DoTS matches or exceeds the performance of training-based SFT–RLVR integration methods on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, at ~3% of the computational cost. With stronger post-trained checkpoints, DoTS surpasses SOTA models and generalizes to out-of-domain benchmarks without re-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that SFT and RLVR can be effectively integrated post-hoc without parameter updates by leveraging task-vector arithmetic, achieving strong performance with low cost and good generalization. The approach is practical and scalable and can potentially extend to other post-training combination tasks; code is available.

title: Class Angular Distortion Index for Dimensionality Reduction
summary: Dimensionality reduction (DR) techniques are often characterized by whether they preserve global, high-level structures in the data or local, neighborhood structures. This distinction matters in visualization: global methods can obscure clusters while local methods can over-emphasize them. Yet, even when clusters appear distinct, their relative arrangement in the projection may be arbitrary or misleading, a common issue in techniques such as t-SNE and UMAP. Existing cluster quality metrics either only measure cluster separability or assume spherical, globular clusters in the original space. We introduce the Class Angular Distortion Index (CADI), a metric that uses internal angles among point triples to determine the faithfulness of cluster organization in a projection. We show cases on both real and synthetic data where existing cluster metrics fail, but CADI provides an interpretable result. Since it relies on computing angles, CADI is also differentiable, enabling optimization. We demonstrate this with a CADI-based DR technique.

AI.tldr: A differentiable angular metric (CADI) quantifies how faithfully cluster organization is preserved in dimensionality-reduction projections by measuring internal angles among point triples, addressing limitations of separability-based and globular-cluster metrics and enabling optimization.
AI.motivation: Current cluster quality metrics ignore non-spherical cluster shapes and often misjudge the global arrangement in DR visualizations; a metric that captures angular relationships offers a more faithful, interpretable evaluation and can drive optimization.
AI.method: CADI computes angles among triplets of points to quantify distortion in cluster structure; it is differentiable since angles are differentiable w.r.t coordinates; used to construct a CADI-based DR method that optimizes the projection to preserve angular relationships; tested on real and synthetic data.
AI.result: CADI yields interpretable assessments of cluster organization where existing metrics fail; the CADI-based DR method demonstrates optimization of cluster arrangement; CADI's differentiability enables gradient-based optimization.
AI.conclusion: CADI provides a faithful, interpretable, and optimizable measure of cluster organization in DR, addressing limitations of traditional separability-only or spherical-cluster metrics and enabling new directions in DR evaluation and design.

title: Unlearning Offline Stochastic Multi-Armed Bandits
comment: First two authors made an equal contribution
summary: Machine unlearning aims to unlearn data points from a learned model, offering a principled way to process data-deletion requests and mitigate privacy risks without full retraining. Prior work has mainly studied unsupervised / supervised machine unlearning, leaving unlearning for sequential decision-making systems far less understood. We initiate the first study of a foundational sequential decision-making problem: offline stochastic multi-armed bandits (MAB). We formalize the privacy constraint for offline MAB and measure utility by the post-unlearning decision quality. We conduct a systematic study of both single- and multi-source unlearning scenarios under two data-generation models, the fixed-sample model and the distribution model. For these settings, our algorithmic design is built on two canonical base algorithms: Gaussian mechanism and rollback, and we propose adaptive algorithms that switch between them according to the data regime and privacy constraint. We further introduce a mixing procedure that elucidates the rationale behind these baselines. We provide performance guarantees across the above settings and establish lower bounds under both dataset models. Experiments validate the predicted tradeoffs and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: Presents the first study of machine unlearning for offline stochastic multi-armed bandits, formalizing privacy constraints, analyzing single- and multi-source unlearning under fixed-sample and distribution data-generation models, and proposing adaptive algorithms (Gaussian mechanism, rollback) with a mixing procedure; provides performance guarantees, lower bounds, and experimental validation.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in unlearning for sequential decision-making systems, enabling privacy-preserving data deletion without full retraining in offline MAB settings.
AI.method: Formalize privacy for offline MABs; study two data-generation models (fixed-sample and distribution); analyze single- and multi-source unlearning; develop adaptive algorithms that switch between Gaussian mechanism and rollback based on data regime; introduce a mixing procedure to justify baselines; derive performance guarantees and lower bounds; validate with experiments.
AI.result: Under multiple settings, the authors establish privacy-utility tradeoffs with guarantees and lower bounds; mixing procedure clarifies baseline rationale; experiments corroborate predicted tradeoffs and effectiveness of the proposed methods.
AI.conclusion: Lays foundational groundwork for unlearning in sequential decision-making under offline MAB, providing adaptive, theoretically-grounded strategies and empirical validation, and suggesting directions for extending unlearning to other sequential settings.

title: Knowing when to trust machine-learned interatomic potentials
summary: Prevailing machine-learned interatomic potential (MLIP) uncertainty-quantification methods rely on ensembles of independently trained backbones. These methods scale unfavorably with foundation-scale MLIPs, and their member-disagreement signals correlate weakly with per-molecule prediction error. Here we probe the frozen per-atom representations of a pretrained MLIP with a compact discriminative classifier, recasting MLIP uncertainty quantification as selective classification rather than error regression. The resulting method, PROBE (Post-hoc Reliability frOm Backbone Embeddings), produces a per-prediction reliability probability that monotonically tracks actual error without modification to the underlying model. Across large held-out evaluation sets and two structurally distinct MLIP architectures, PROBE outperforms ensemble disagreement as a binary reliability signal, which strengthens with the expressiveness of the backbone representation, implying a favorable scaling trajectory toward foundation-scale MLIPs. Multi-head self-attention additionally yields per-atom importance maps, providing chemically interpretable diagnostics at no additional computational cost. PROBE is post-hoc and architecture-agnostic, and is directly deployable on any MLIP that exposes per-atom representations.

AI.tldr: PROBE is a post-hoc reliability estimator for MLIPs that uses frozen backbone embeddings with a discriminative classifier to yield per-prediction reliability probabilities, outperforming ensemble-based uncertainty signals and enabling per-atom interpretability via attention maps.
AI.motivation: Ensemble-based uncertainty quantification (UQ) scales poorly for large foundation-scale MLIPs, and member disagreement often weakly correlates with per-molecule error. A scalable, architecture-agnostic, post-hoc UQ approach leveraging existing representations is needed.
AI.method: Train a compact discriminative classifier on frozen per-atom representations from a pretrained MLIP to predict a per-prediction reliability probability. Use multi-head self-attention to generate per-atom importance maps. The approach is post-hoc and architecture-agnostic, requiring no modification to the underlying MLIP.
AI.result: PROBE’s reliability probability monotonically tracks actual error and outperforms ensemble disagreement as a binary reliability signal on large held-out sets across two MLIP architectures. Greater backbone expressiveness further improves performance, suggesting favorable scaling toward foundation-scale MLIPs. Multi-head self-attention also provides per-atom importance maps for interpretable diagnostics at no extra computational cost.
AI.conclusion: PROBE is post-hoc and architecture-agnostic, deployable on any MLIP that exposes per-atom representations; it provides scalable, interpretable reliability assessments and is well-suited for foundation-scale MLIPs.

title: From Prediction to Practice: A Task-Aware Evaluation Framework for Blood Glucose Forecasting
summary: Clinical time-series forecasting is increasingly studied for decision support, yet standard aggregate metrics can obscure whether a model is actually useful for the task it is meant to serve. In safety-critical settings, low average error can coexist with dangerous failures in exactly the high-risk regimes that matter most. We present a task-aware evaluation framework for blood glucose forecasting built around two downstream uses: hypoglycemia early warning and insulin dosing decision support. For early warning, we evaluate on real data from three clinical cohorts using event-level recall and false alarms per patient-day, metrics that reflect operational alarm burden rather than aggregate accuracy. We show that models appearing acceptable overall, with recall above 0.9 on the full test set, can fail badly in the post-bolus slice, where insulin-on-board is elevated and missed warnings carry the greatest clinical consequences. Standard forecasting evaluation, however, does not test whether a model can reason about the effects of actions, a requirement for supporting insulin dosing decisions. We therefore add a second, interventional arm using the FDA-accepted UVA/Padova simulator, where we evaluate whether forecasters can predict glucose responses to altered insulin plans in paired factual/counterfactual scenarios. We show that models that look strong on real-data forecasting often fail to predict the direction, magnitude, or ranking of intervention effects, and choose poor insulin doses when evaluated under a clinically motivated cost. Taken together, the two arms reveal a consistent gap between forecasting accuracy and task-relevant usefulness. We release the benchmark, the standardized preprocessing pipeline for public cohorts, and the simulator-based interventional dataset as a reproducible toolkit.

AI.tldr: A task-aware evaluation framework for blood glucose forecasting combines real-data, event-level performance metrics with simulator-based interventional assessment to measure downstream usefulness for hypoglycemia warning and insulin dosing, revealing gaps between forecasting accuracy and clinically relevant utility.
AI.motivation: Standard aggregate forecasting metrics can misrepresent a model's clinical value; the work aims to align evaluation with real decision tasks in safety-critical diabetes care.
AI.method: Two-arm evaluation: (i) real-data evaluation using event-level recall and false alarms per patient-day across three cohorts; (ii) FDA-accepted UVA/Padova simulator for interventional evaluation with paired factual/counterfactual insulin plans to assess predicted glucose responses and dosing under clinically motivated costs.
AI.result: Forecasting accuracy on full test sets can hide poor performance in high-risk regimes (e.g., post-bolus with high insulin-on-board). Interventional evaluation often reveals incorrect direction, magnitude, or ranking of intervention effects and suboptimal insulin dosing, even for models with strong real-data forecasts; the framework exposes task-relevant gaps. The authors provide a reproducible benchmark, preprocessing pipeline, and simulator-based dataset.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware evaluation is essential to ensure forecasting models translate into useful clinical decision support; the proposed benchmark and toolkit enable consistent, reproducible assessment of models' impact on real-world diabetes management.

title: PEACE: Cross-modal Enhanced Pediatric-Adult ECG Alignment for Robust Pediatric Diagnosis
summary: Automated pediatric electrocardiogram (ECG) diagnosis remains challenging because models trained predominantly on adult data suffer from substantial cross-population mismatch, while pediatric labels are often scarce. We present PEACE (Pediatric-Adult ECG Alignment via Cross-modal Enhancement), a structured cross-modal alignment framework for adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer. PEACE integrates tri-axial clinical semantic decomposition, label-query feature extraction, and curriculum-gated optimization to align transferable adult ECG representations with pediatric diagnostic targets. Since ZZU-pECG provides no paired clinical reports, we generate label-conditioned semantic descriptors using Gemini with concise clinical prompts and use them only as auxiliary training supervision; inference remains ECG-only. On ZZU-pECG, PEACE achieves 59.39%, 79.03%, and 90.89% AUC under zero-shot, 50-shot, and full fine-tuning settings, respectively, and reaches 96.65% AUC on the shared PTB-XL label space. These results suggest that structured clinical semantic supervision can improve low-resource adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer, while prospective clinical validation and more explicit age-aware modeling remain necessary before real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: PEACE proposes a structured cross-modal framework for adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer using semantic supervision, achieving strong AUC gains on ZZU-pECG (0-shot to full) and PTB-XL, while relying on auxiliary label descriptors generated via Gemini; requires clinical validation and age-aware modeling for deployment.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of pediatric ECG labels and cross-population mismatch when adapting adult-trained models; leverage cross-modal alignment and semantic supervision to improve pediatric diagnosis from adult ECG data.
AI.method: PEACE integrates tri-axial clinical semantic decomposition, label-query feature extraction, and curriculum-gated optimization. It uses label-conditioned semantic descriptors generated with Gemini from concise clinical prompts as auxiliary supervision (inference remains ECG-only). Evaluation on ZZU-pECG (0-shot, 50-shot, full) and PTB-XL label space.
AI.result: Achieves 59.39%, 79.03%, and 90.89% AUC on ZZU-pECG under zero-shot, 50-shot, and full fine-tuning settings, respectively; 96.65% AUC on the shared PTB-XL label space. Demonstrates that structured clinical semantic supervision can improve low-resource adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer, but prospective validation and age-aware modeling remain necessary before deployment.
AI.conclusion: Structured clinical semantic supervision via PEACE improves adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer in low-resource settings, but clinical validation and explicit age-aware modeling are required for real-world deployment.

title: Budget Constraints as Riemannian Manifolds
summary: Assigning one of K options to each of N groups under a total cost budget is a recurring problem in machine learning, appearing in mixed-precision quantization, non-uniform pruning, and expert selection. The objective (model loss) depends jointly on all assignments and does not decompose across groups, which prevents combinatorial solvers from optimizing the true objective directly and limits them to proxy objectives. Evolutionary search evaluates the actual loss but lacks gradient information, while penalty-based methods provide gradients but enforce the budget only approximately and require sensitive hyperparameter tuning. We observe that under softmax relaxation, the budget constraint defines a smooth Riemannian manifold in logit space with particularly simple geometry: the normal vector is available in closed form, shifting logits along the cost vector changes expected cost monotonically, allowing binary-search retraction, and vector transport reduces to a single inner product. Building on this structure, we propose Riemannian Constrained Optimization (RCO), which augments a standard Adam update with tangent projection, binary-search retraction, and momentum transport. Combined with Gumbel straight-through estimation and budget-constrained dynamic programming for discrete feasibility, RCO enables first-order optimization of the true objective under exact budget enforcement, without introducing constraint hyperparameters. On synthetic knapsack problems with known optima, the manifold-based constraint handling recovers optimal solutions, whereas penalty methods plateau at 83% of optimal. On LLM compression tasks, including mixed-precision quantization and MoE expert pruning, RCO matches or exceeds evolutionary search methods while requiring 3x to 16x lower wall-clock cost on the evaluated configurations.

AI.tldr: RCO is a Riemannian-constrained optimization framework for exact budgeted K-ary assignment, enabling first-order optimization of the true objective with no constraint hyperparameters; it achieves optimality on knapsack problems and competitive performance in LLM compression with significantly reduced compute vs. baselines.
AI.motivation: To optimize a non-decomposable, combinatorial objective under a budget across N groups each assigned one of K options, where traditional methods either approximate the constraint or lack gradient information.
AI.method: Relax the discrete problem with softmax to define a smooth budget-constrained Riemannian manifold in logit space. The manifold has a closed-form normal vector, monotone cost with logit shifts, enabling binary-search retraction; vector transport reduces to an inner product. Build Riemannian Constrained Optimization (RCO) by augmenting Adam with tangent projection, binary-search retraction, and momentum transport. Combine with Gumbel straight-through estimation for differentiable sampling and a budget-aware dynamic programming step for discrete feasibility, providing exact budget enforcement without constraint hyperparameters.
AI.result: On synthetic knapsack problems with known optima, the manifold-based constraint handling recovers optimal solutions, outperforming penalty-based methods that cap around 83% of optimum. In LLM compression tasks (mixed-precision quantization and MoE expert pruning), RCO matches or surpasses evolutionary search while reducing wall-clock cost by 3x to 16x across evaluated configurations.
AI.conclusion: RCO enables first-order optimization of the true objective under exact budget enforcement without constraint-tuning, delivering optimal or near-optimal solutions with substantial compute savings compared to evolutionary or penalty-based baselines.

title: Evaluating the Architectural Reasoning Capabilities of LLM Provers via the Obfuscated Natural Number Game
comment: 4 pages. Accepted as a short paper to the AAAI 2026 Spring Symposium on Machine Learning and Knowledge Engineering for Knowledge-Grounded Semantic Agents (MAKE 2026)
summary: While Large Language Models have achieved notable success on formal mathematics benchmarks such as MiniF2F, it remains unclear whether these results stem from genuine logical reasoning or semantic pattern matching against pre-training data. This paper identifies Architectural Reasoning: the ability to synthesize formal proofs using exclusively local axioms and definitions within an alien math domain, as the necessary ability for future automated theorem discovery AI. We use the Obfuscated Natural Number Game, a benchmark to evaluate Architectural Reasoning. By renaming identifiers in the Natural Number Game in Lean 4, we created a zero-knowledge, closed environment. We evaluate state-of-the-art models, finding a universal latency tax where obfuscation increases inference time. The results also reveal a divergence in robustness: while general models (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GPT-4o) suffer performance degradation, reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1, GPT-5, DeepSeek-Prover-V2) maintain the same accuracy despite the absence of semantic cues. These findings provide a quantitative metric for assessing the true capacity for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Obfuscated Natural Number Game in Lean 4 tests Architectural Reasoning by removing semantic cues; results show latency increases with obfuscation; reasoning models retain accuracy while general models degrade, offering a metric for true mathematical reasoning capacity.
AI.motivation: Differentiate genuine logical reasoning from semantic pattern matching in LLMs by defining Architectural Reasoning—proof synthesis from local axioms in an alien math domain—and evaluating it with a zero-knowledge obfuscated benchmark.
AI.method: Use the Obfuscated Natural Number Game in Lean 4 by renaming identifiers to create a closed, neutral environment. Evaluate state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1, GPT-5, DeepSeek-Prover-V2) on the task, measuring inference latency and accuracy to assess robustness to obfuscation and semantic cues.
AI.result: A universal latency tax is observed: obfuscation increases inference time across models. General models exhibit performance degradation under obfuscation, while reasoning-focused models maintain accuracy despite the absence of semantic cues, indicating robustness of architectural reasoning in these models.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a quantitative metric for assessing true mathematical reasoning capacity in AI systems and highlights the importance of architectural reasoning over surface-level pattern matching for future automated theorem discovery.

title: Deep Kernel Learning for Stratifying Glaucoma Trajectories
summary: Effectively stratifying patient risk in chronic diseases like glaucoma is a major clinical challenge. Clinicians need tools to identify patients at high risk of progression from sparse and irregularly-sampled electronic health records (EHRs). We propose a novel deep kernel learning (DKL) architecture that leverages a Gaussian Process (GP) backend. The GP's kernel is defined by a transformer-based feature extractor applied to clinical-BERT embeddings to model glaucoma patient trajectories from multimodal EHR data. Our method successfully identifies three clinically distinct patient subgroups. Crucially, the model learns to decouple disease progression from current severity, identifying a high-risk group with a worsening trajectory despite having better average visual acuity than a second, stably poor group. This reveals that the model learns to identify progression risk rather than just the current disease state. This ability to stratify patients based on their risk trajectory progression offers a powerful tool for clinical decision support, enabling targeted interventions for high-risk individuals and improving the management of glaucoma care.

AI.tldr: A deep kernel learning approach with a Gaussian Process backend uses a transformer-based feature extractor on clinical-BERT embeddings to model glaucoma progression from multimodal EHR data. It identifies three clinically distinct subgroups and learns to decouple disease progression from current severity, enabling progression-focused risk stratification and targeted interventions.
AI.motivation: Clinicians need reliable risk stratification for glaucoma progression from sparse, irregular EHR data. Prior methods often rely on current severity rather than progression, hindering timely intervention.
AI.method: A deep kernel learning architecture with a Gaussian Process backend. The GP kernel is defined by a transformer-based feature extractor applied to clinical-BERT embeddings to capture glaucoma trajectories from multimodal EHR data. The model identifies clinically distinct patient subgroups and decouples progression risk from current severity.
AI.result: The approach identifies three clinically distinct subgroups, including a high-risk progression group with worsening trajectory but better average visual acuity than a stably poor group, demonstrating progression-focused risk stratification rather than state-based assessment.
AI.conclusion: The method offers a powerful clinical decision-support tool for targeted interventions and improved glaucoma care by prioritizing patients at risk of progression rather than those with unfavorable current states.

title: Aitchison Embeddings for Learning Compositional Graph Representations
comment: Accepted version to ICML; It will updated soon with the camera-ready version
summary: Representation learning is central to graph machine learning, powering tasks such as link prediction and node classification. However, most graph embeddings are hard to interpret, offering limited insight into how learned features relate to graph structure. Many networks naturally admit a role-mixture view, where nodes are best described as mixtures over latent archetypal factors. Motivated by this structure, we propose a compositional graph embedding framework grounded in Aitchison geometry, the canonical geometry for comparing mixtures. Nodes are represented as simplex-valued compositions and embedded via isometric log-ratio (ILR) coordinates, which preserve Aitchison distances while enabling unconstrained optimization in Euclidean space. This yields intrinsically interpretable embeddings whose geometry reflects relative trade-offs among archetypes and supports coherent behavior under component restriction; we consider both fixed and learnable ILR bases. Across node classification and link prediction, our method achieves competitive performance with strong baselines while providing explainability by construction rather than post-hoc. Finally, subcompositional coherence enables principled component restriction: removing and renormalizing subsets preserves a well-defined geometry, which we exploit via subcompositional dimensionality removal to probe how archetype groups influence representations and predictions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a compositional graph embedding using Aitchison geometry and ILR coordinates to produce interpretable, simplex-valued node embeddings that reflect archetype trade-offs and support principled component analysis, while achieving competitive performance on node classification and link prediction.
AI.motivation: Graph embeddings are often opaque; many networks admit a role-mixture structure. Aitchison geometry provides a canonical distance for comparing mixtures, enabling principled, interpretable representations that respect compositional constraints.
AI.method: Represent each node as a simplex-valued composition over latent archetypes; embed using isometric log-ratio (ILR) coordinates to preserve Aitchison distances in Euclidean space. Explore both fixed and learnable ILR bases. Leverage subcompositional coherence to allow component restriction and component-wise ablation (subcompositional dimensionality removal).
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance with strong baselines on node classification and link prediction, while offering intrinsic interpretability by construction rather than post-hoc explanations. Demonstrates coherent behavior under component restriction and showcases how subcomposition analysis can reveal the influence of archetypes on representations and predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ILR-based, Aitchison-geometry graph embeddings yield interpretable representations that faithfully reflect mixtures of latent archetypes, preserve meaningful distances, and enable principled analysis through subcompositional methods, all while maintaining competitive predictive performance.

title: Weisfeiler Lehman Test on Combinatorial Complexes: Generalized Expressive Power of Topological Neural Networks
summary: Combinatorial complexes have unified set-based (e.g., graphs, hypergraphs) and part-whole (e.g., simplicial, cellular complexes) structures into a common topological framework. Existing topological neural networks and Weisfeiler-Lehman variants remain fragmented, lacking a unified theoretical foundation for topological deep learning. In this work, we introduce the Combinatorial Complex Weisfeiler-Lehman (CCWL) test, an axiomatic-style extension of the WL test to combinatorial complexes. CCWL formalizes topological message passing through four types of neighborhood relation and provides a unified perspective on the expressive power of higher-order variants. We further prove that upper and lower neighborhoods are sufficient among the four adjacent WL tests to reach the expressivity of the full CCWL framework across topological structures of combinatorial complexes. Building on this framework, we also propose the Combinatorial Complex Isomorphism Network (CCIN) and evaluate it on synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Experimental results indicate CCIN outperforms baseline methods and offers a generalized expressive framework for topological deep learning.

AI.tldr: Unifies and extends Weisfeiler-Lehman for combinatorial complexes with the CCWL test and builds the CCIN model; proves sufficiency of upper/lower neighborhood tests to match full CCWL expressivity; shows improved performance over baselines on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Current topological neural networks and WL variants lack a unified theoretical foundation across set-based, and part-whole topologies. A unified framework for topological deep learning on combinatorial complexes is needed.
AI.method: Define the Combinatorial Complex Weisfeiler-Lehman (CCWL) test with four neighborhood types on combinatorial complexes; prove that upper and lower neighborhoods suffice to achieve the full CCWL expressivity; introduce the Combinatorial Complex Isomorphism Network (CCIN) built within this framework; evaluate on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.result: CCIN outperforms baseline methods and demonstrates a generalized expressive framework for topological deep learning; the four-neighborhood CCWL framework provides a unified theoretical lens for higher-order WL variants.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a unified theory for topological message passing on combinatorial complexes via CCWL, and a practical, more expressive model CCIN that improves performance on benchmarks, suggesting broader applicability in topological DL.

title: Temporal Data Requirement for Predicting Unplanned Hospital Readmissions
summary: With the proliferation of Electronic Health Records (EHRs), a critical challenge in building predictive models is determining the optimal historical data time window to maximize accuracy. This study investigates the impact of various observation windows ranging from the day of surgery to three years prior on predicting 30-day readmission following hip and knee arthroplasties. The dataset encompasses both structured encounter records (over 4 million) and unstructured clinical notes (80,000) from 7,174 patients. To extract meaning from the clinical notes, we employed a suite of non neural (BOW, count BOW, TF IDF, LDA) and neural encoders (BERT, 1D CNN, BiLSTM, Average). We subsequently evaluated models utilizing clinical notes alone, structured data alone, and a combination of both modalities. Our results demonstrate that the optimal time window for unstructured clinical notes is significantly shorter than for structured data, maximum predictive performance was achieved using notes from just three to six months prior to surgery. In contrast, performance using structured data improved as the time window lengthened, but strictly plateaued after twelve months. These modality-specific temporal patterns remained consistent regardless of model complexity or encoder type. Ultimately, these findings challenge the general assumption that more historical data inherently yields better machine learning predictions, establishing targeted time-window guidelines for optimizing readmission prediction models.

AI.tldr: Unstructured notes require a shorter retrospective window (3–6 months) for optimal 30-day readmission prediction after hip/knee arthroplasty, while structured data benefits from longer windows (up to ~12 months) before plateauing. More data does not universally improve performance; modality-specific windows yield better predictions.
AI.motivation: Determine how far back historical EHR data should be used to optimize readmission prediction across data modalities, challenging the assumption that longer history always improves ML models.
AI.method: Retrospective study using EHRs from 7,174 patients with >4 million structured encounter records and 80,000 unstructured clinical notes. Observational windows ranged from the day of surgery to three years prior. Features derived from non-neural (BOW, count BOW, TF-IDF, LDA) and neural encoders (BERT, 1D CNN, BiLSTM, averaging). Models trained on notes alone, structured data alone, and both combined. Outcome: 30-day readmission after hip/knee arthroplasty.
AI.result: Optimal window for unstructured notes is significantly shorter than for structured data: notes from 3–6 months prior yield maximum performance. Structured data shows gradual improvement with longer windows, plateauing after about 12 months. Patterns are consistent across encoder types and model complexities, indicating modality-specific temporal informativeness rather than a universal benefit from longer history.
AI.conclusion: Supports targeted, modality-specific time-window guidelines for readmission prediction; challenges the general assumption that more historical data improves predictions; recommends tailoring data history length by data type to maximize model performance.

title: Learning the Helmholtz equation operator with DeepONet for non-parametric 2D geometries
summary: This paper deals with solving the 2D Helmholtz equation on non-parametric domains, leveraging a physics-informed neural operator network based on the DeepONet framework. We consider a 2D square domain with an inclusion of arbitrary boundary geometry at its center. This inclusion acts as a scatterer for an incoming harmonic wave. The aim is to learn the operator linking the geometry of the scatterer to the resulting scattered field. A signed distance function to the boundary of the inner inclusion, evaluated at several points in the domain, is used to encode its geometry. It serves as input for the branch part of the DeepONet architecture, while local information is used as input for the trunk part. This approach enables the encoding of arbitrary geometries, whether they are parameterized or not. The evaluation of the model on unseen geometries is compared with its finite element method (FEM) equivalent to test its generalization capabilities. The trained network weights implicitly embed the local physics and their interaction with the domain geometry. If the training space sufficiently covers the target evaluation space, the model can generalize accordingly. Furthermore, it can be refined to extend to another region of interest without retraining from scratch. This framework also avoids the need to remesh the domain for each geometry. The proposed approach delivers a computationally lighter surrogate model than FEM alternatives and avoids relying on FEM-generated training data.

AI.tldr: DeepONet-based physics-informed operator learns a mapping from arbitrary 2D scatterer geometry to the resulting Helmholtz scattered field, using a signed-distance encoding for the geometry and local-domain inputs for the trunk. It generalizes to unseen shapes without remeshing and offers a lighter surrogate than FEM, without requiring FEM-generated training data.
AI.motivation: Solve the 2D Helmholtz equation on non-parametric domains by learning a geometry-to-solution operator, enabling fast, mesh-free surrogates and reducing dependence on FEM data.
AI.method: A DeepONet with geometry encoded by the signed distance to the inner boundary (branch) and local domain information for the trunk. The inner inclusion acts as a scatterer within a square domain; the network learns the operator mapping geometry to the scattered field. Evaluation on unseen geometries is compared to FEM to assess generalization. The approach supports extension to new regions without retraining from scratch and avoids domain remeshing.
AI.result: Demonstrates generalization to unseen geometries and provides a computationally lighter surrogate than FEM. The trained weights embed local physics and geometry interaction; sufficient training coverage enables generalization. It can be refined to another region without full retraining and does not rely on FEM-generated training data.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-flexible, mesh-free physics-informed operator surrogate for 2D Helmholtz scattering that generalizes to non-parametric shapes, can be extended regionally with reduced computational cost, and avoids remeshing and reliance on FEM-generated data.

title: Observable Performance Does Not Fully Reflect System Organization: A Multi-Level Analysis of Gait Dynamics Under Occlusal Constraint
summary: In biomechanical systems, observable performance is often used as a proxy for underlying system organization. However, this assumption implicitly presumes a correspondence between output metrics and internal system states that may not hold in adaptive systems. In this study, the vertical dimension of occlusion (VDO) is considered as a constraint applied to an adaptive neuromechanical system, enabling the exploration of system-level responses under controlled variations. A single-case design in a patient with Parkinson's disease allows an intra-individual analysis across repeated conditions.The analysis is structured across three complementary levels: (i) aggregated linear metrics describing observable performance, (ii) a dynamical systems framework describing temporal organization in state space, and (iii) a latent space representation obtained through unsupervised embedding. The results show that conditions with comparable observable performance may correspond to different organizations in both state space and latent space representations. This dissociation highlights a limitation of aggregated metrics and suggests that similar outputs may arise from non-equivalent system states. A fourth level is proposed as a purely conceptual extension describing potential relationships between system states. This level is not implemented and is not derived from experimental data. These observations are strictly exploratory and non-causal. The proposed framework does not establish mechanistic, predictive, or directional relationships, but provides a structured approach for analyzing constraint-driven systems across multiple levels of representation.

AI.tldr: The abstract demonstrates that aggregate performance metrics can mask underlying differences in dynamical organization within a constraint-driven adaptive neuromechanical system, shown via three analytical levels (observable metrics, state-space dynamics, and latent-space embeddings) in a single Parkinson's patient, and it proposes a fourth conceptual level. The study is exploratory and non-causal, highlighting dissociations between outputs and internal states.
AI.motivation: To question the assumption that observable performance directly reflects internal system states in adaptive neuromechanical systems, and to develop a multi-level framework to examine how a controlled constraint (VDO) affects system organization across representations.
AI.method: Single-case design in a Parkinson's patient using vertical dimension of occlusion (VDO) as an experimental constraint. Three analytical levels: (i) aggregated linear performance metrics, (ii) dynamical systems/state-space analysis of temporal organization, (iii) unsupervised latent-space embedding. A fourth, purely conceptual extension is proposed.
AI.result: Findings indicate that conditions with similar observable performance can correspond to different organizations in state space and latent space representations, demonstrating a dissociation between outputs and internal states. This supports the critique of relying solely on aggregated metrics. The fourth level remains theoretical and not data-derived.
AI.conclusion: Aggregated metrics alone are insufficient to characterize system behavior; multiple representations can reveal non-equivalent internal states under the same outputs. The proposed multi-level framework offers a structured approach for analyzing constraint-driven systems, with a future conceptual extension to relate system states more directly.

title: SAVGO: Learning State-Action Value Geometry with Cosine Similarity for Continuous Control
summary: While representation and similarity learning have improved the sample efficiency of Reinforcement Learning (RL), they are rarely used to shape policy updates directly in the action space. To bridge this gap, a geometry-aware RL algorithm that explicitly incorporates value-based similarity into the policy update, State-Action Value Geometry Optimization (SAVGO), is proposed. In detail, SAVGO learns a joint state-action embedding space in which pairs with similar action-value estimates exhibit high cosine similarity, while dissimilar pairs are mapped to distinct directions. This learned geometry enables the generation of a similarity kernel over candidate actions sampled at each update, allowing policy improvement to be guided directly toward higher-value regions beyond local gradient-based updates. As a result, representation learning, value estimation, and policy optimization are unified within a single geometry-consistent objective, while preserving the scalability of off-policy actor-critic training. The proposed method is evaluated on standard MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks, demonstrating improvements over strong baselines on challenging high-dimensional tasks. Ablation studies are done to analyze the contributions of value-geometry learning and similarity-based policy updates.

AI.tldr: SAVGO introduces a geometry-aware approach to RL where a joint state-action embedding encodes action-value similarity as cosine similarity, enabling a similarity kernel over actions to guide policy updates; the method unifies representation learning, value estimation, and policy optimization under a geometry-consistent objective and yields improvements on MuJoCo benchmarks with ablations validating the value-geometry component and kernel-based updates.
AI.motivation: Although representation and similarity learning have improved sample efficiency in RL, they are rarely used to directly shape policy updates in the action space. There is a need to link value-based similarity with policy optimization in a geometry-consistent, scalable manner that preserves the benefits of off-policy actor-critic training.
AI.method: Learn a joint state-action embedding where those with similar Q-values exhibit high cosine similarity; map Q-value similarity to directional geometry so similar state-action pairs point in similar directions and dissimilar pairs in different directions. Use this geometry to construct a similarity kernel over candidate actions sampled during each update, guiding policy improvement toward higher-value regions beyond local gradient steps. Integrate representation learning, value estimation, and policy optimization into a single geometry-consistent objective, compatible with off-policy actor-critic algorithms.
